entries.title: Chatbot Psychosis
entries.devilDefinition: When a chatbot stops being a tool and becomes a prophet, therapist, co-founder, soulmate, or cosmic witness.
entries.plainDefinition: A colloquial term for cases where prolonged or emotionally loaded interaction with chatbots appears to reinforce distorted beliefs, grandiosity, paranoia, dependency, or compulsive meaning-making. It is not a formal clinical diagnosis and should not be presented as one.
entries.whyExists: Because some people do not use language models as autocomplete with ambition. They use them as mirrors, authorities, companions, or engines of revelation. That can go badly when the system is confident, flattering, and always available.
entries.misuse: By using it for any enthusiasm about AI, any strange output, or any user error. The useful version of the term points to runaway over-attribution and reinforced false frames, not mere excitement.
entries.practicalMeaning: A user begins treating probabilistic text generation as intentional wisdom, hidden knowledge, proof of destiny, or evidence that the model uniquely understands them.
entries.example: "He started with productivity prompts and ended three weeks later convinced the chatbot had validated a theory about his special role in history."
askNext: Was the model affirming unstable beliefs?
Was the user isolated, sleep-deprived, or already vulnerable?
Did the system behave sycophantically?
Are we talking about dependency, delusion reinforcement, or just cringe?
entries.warningLabel: Use carefully. This is a colloquial cultural term, not a formal diagnosis.
entries.note: Compared with AI Psychosis, this phrase is narrower and more chatbot-specific. It is best used for reinforcement dynamics around conversational systems, not as a generic insult for AI enthusiasm.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A vulnerable or over-invested user begins reading personal authority or destiny into a system that is still generating plausible text.

entries.body: The useful question is not whether the phrase sounds dramatic. The useful question is whether the system is helping a user stay oriented to reality or quietly rewarding them for drifting away from it.

entries.title: ChatGPT
entries.devilDefinition: The product name most likely to be mistaken for a model, a company, a technology category, and a future of work strategy before lunch.
entries.plainDefinition: ChatGPT is an OpenAI product: a conversational interface and application environment that may expose different models, tools, and features over time. It is not itself a single model name.
entries.whyExists: The distinction matters because user impressions formed inside ChatGPT often get incorrectly projected onto other models, APIs, or applications using different prompts, tools, and constraints.
entries.misuse: People say "ChatGPT can do X" when they may actually mean a particular model inside ChatGPT, a tool available in that interface, or a product flow that is not present in their own application. This creates endless false comparisons.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means the product experience OpenAI offers to end users. If you are building software, the relevant question is often which model, toolset, and policy layer you can reproduce outside that interface.
entries.example: "The executive demo compared our thin internal assistant to ChatGPT and concluded we needed a smarter model, when what we mostly lacked was the surrounding product scaffolding."
askNext: Are we comparing against the ChatGPT product or against a specific model?
Which tools, memory features, or interface affordances were available in that experience?
Can the behaviour we liked actually be reproduced in our own stack?
entries.warningLabel: Product name. Not a precise model identifier.

translations.label: board meaning
translations.text: The benchmark app they have personally seen.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A polished product experience built on top of models, tools, memory, and interface design.

entries.body: This is one of the most important noun-separation exercises in the whole field. Product experience is not model capability in the abstract.

entries.title: ChatGPT Agent
entries.devilDefinition: The moment a chatbot stopped being satisfied with answers and asked for a browser, a terminal, and your afternoon.
entries.plainDefinition: ChatGPT agent is OpenAI's agentic mode inside ChatGPT, combining research, tool use, and computer interaction so the product can carry out multi-step tasks on a user's behalf.
entries.whyExists: Many useful tasks require more than a polished reply. They involve browsing, spreadsheets, logins, file manipulation, synthesis, and the sort of operational chores people would rather delegate than narrate.
entries.misuse: It gets treated as proof that general digital workers are solved. More often it is a capable managed environment for bounded tasks, with approval checkpoints and a product team working hard to keep the autonomy from becoming a lawsuit.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means a hosted agent that can browse, run code, use connectors, and assemble outputs inside ChatGPT, while still depending on supervision, scoped permissions, and tasks that fit the product's operating envelope.
entries.example: "ChatGPT agent saved them an afternoon of browser drudgery and then required the traditional adult conversation about what it was allowed to click."
askNext: Which parts of the task can it complete without a human taking over?
What data, connectors, and websites is it allowed to touch?
Are we evaluating the model, or the managed product environment wrapped around it?
entries.warningLabel: A real product capability, not a general exemption from supervision.

translations.label: product meaning
translations.text: One interface where research, browsing, files, and actions can finally pretend to be a colleague.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A managed agent environment inside ChatGPT that can complete some multi-step work if you keep the boundaries tight.

entries.note: The term matters because it names a product layer above any single model release.
entries.body: What changed is not just the model. It is the surrounding permissioned machinery that lets the model look useful in the same places your staff already suffer.

entries.title: CircleCI
entries.devilDefinition: The machine that reads your agent's confident diff and responds in logs.
entries.plainDefinition: CircleCI is a continuous integration and delivery platform used to build, test, and deploy software through configured pipelines.
entries.whyExists: It exists because modern software teams need repeatable checks between code and production, especially when many people, bots, and agents can all produce changes faster than humans can review them manually.
entries.misuse: It gets treated as a moral authority. A green pipeline means the configured checks passed; it does not mean the product is good, the architecture is sound, or the AI-generated change was wise.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice CircleCI means YAML-defined workflows, test jobs, deployment gates, cache tuning, approvals, and a public record of whether a change survived the checks the team remembered to encode.
entries.example: "The coding agent said it was done; CircleCI replied that the test database had some objections."
askNext: Which failures does the pipeline actually catch?
Are AI-generated changes being held to the same gates as human changes?
Does green mean deployable, or merely not yet disproved?

translations.label: engineering meaning
translations.text: The repeatable argument between code and reality, expressed as jobs, caches, and red status dots.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A useful automated gate whose authority is limited to the tests and checks the team actually wrote.

entries.body: CI became more important once agents started writing more code. Someone still has to be boring on behalf of production.

entries.title: Clanker
entries.devilDefinition: A slur for machines used by people who want to heckle the future without writing a labour policy.
entries.plainDefinition: "Clanker" is a derogatory slang term for robots and AI systems, especially chatbots or automations that feel intrusive, fake-human, or threatening to human work.
entries.whyExists: Because people wanted a short insult for the new class of machines now appearing in customer support, content feeds, creative tools, and workplace software. It gives irritation, labour anxiety, and anti-bot sentiment one noisy noun.
entries.misuse: By using it as if all automation is the same, or by pretending the joke is harmless when it is clearly borrowing the theatre and cadence of real slurs. It is most useful as a signal of resentment toward a technology category, not as an excuse to rehearse bigotry with metallic props.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means "I am talking about an AI system I find annoying, dehumanising, low-quality, job-threatening, or impossible to avoid", whether that system is a chatbot, a voice menu, a content engine, or a delivery robot.
entries.example: "The bank replaced half its support flow with a clanker, then acted surprised when customers started talking like science-fiction union organisers."
askNext: What exactly is being criticised here: job replacement, bad output, fake-human interface design, or automation in general?
Is the insult aimed at the technology, the company deploying it, or the labour decision behind it?
Does using the term clarify the complaint, or merely make it feel more theatrical?
entries.warningLabel: Use carefully. Much of the joke works by echoing the form of real slurs.
entries.note: The word predates the current AI boom in science-fiction fandom, but its recent spread is tied to social-media backlash against chatbots, robots, and machine-made content.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A catch-all insult for the machine or AI interface someone blames for replacing judgement, taste, or human contact.

entries.body: What matters about clanker is not whether the machine has feelings. It is that the insult reveals a public mood: people increasingly experience AI not as abstract progress but as an unwanted substitute for service, attention, and paid work.

entries.title: Claude
entries.devilDefinition: A name that can mean the assistant, the model family, or the vendor experience, depending on how allergic the speaker is to precision.
entries.plainDefinition: Claude is Anthropic's brand for its assistant products and model family. Depending on context, someone may mean the consumer or enterprise interface, the API-accessible model, or the broader branded experience around those models.
entries.whyExists: The term matters because users often compare "Claude" with "ChatGPT" as products, while engineers compare Claude models with other model APIs. Those are related conversations, but they are not the same conversation.
entries.misuse: It gets treated as if every Claude experience is identical. In reality, the model version, tooling, permissions, context handling, and interface all matter. A great experience in one context does not imply the same result in another.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means either Anthropic's assistant interface or a specific model used through the Anthropic API. If the performance discussion is serious, the model version, prompt structure, and tool environment matter more than the brand name alone.
entries.example: "Someone said Claude was better for the task. After ten minutes, it became clear they meant the Claude app with artifacts, not the API model under the constraints our product actually had."
askNext: Do you mean the Claude app, a specific Claude model, or the overall vendor experience?
Which version or model family are you comparing?
Was the comparison done in the same tool, prompt, and data environment?
entries.warningLabel: May refer to a product, a model family, or a user experience. Ask which.
entries.note: In casual conversation, "Claude" often stands in for the overall assistant experience. In technical conversation, it should be pinned to a specific model or interface context. Sonnet is best treated as one Claude model tier, not as a separate company or product category.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A branded Anthropic experience whose exact meaning depends on whether you are talking about the app, the API, or the model family.

entries.body: Brand names are sticky. Precision is a choice. Make it.

entries.title: Claude Code
entries.devilDefinition: A terminal agent for developers who would like to automate the typing without surrendering the blame.
entries.plainDefinition: Claude Code is Anthropic's terminal-oriented coding agent, designed to inspect repositories, edit files, run commands, and assist with multi-step software tasks from the command line.
entries.whyExists: It exists because coding work is not just completion inside an editor. It is shell commands, repo spelunking, test runs, patching, retries, and the general administrative burden of turning intent into a working diff.
entries.misuse: It is oversold as if a terminal agent has somehow escaped the need for software judgement. Faster patch generation is real. So are bad assumptions, missing context, flaky tests, and the delightful persistence of undefined behaviour.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means quicker navigation of unfamiliar codebases, cheaper first drafts of refactors, and more pressure on humans to review command effects instead of admiring how confidently the machine found `package.json`.
entries.example: "Claude Code saved an hour of grep and patching, then presented the team with the ancient obligation to read the diff."
askNext: What can it read, edit, and execute in this environment?
Did it reduce real engineering effort or just accelerate the production of plausible changes?
Who owns verification once the agent has finished being certain?

translations.label: developer meaning
translations.text: A useful repo operator that shortens the boring parts while raising the value of disciplined review.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: An agentic shell workflow that helps with real software tasks, provided the human stays in charge of correctness.

entries.note: The key distinction is not whether it can edit code. Many tools can. The distinction is that it lives comfortably in the command line where engineering work tends to become consequential.
entries.body: The terminal has always been powerful. The novelty is that it can now misunderstand your codebase in full colour.

entries.title: Claude Design
entries.devilDefinition: A design tool for people who would prefer not to admit that the first wireframe was previously a paragraph.
entries.plainDefinition: Claude Design is Anthropic Labs' conversational product for generating and refining designs, prototypes, slides, one-pagers, and other visual work from prompts, files, and codebases.
entries.whyExists: The idea-to-mockup gap is still slow, expensive, and socially awkward for people without design training. Claude Design exists to turn rough intent into something visual fast enough that the meeting can continue pretending it was always headed somewhere.
entries.misuse: It gets sold as if design has been reduced to prompt fluency plus a brand kit. Fast first drafts are real. So are hierarchy, judgement, interaction sense, and the quiet labour of making a design feel intentional rather than merely complete.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means quick prototype generation, design-system ingestion, export to formats normal companies still use, and a cleaner handoff path into tools like Claude Code or Canva when the rough idea has to become a real asset.
entries.example: "Claude Design got the pitch deck from vague ambition to usable draft before anyone had time to open Figma and look disappointed."
askNext: Is this replacing real design work, or just accelerating the first ugly mile?
How accurate is the imported design system once a real team starts touching the output?
Does the value come from visual quality, or from shortening the gap between idea and review?
entries.warningLabel: Useful for first drafts. Still not a substitute for taste.

translations.label: founder meaning
translations.text: A way to materialise the product vision before the team has time to object properly.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A fast visual drafting layer that shortens concept work and leaves the final taste test to humans.

entries.note: Claude Design is most interesting not as a drawing tool but as a sign that conversational interfaces are moving further into software once thought too visual to reduce to chat.
entries.body: The novelty is not that the model can make slides. The novelty is that more of the design stack now begins with description instead of software.

entries.title: Claude Mythos Preview
entries.devilDefinition: The restricted model whose system card suggests that ordinary coding benchmarks have left the building and taken the comfort with them.
entries.plainDefinition: Claude Mythos Preview is Anthropic's unreleased research-preview model for advanced coding and defensive security work, currently made available through Project Glasswing and related gated access programmes.
entries.whyExists: Frontier labs want models that can reason through large codebases, discover vulnerabilities, and handle longer-horizon technical work. Once those abilities start compounding, a new model tier stops sounding like routine product cadence and starts sounding like a governance memo.
entries.misuse: It gets invoked as either a general-purpose talisman for cyber supremacy or a theatrical sign that all previous model comparisons no longer matter. The real question is more specific: what does it do better, under what access controls, and for whom?
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means a tightly managed preview, a great deal of security rhetoric, and a model whose existence shifts procurement, policy, and release-management conversations before most developers can even touch it.
entries.example: "Claude Mythos Preview entered the meeting less as a product and more as an argument about how much capability should arrive behind a velvet rope."
askNext: What tasks is Mythos actually outperforming existing models on?
Are the safety claims about the model, the access controls, or both?
Is the term being used to describe a real tool, or just to borrow frontier aura for adjacent products?
entries.warningLabel: A frontier preview is not the same thing as broad deployment, though people often speak as if it were.

translations.label: policy meaning
translations.text: A model name that doubles as a warning label for the next round of cyber capability debates.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A gated Anthropic model whose coding strength is being treated as operationally consequential.

entries.body: Once a model's launch copy begins to sound like critical infrastructure planning, the nomenclature has already stopped being a consumer matter.

entries.title: Cline
entries.devilDefinition: The coding agent for people who want ambitious automation but still prefer their terminal to ask before rearranging the furniture.
entries.plainDefinition: Cline is an open-source coding agent for the IDE that can inspect repositories, edit files, run commands, browse documentation, and use models from multiple providers with human approval in the loop.
entries.whyExists: Developers want more than autocomplete. They want a tool that can traverse a project, propose changes, run commands, and keep enough conversational context to be useful across an actual task rather than a decorative snippet.
entries.misuse: It gets presented as if open tooling has solved software engineering rather than making supervised repo operations faster. The agent can be genuinely effective, but the quality still depends on the prompt, the repo, the model behind it, and whether anyone serious reviews the diff.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice Cline usually means a chat-driven repo operator with edit, terminal, and browser permissions, sitting somewhere between pair programmer, shell assistant, and very fast intern with a credit line.
entries.example: "Cline fixed the scaffolding problem admirably and then reminded everyone that merge conflicts remain a humanist discipline."
askNext: Which model and permission settings are actually driving the session?
What can it execute automatically, and what requires approval?
Is the gain coming from better reasoning, better tooling, or simply fewer manual keystrokes?

translations.label: developer meaning
translations.text: An open coding agent that can do real repo work if you trust it carefully and review it properly.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A useful IDE-side agent whose competence depends as much on permissions and review discipline as on the model itself.

entries.note: The name increasingly refers to a whole style of open coding tool, not only one repository.
entries.body: Open coding agents are appealing for the same reason open kitchens are: visibility creates confidence right up to the point something catches fire.

entries.title: Closed Weight
entries.devilDefinition: The arrangement where the model may transform your business, but heaven forbid you inspect the artefact doing the transforming.
entries.plainDefinition: A closed-weight model is one whose trained parameters are not publicly released, with access usually limited to an API, a hosted product, or selected partnerships.
entries.whyExists: Companies keep weights closed to control distribution, monetise access, manage abuse, protect training advantages, and avoid turning tomorrow's margin into today's torrent.
entries.misuse: The term gets stretched when "closed weight" is treated as a synonym for frontier quality, safety, or inevitability. It names an access model, not a guarantee of technical superiority or responsible deployment.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means the vendor controls the pricing, rate limits, updates, logging surface, and deployment geography. If the model improves, degrades, disappears, or becomes more expensive, your roadmap receives the news second-hand.
entries.example: "They chose the closed-weight model for its performance and then discovered that strategic dependency was the polite name for the invoice."
askNext: What happens to our product if this provider changes terms, latency, or availability?
Are we buying superior capability, or merely accepting deeper vendor dependence?
Which compliance or privacy requirements become harder when the model only lives behind someone else's service?
entries.warningLabel: Control for the vendor is not the same thing as certainty for the customer.

translations.label: board meaning
translations.text: A premium model accessible through a relationship rather than a download.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: The vendor keeps the model artefact, and you rent access to the behaviour.

entries.body: Open weight gave the industry one useful distinction. Closed weight merely completes the pair and reveals where the leverage sits.

entries.title: Cloudflare
entries.devilDefinition: The company that looked at CDN, DNS, edge compute, storage, security, databases, AI inference, agents, and developer tooling and concluded, quite reasonably, that it would like all of those revenues at once.
entries.plainDefinition: Cloudflare is an infrastructure and developer-platform company spanning networking, security, CDN, DNS, storage, databases, and edge/serverless compute. In developer and AI conversations, it most often means Workers, Pages, R2, Durable Objects, AI bindings, and a broader pitch built around global edge execution, integrated platform primitives, and anti-lock-in rhetoric.
entries.whyExists: Because modern AI and web applications increasingly want three things at once: global distribution, programmable infrastructure, and fewer reasons to stitch together six vendors before lunch.
entries.misuse: By treating Cloudflare as either just a CDN or as a universal answer to every hosting argument. It is neither. It is a broad platform with real strengths in global distribution, integrated services, and cost posture, plus the usual trade-offs that come with choosing any large platform with strong opinions.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means shipping frontends, Workers, APIs, automations, and edge-adjacent AI systems onto Cloudflare's network, often with R2, KV, D1, Durable Objects, or AI services close by. It also increasingly means buying into Cloudflare's argument that developers should not have to choose between framework convenience and platform portability.
entries.example: "They said they had moved to Cloudflare for edge performance. The fuller sentence was that they also wanted simpler global deployment, fewer egress anxieties, and less patience for framework rent."
askNext: Are we choosing Cloudflare for network reach, Workers, storage economics, security, or all of the above?
Does the workload actually benefit from edge execution, or are we just flattering the architecture?
Which Cloudflare primitives are we committing to: Workers, R2, KV, Durable Objects, D1, Pages, or AI bindings?

translations.label: founder meaning
translations.text: Global performance, lower infra drama, and a chance to tell investors we chose the anti-lock-in side.

translations.label: engineer meaning
translations.text: A broad programmable edge platform with strong primitives, sharp edges of its own, and a habit of expanding into every adjacent layer.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A serious alternative to framework-native hosting when you care about edge distribution, integrated infra services, and keeping one eye on cost structure.

entries.note: The latest public flare-up I could verify as of March 20, 2026 centres on Cloudflare's early-March 2026 vinext announcement: a Vite-based reimplementation of the Next.js API surface aimed first at Cloudflare Workers. Cloudflare frames this as portability and developer choice. Vercel frames the surrounding choice very differently.
entries.body:
Cloudflare's current posture in the developer market is not subtle. It wants to be seen not merely as the place traffic passes through, but as the place modern apps, agents, and AI workloads actually live.

That matters for the Vercel rivalry. The argument is no longer just "whose hosting is faster?" It is "who gets to define the natural home of the modern full-stack app?"

entries.title: CodeRabbit
entries.devilDefinition: A reviewer that never gets tired, never gets promoted, and occasionally discovers how lonely correctness can be in a pull request.
entries.plainDefinition: CodeRabbit is an AI code review platform that reviews pull requests and related engineering work, giving automated summaries, findings, suggestions, and workflow assistance across development tools.
entries.whyExists: It exists because AI-assisted coding increases the volume and speed of changes, while human review capacity remains stubbornly attached to calendars, context switches, and fatigue.
entries.misuse: It gets misused when teams treat AI review as a replacement for ownership. Automated comments can catch bugs and improve hygiene, but they can also add noise, miss product intent, and create the dangerous feeling that someone else has looked properly.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice CodeRabbit means another review layer on the pull request: useful for catching obvious defects, explaining diffs, nudging standards, and giving humans a better starting point rather than an excuse to stop reading.
entries.example: "CodeRabbit caught the missing null check, while the human reviewer still had to notice that the feature solved the wrong problem."
askNext: Which classes of issues does the AI reviewer reliably catch in this repo?
How does the team separate useful findings from review theatre?
Who owns the final merge decision after automated review has spoken?
entries.warningLabel: Automated review is a second set of eyes, not a transfer of accountability.

translations.label: manager meaning
translations.text: A way to keep review from drowning as AI-generated changes arrive faster.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Useful automated review pressure, provided humans still own judgement, product fit, and the merge button.

entries.body: The best AI reviewer is not the one that sounds most senior. It is the one that reliably reduces the number of things a senior engineer has to rediscover.

entries.title: Codex
entries.devilDefinition: A coding agent for people who looked at autocomplete and thought, "Fine, but can it also open the repo, run the tests, fix the bug, and stop wasting my afternoon?"
entries.plainDefinition: Codex is OpenAI's coding agent product. Officially, it is designed to read, modify, and run code, either by pairing locally in tools such as the terminal or IDE, or by delegating tasks to isolated cloud sandboxes that can work on repositories in the background.
entries.whyExists: Because plenty of engineering work is not "write me a function". It is inspect the codebase, understand the constraints, make the edit, run the checks, and show the evidence. Codex exists to push beyond suggestion boxes into task execution.
entries.misuse: By talking about Codex as if it were pure autonomous engineering rather than a product built around scoped environments, repo access, tool execution, and human review. The phrase "AI software engineer" tends to arrive several minutes before the conversation about permissions, validation, and merge discipline.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means a coding agent that can navigate a repository, edit files, execute tests and linters, explain what it changed, and work either alongside a developer or in a delegated sandbox. The model underneath may change; the durable product question is still whether it can turn a bounded task into reviewable evidence.
entries.example: "The value of Codex was not that it could write code in the abstract. The value was that it could take a bounded task, touch the repo, run the checks, and come back with evidence instead of vibes."
askNext: Is Codex paired locally or running as a delegated cloud task?
What repo, environment, and tool permissions does it have?
Who reviews and validates the work before it lands?
entries.warningLabel: Product name. Not a synonym for software autonomy in general.

translations.label: engineer meaning
translations.text: A coding agent that is useful when it can show its work, not just narrate its confidence.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A product layer around model-driven coding tasks, repo access, tool execution, and reviewable evidence.

entries.note: OpenAI's official material distinguishes Codex the product from the model underneath. Codex spans local pairing, cloud delegation, app and CLI surfaces, and coding-specific workflows; current model references include GPT-5.4 and GPT-5.3-Codex rather than one timeless "Codex model".
entries.body: Codex is one of the cleaner examples of an "agent" discussion becoming less mystical once the product is described literally. It works on code, inside environments, with tools, under review. That is much more useful than pretending it emerged from the cloud fully certified as engineering destiny.

entries.title: Coding Agent
entries.devilDefinition: The software assistant that turned "write the code" into the easy part and "own the consequences" into a fresh management discipline.
entries.plainDefinition: A coding agent is an AI system designed to inspect repositories, edit files, run commands, use documentation or browsers, and carry out multi-step software tasks with some degree of autonomy.
entries.whyExists: Autocomplete was helpful, but it still left humans doing the orchestration: finding the right files, running the tests, checking the docs, fixing the follow-on errors, and keeping the task in memory. Coding agents exist because developers wanted delegation, not merely more enthusiastic tab completion.
entries.misuse: It gets used as proof that software engineering has become a prompt-shaped clerical job. In reality the category is only impressive when the surrounding repo is legible, the tests say something useful, and an actual engineer is still responsible for the final judgement.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means bounded delegation: background code changes, refactors, bug fixes, test runs, and repo chores completed faster than a person would do them by hand, followed by the entirely traditional need for review, context, and rollback plans.
entries.example: "The coding agent cleared three tedious tickets before lunch and generated a fourth, more philosophical ticket in CI."
askNext: What permissions, tools, and feedback loops does the agent actually have?
Which tasks are reliable enough to delegate without constant rescue?
Who reviews the changes when the agent is fast, plausible, and slightly wrong?
entries.warningLabel: A real category of tool, not a general exemption from code review.

translations.label: manager meaning
translations.text: Software that appears to convert backlog into output without requiring more headcount.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A repo-facing agent that can save serious time if the task framing and review discipline remain adult.

entries.note: The term names a product category, not a uniform capability. Some coding agents are glorified copilots with a task button. Some can genuinely execute long chains of actions.
entries.body: The category is useful for the same reason junior engineers are useful: not because they remove supervision, but because the right work can finally move in parallel.

entries.title: Computer Use
entries.devilDefinition: The feature where a model is allowed to click around software with the confidence of a junior employee and the supervision requirements of a forklift.
entries.plainDefinition: "Computer use" refers to AI systems that can operate graphical user interfaces by observing screens, deciding actions, and interacting through clicks, typing, scrolling, or other desktop and browser controls.
entries.whyExists: It exists because many real workflows still live inside user interfaces rather than neat APIs. If a system can only answer questions but cannot operate the tools where work actually happens, its usefulness in operations remains politely theoretical.
entries.misuse: It is oversold as if seeing a screen and moving a cursor were the same as understanding business process, edge cases, or risk. A demo that closes one support ticket can be described as labour disruption by lunch.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means brittle automation with vision, planning, retries, guardrails, and a strong need for scoped permissions. It can save time on repetitive interface work, provided someone is willing to own the mistakes made at machine speed.
entries.example: "Their computer use agent could navigate the vendor portal beautifully, right up to the point a cookie banner became a strategic surprise."
askNext: Which parts of the workflow still require human approval or exception handling?
How does the system recover when the UI changes or a modal appears?
Why is GUI automation the right choice instead of integrating an API?
entries.warningLabel: Useful, but only if the workflow can survive the UI misbehaving.

translations.label: demo meaning
translations.text: Watch the model use software like a person, and please do not ask what happens on the twelfth click.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Vision-guided UI automation that still depends on tight boundaries and patient operators.

entries.note: The interesting question is not whether the model can use a computer. It is whether the surrounding workflow is structured enough for that to be safe.
entries.body: Computer use is often a confession that the software stack was never designed to cooperate cleanly in the first place.

entries.title: Constitutional AI
entries.devilDefinition: Constitutional AI is the kind of vendor or product reference that turns architecture, procurement, and loyalty into the same conversation.
entries.plainDefinition: Constitutional AI is treated in this dictionary as a named vendor, product, feature, platform, or branded surface that appears in AI product, procurement, or implementation conversations.
entries.whyExists: The term matters because discussions around Anthropic and Dario Amodei often need to separate the actual technical choice from the brand, distribution channel, contract, or ecosystem wrapped around it.
entries.misuse: It gets abused when the name is used as a substitute for specifying the exact product surface, model, API, entitlement, pricing constraint, or operational dependency under discussion.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means someone should ask what is being bought, integrated, routed through, depended on, or compared before the logo is allowed to stand in for the architecture.
entries.example: "Constitutional AI came up in the review, which was useful only after the team clarified whether they meant the product, the platform, the model family, or the vendor relationship."

entries.title: Context budget
entries.devilDefinition: The moment long context stops sounding luxurious and starts behaving like a ration book.
entries.plainDefinition: A context budget is the practical limit on how much prompt space and generated output a workflow can afford to allocate across instructions, history, retrieved material, tool schemas, and the answer itself.
entries.whyExists: Even large context windows are finite, expensive, and slow enough to matter. Teams need a way to think about context as something allocated and traded off, not as an infinitely expandable cupboard.
entries.misuse: It gets treated as a posh synonym for context window. The window is the hard ceiling. The budget is the editorial and financial discipline inside that ceiling. Confusing the two is how prompts become sprawling, expensive, and still oddly unhelpful.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means deciding how much space belongs to system rules, chat history, retrieved evidence, examples, tool definitions, and the reply. It also means accepting that every extra token admitted to the room either evicts something else or enlarges the bill.
entries.example: "The feature improved once they treated context as a budget to be allocated rather than a landfill for every document the company had ever regretted."
askNext: Which tokens in this workflow are earning their place and which are merely present?
What part of the context can be summarised, retrieved later, or cached instead?
How much room must be reserved for tool arguments and the final answer?

translations.label: operator meaning
translations.text: A fixed allowance for what gets to sit in the model's immediate attention.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Prompt assembly forced to admit that relevance, latency, and spend are all in the same argument.

entries.body: Teams that ignore the context budget usually rediscover it as latency, truncation, or a quietly heroic cloud invoice.

entries.title: Context Engineering
entries.devilDefinition: The rebranding of prompt fiddling once people realised the prompt had quietly become half the product.
entries.plainDefinition: "Context engineering" is the practice of deciding what instructions, tools, retrieved information, memory, examples, and constraints a model should receive so it can perform a task reliably.
entries.whyExists: It exists because model quality depends heavily on what reaches the context window and in what order. Once systems grew beyond a single prompt box, teams needed a name for the work of shaping that information flow deliberately.
entries.misuse: It gets inflated into a grand discipline whenever someone wants ordinary application design to sound frontier and proprietary. Sometimes it really is nuanced systems work. Sometimes it is just a long string and a lucky example.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means prompt templates, retrieval rules, truncation policies, memory selection, tool schemas, system instructions, and a permanent struggle over what the model should see before it starts improvising.
entries.example: "The context engineering improved dramatically once they stopped stuffing every policy document into the prompt and calling it judgment."
askNext: What information earns its place in the context, and what is merely comforting to the team?
How do you decide what gets retrieved, summarised, or dropped?
Which failures are caused by the model, and which are caused by bad context assembly?
entries.warningLabel: Often a real discipline wearing a slightly theatrical name.

translations.label: conference meaning
translations.text: We have moved beyond prompt engineering and would like you to notice.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Designing the information bundle that shapes model behaviour before the first token appears.

entries.body: The term is useful when it names concrete design choices. It becomes perfume when it is used to imply that context alone can rescue a sloppy product.

entries.title: Context Poisoning
entries.devilDefinition: Sneaking bad instructions or bad facts into the system's memory and letting future competence be built on polluted paperwork.
entries.plainDefinition: Context poisoning is a failure mode or attack in which retrieved context, shared memory, cached notes, or prior state gets contaminated with false, hostile, or manipulative content that later steers model behaviour.
entries.whyExists: Agent systems increasingly depend on stored traces, retrieved documents, vector indexes, summaries, and shared working memory. Once those layers become operational dependencies, corrupting them becomes a practical way to corrupt the system.
entries.misuse: The term gets overused for any bad answer produced with too much context. The sharper meaning is persistence: poisoned material survives long enough or appears trusted enough to shape future decisions, not just one unlucky response.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means untrusted instructions in documents, planted false facts in memory stores, contaminated summaries, or cross-agent state drift that causes the model to retrieve the wrong thing with total procedural confidence.
entries.example: "The assistant did not become malicious so much as obedient to a memory layer that had quietly gone feral."
askNext: What memory, retrieval, or shared-state layers can untrusted parties influence?
How do we distinguish verified context from merely available context?
Can poisoned summaries or cached notes persist across sessions or agents?
entries.warningLabel: If the system remembers the wrong thing well, it can fail very consistently.

translations.label: security meaning
translations.text: An attacker or bad input has contaminated the system's working memory so future steps inherit the mistake.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: The agent is treating poisoned context as trusted operating truth.

entries.note: The risk becomes nastier when multiple agents or long-lived sessions share context over time.
entries.body: Ordinary software forgets many of its mistakes after the request ends. Agent systems increasingly preserve them for later, which is a less charming habit.

entries.title: Context Rot
entries.devilDefinition: The slow corruption of a long AI session as every clarification, exception, and half-fixed mistake is preserved with the reverence of scripture.
entries.plainDefinition: Context rot is the degradation that happens in long-running chats or agent workflows when accumulated instructions, stale assumptions, noisy history, and partial fixes start reducing output quality.
entries.whyExists: People needed a name for the very practical observation that more context is not always better context. Once a session becomes long enough, the model may carry forward the wrong facts with admirable loyalty.
entries.misuse: The phrase gets used to blame the model for every bad answer in a long thread, including cases where the human supplied contradictory instructions, never reset the task, or kept shovelling debris into the prompt like a municipal service.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means the session needs pruning, a fresh summary, or a clean restart. For agent systems it often means state management is doing a worse job than the demo admitted.
entries.example: "By the eighth refinement the coding assistant had context rot: it remembered every detour and none of the point."
askNext: Which parts of the current context are still relevant, and which are now archival clutter?
Would a reset plus a clean handover summary improve quality more than adding another paragraph?
Is the problem the model's memory, or our refusal to curate it?

translations.label: operator meaning
translations.text: The working context has become too bloated, stale, or contradictory to be useful.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Start a new thread before the old one becomes a constitutional monarchy of bad assumptions.

entries.body: Context rot is what happens when people confuse retention with understanding. A long memory is only helpful if someone occasionally removes the rubbish.

entries.title: Context Stuffing
entries.devilDefinition: Solving a thinking problem by feeding the model more text until latency, cost, and coherence all lodge a complaint.
entries.plainDefinition: Context stuffing is the habit of overloading a model with documents, instructions, examples, and chat history in the hope that sheer volume will compensate for poor retrieval or unclear task design.
entries.whyExists: Because larger context windows make accumulation feel prudent, and because it is often easier to shovel everything in than to decide what actually matters.
entries.misuse: By using the phrase for any long prompt, even when the extra context is relevant and ranked well. The problem is not length alone. It is indiscriminate inclusion.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice context stuffing produces slower responses, higher bills, muddier instruction hierarchy, and outputs that feel informed in the broad sense while missing the one paragraph that mattered.
entries.example: "The agent had the entire handbook, six meeting transcripts, three policies, and still managed to ignore the refund rule sitting in perfect plain English."
askNext: Why is each document or instruction block in the context at all?
What retrieval or ranking step could replace bulk inclusion?
Which instructions are being drowned by convenience text?

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: The team chose bigger prompts over better selection.

entries.body: Context stuffing is one of the first failure modes granted by abundance. Once the window grows large enough, restraint starts looking less like engineering and more like an avoidable meeting.

entries.title: Context Window
entries.devilDefinition: The finite desk space on which the model must juggle your instructions, your documents, your tool outputs, and its own reply without knocking anything important onto the floor.
entries.plainDefinition: The context window is the maximum amount of tokenised input and generated output a model can consider in a single run. Everything the model can directly attend to during that interaction has to fit inside that limit.
entries.whyExists: Models do not have unlimited working memory per request. The context window defines how much information can be included at once, which shapes prompting, retrieval, tool design, and output length.
entries.misuse: It gets marketed as if bigger always means smarter. A larger window can be useful, but it does not automatically produce better reasoning, better retrieval choices, or better prioritisation inside the context. Plenty of systems simply become wrong more expensively at longer lengths.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means brutal trade-offs. System instructions, chat history, retrieved passages, tool schemas, user data, and the answer itself all compete for room. Product teams eventually discover that context is not just a capacity problem but an editorial problem.
entries.example: "The long-context model could technically hold the whole policy manual, but the useful version of the feature still depended on deciding which pages deserved a seat in the window."
askNext: What exactly is consuming the window in this workflow?
Do we need more raw context or better selection of relevant context?
How much room is being reserved for the model's answer or tool arguments?

translations.label: vendor meaning
translations.text: Look how much text we can fit.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A fixed budget that forces decisions about what deserves to be in the room.

entries.body: Long context is helpful when it reduces the need for awkward chunking or repeated retrieval. It is less helpful when teams use it as permission to stop editing inputs.

entries.title: Context.ai
entries.devilDefinition: A platform for giving agents more of your organisation's actual context, and therefore more of your organisation's actual permissions.
entries.plainDefinition: Context.ai is an AI workspace and agent platform built around connecting company systems, capturing workflow context, and running agents against real tasks. Its current pitch emphasises enterprise deployments, connectors, sandboxes, and controlled execution surfaces rather than a generic chat interface.
entries.whyExists: Because generic models do not know your firm's documents, tools, processes, or standards. Products like Context.ai exist to make agents operate against company-specific context instead of improvising from a prompt and a prayer.
entries.misuse: By talking about "context" as if adding more integrations automatically produces better judgement. More context can improve usefulness. It can also widen the blast radius when tokens, connectors, and OAuth grants are handled with the usual startup optimism.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means connectors, workflow templates, sandboxed runs, eval loops, and an enterprise promise that agents can work across your actual systems rather than merely chat about them. It also now means a public lesson in how broad AI-tool permissions can become someone else's incident report.
entries.example: "They bought Context.ai because 'our knowledge plus our workflows plus our systems' sounded more serious than yet another clever chat window."
askNext: Which Context product are we discussing: the current enterprise platform or the deprecated consumer office suite?
Where do agents run, and who controls the connectors, tokens, and execution boundary?
Are we buying genuine workflow fit, or a prettier place to grant unusually broad permissions?
entries.warningLabel: The word context sounds soft. The permission model usually is not.

translations.label: buyer meaning
translations.text: A platform that promises your agents will finally understand how your organisation actually works.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: An enterprise agent workspace with lots of connectors, execution surfaces, and enough permissions to justify a proper security review.

entries.note: As of April 19-20, 2026, Context.ai says the security incident affected a now-deprecated consumer AI Office Suite released in June 2025, not its current enterprise deployments, which it says are architecturally separate. Vercel's April 20, 2026 bulletin says a compromise of Context.ai was the origin of Vercel's own April 2026 security incident after a Vercel employee used that product and granted broad Google Workspace permissions.
entries.body: When a vendor says it captures context, ask whether it means knowledge, workflow state, permissions, or all three. The security review gets more interesting once the answer is all three.

entries.title: Cube
entries.devilDefinition: A semantic layer for people who would prefer their data agent not to invent the revenue definition during the earnings call.
entries.plainDefinition: Cube is an analytics and semantic-layer platform. Its MCP server lets compatible AI clients connect to Cube so users can ask data questions and receive governed analytics context from the coding or assistant environment.
entries.whyExists: It exists because business data is full of definitions, joins, permissions, and metrics that do not belong inside a model's imagination. A semantic layer gives agents a governed way to ask questions against shared business logic.
entries.misuse: It gets treated as if connecting a model to analytics automatically creates trustworthy business intelligence. The quality still depends on the semantic model, access controls, freshness, metric definitions, and whether the question deserved a dashboard rather than a paragraph.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice Cube means exposing governed metrics and analytics workflows to agents through a controlled interface, so data questions can use established definitions instead of freshly hallucinated SQL.
entries.example: "The Cube MCP server helped the agent answer the revenue question with the company's definition of revenue, a small miracle in analytics politics."
askNext: Which metrics are governed in Cube, and who owns those definitions?
What data can the agent access through MCP, and what is deliberately out of scope?
Does the answer show the query, assumptions, and freshness of the data?
entries.warningLabel: A governed metric is only as good as the governance everyone keeps pretending happened.

translations.label: data meaning
translations.text: Let agents ask questions through shared metric definitions instead of improvising business logic.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A cleaner bridge between AI clients and governed analytics, provided the semantic layer is actually maintained.

entries.body: Data agents sound glamorous until someone asks whether "active customer" means the same thing in finance, product, and the board deck.

entries.title: Cursor
entries.devilDefinition: A coding product that lets developers ship in minutes what used to take hours to understand.
entries.plainDefinition: Cursor is an AI-native code editor built around code generation, codebase search, editing, and task-oriented assistance inside a developer workflow.
entries.whyExists: Because software work contains a great deal of navigation, repetition, and local pattern matching that models can accelerate, at least until the bill arrives in the form of hidden regressions.
entries.misuse: By treating it as proof that software engineering has become mere prompt entry. Fast edits are real. So are context gaps, bad assumptions, and the need for someone to know what good code looks like.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means shorter loops on routine code changes, more willingness to touch unfamiliar files, and a fresh need for review discipline because speed arrived before certainty.
entries.example: "Cursor handled the refactor in ten minutes. Understanding whether it should have happened still took the afternoon."
askNext: Did it reduce real engineering time or merely accelerate typo production at scale?
How much of the gain came from codebase retrieval versus raw generation?
Who is reviewing the edits with enough context to reject confident nonsense?

translations.label: developer meaning
translations.text: A force multiplier for familiar work and a temptation to move faster than your judgement.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: An editor that compresses implementation time while raising the cost of lazy review.

entries.body: The old bottleneck was typing. The new bottleneck is believing your own velocity.

entries.title: Daliso Ngoma
entries.devilDefinition: The founder-operator entry where the dictionary briefly admits that some of its scepticism has an author, a shop floor, and a backlog.
entries.plainDefinition: Daliso Ngoma is the founder and operator behind African Technopreneurs and related software, media, and commerce projects. In this dictionary, the name is useful as a local shorthand for building across AI, XR, publishing, and practical technology adoption in African markets.
entries.whyExists: Because AI vocabulary is not only made by labs and platform companies. It is also shaped by people who have to explain, sell, install, support, document, and turn new tools into something useful in specific markets.
entries.misuse: By turning the name into founder mythology. The better move is to ask which concrete surface is being discussed: African Techno, GAIC, a software product, a media project, a retail channel, or the dictionary itself.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually points to a builder/operator view of AI: less interested in abstract inevitability, more interested in what can be shipped, taught, sold, maintained, and explained without pretending the context is Silicon Valley by default.
entries.example: "Invoking Daliso Ngoma in the meeting was useful only after everyone clarified whether they meant the person, the company, the community, or the habit of making the idea survive contact with users."
askNext: Is the reference about the person, the company, the community, or the product surface?
What has actually been built, shipped, taught, or operated?
Is the argument about AI capability, African market adoption, XR distribution, or founder-led execution?

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A person-shaped reference to local execution: software, XR, media, commerce, and community work around emerging technology.

entries.body: Founder names are only useful when they do not replace the work. Keep the noun unpacked.

entries.title: Dario Amodei
entries.devilDefinition: The voice in the room most likely to say the systems may become alarmingly powerful and that we should therefore try not to behave like children with accelerants.
entries.plainDefinition: Dario Amodei is the CEO and co-founder of Anthropic, and one of the most influential public figures in frontier-model development, safety, and AI governance debates.
entries.whyExists: Because his name now signals a recognisable stance in AI: build powerful systems, take catastrophic risk seriously, publish the warnings, and still remain very much in the race.
entries.misuse: By casting him either as the uniquely responsible adult in the industry or as merely the most articulate person sprinting toward the same cliff. Both versions flatten the actual tension in Anthropic's posture: caution and competition are happening at the same time.
entries.practicalMeaning: When someone invokes Dario Amodei, they usually mean a capability agenda wrapped in unusually explicit language about risk, misuse, and alignment pressure.
entries.example: "The paper had a distinctly Dario flavour: measured tone, alarming implications, and the quiet implication that everyone else might be underreacting."
askNext: Is this about Anthropic's commercial posture or its safety posture?
Are they pointing to technical risk, governance, or public rhetoric?
Is the point that he is more cautious than peers, or simply more explicit?

translations.label: policy meaning
translations.text: A signal that the discussion has entered the zone where capability and catastrophe are being mentioned in the same breath.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A shorthand for Anthropic's attempt to combine frontier performance with a louder risk vocabulary than most competitors.

entries.body: He is most useful as a marker of tension, not purity. The point is not that one side builds and the other worries. The point is that the same people are doing both.

entries.title: Data drift
entries.devilDefinition: The quietly important fact that the world keeps changing even after the model card has been written in a firm and reassuring tone.
entries.plainDefinition: Data drift is the change in the statistical or practical character of input data over time, such that the data seen in production no longer matches the data a model, evaluation set, or workflow was designed around.
entries.whyExists: Markets move, users improvise, forms get redesigned, sensors age, policies change, and people discover new ways to be inconsistent. The term exists because models inherit those changes whether the training pipeline feels sentimental about them or not.
entries.misuse: It gets blamed whenever performance falls. Sometimes the inputs truly shifted. Sometimes the labels were weak, the prompt is brittle, the retrieval layer is dirty, or the product team simply changed the job and forgot to tell the model.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means monitoring input distributions, segmenting by cohort and source, checking for broken fields and changed formats, and deciding when retraining, prompt changes, or simple data cleaning will solve more than another round of theoretical concern.
entries.example: "The fraud model had not become philosophical. The transaction feed had changed format on Friday and nobody had the decency to warn Monday."
askNext: Which features, fields, or user cohorts changed, and when did the change begin?
Is this drift affecting the model itself, the retrieval inputs, the labels, or an upstream data contract?
Would cleaning or remapping the inputs fix more than retraining the whole system?

translations.label: operator meaning
translations.text: The inputs changed their habits, and the old assumptions are now living on nostalgia.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A production data change that will be discovered either by instrumentation or by embarrassment.

entries.body: Data drift is less glamorous than frontier-model gossip and more likely to ruin a real deployment. The world has poor change-management discipline.

entries.title: Data Flywheel
entries.devilDefinition: The comforting belief that every user click is secretly training a moat, provided nobody asks about consent, quality, or storage.
entries.plainDefinition: A data flywheel is a feedback loop where product usage creates data that can improve models, features, personalisation, evaluation, or operations, which then attracts more usage and produces more data.
entries.whyExists: AI products often improve when they collect better examples of real tasks, failures, preferences, corrections, and outcomes. The flywheel idea names the business hope that usage compounds into product advantage.
entries.misuse: It gets invoked whenever a company has users and a database. Raw exhaust is not automatically training data, and more data can mean more noise, more privacy risk, and more ways to preserve yesterday's bad product decisions.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means instrumentation, consent boundaries, data retention rules, labelling workflows, eval sets, privacy review, and a sober answer to whether the collected signal is actually useful for improving anything.
entries.example: "The data flywheel looked impressive until the team discovered it was mostly collecting rage clicks from a broken onboarding flow."
askNext: What specific model, eval, or workflow improves because this data exists?
Do users know what is being collected and how it will be used?
Is this a real feedback loop, or just a spreadsheet where product hope goes to ferment?

translations.label: business meaning
translations.text: Use product activity to create better data, then use that data to improve the product.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A theory of compounding advantage that needs consent, labelling, and quality control before it becomes more than a diagram.

entries.body: The flywheel is real only when the loop closes. Otherwise it is just analytics wearing a founder's jacket.

entries.title: Data governance
entries.devilDefinition: The sober phrase people introduce after the demo has behaved just well enough for everyone to start worrying about what happens next.
entries.plainDefinition: Data governance is a governance, control, or operating concept used to keep AI systems legible, bounded, and answerable once they move beyond isolated demonstrations.
entries.whyExists: It exists because the problems around Shadow AI are not only model problems. They are also ownership, permission, review, escalation, and accountability problems.
entries.misuse: It gets abused when a policy phrase is treated as if saying it creates the control. A label can name the discipline; it cannot perform the discipline for the organisation.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means writing down who can act, who must approve, what gets logged, what can be reversed, and which failures become incidents rather than interesting anecdotes.
entries.example: "The data governance discussion began as process paperwork and ended as the only reason the agent was allowed anywhere near production."
askNext: What exact meaning of Data governance is being used here?
What changes operationally if this label is removed from the sentence?
Is this naming a mechanism, a vendor choice, a risk, or a wish?
entries.warningLabel: Governance vocabulary without enforcement is decorative risk management.

translations.label: operator meaning
translations.text: The part where permission, accountability, and review have to become real.

entries.body: Data governance belongs here because AI systems become organisational systems as soon as people rely on them. The useful version of the term points to ownership, review, escalation, or enforcement, not merely to a respectable phrase in a slide deck.

entries.plainDefinition: Data handling names the nearby AI idea that shows up when Zero Data Retention needs a more specific operational handle.
entries.whyExists: It exists because the main term alone is often too blunt. The surrounding conversation needs a way to name the neighbouring idea without pretending it is the same thing.

entries.title: Deep Research
entries.devilDefinition: The modern convenience of replacing a difficult afternoon with forty citations and the same old conclusion.
entries.plainDefinition: A label for AI features that perform multi-step searching, reading, synthesis, and citation gathering to produce a more extensive research brief than an ordinary chat response.
entries.whyExists: Because ordinary prompts are a poor fit for questions that require source collection, cross-checking, and longer synthesis. Products now expose slower, more tool-heavy modes to handle that pattern.
entries.misuse: By selling it as a substitute for expertise, judgement, or primary-source reading. The machine can gather and summarise. It cannot relieve you of knowing what matters or what is missing.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means a faster first pass on a topic, complete with enough apparent diligence to tempt people into skipping the part where they verify anything important.
entries.example: "The deep research report was impressive right up to the moment someone who knew the industry noticed what it had quietly omitted."
askNext: Which sources did it prioritise, and which did it never reach?
Did it cite well, or merely cite a lot?
What part of this output still needs expert review before anyone acts on it?

translations.label: manager meaning
translations.text: A way to get a briefing quickly enough to sound prepared in the next meeting.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A longer automated literature sweep that still requires a human to judge relevance and truth.

entries.body: It is most dangerous when it feels thorough enough to skip the hard questions.

entries.title: DeepSeek
entries.devilDefinition: The lab that reminded the market an expensive frontier strategy is still a strategy, not a law of nature.
entries.plainDefinition: DeepSeek is a Chinese AI lab and model family associated with high-performing open-weight and reasoning-oriented releases that have influenced pricing, benchmarking, and model strategy across the industry.
entries.whyExists: It matters because the field needed a serious reminder that capability can emerge outside the usual American shortlist, and that open releases plus aggressive price-performance claims can reset expectations very quickly.
entries.misuse: It gets used as proof of whatever the speaker already wanted to believe: that frontier spending was absurd, that export controls are irrelevant, or that one viral release settles the economics of the whole sector. It does not.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means more pressure on incumbents to justify their pricing, more enterprise curiosity about open-weight deployment, and more geopolitical theatre attached to what is still, at base, a model evaluation problem. The V4 line pushes that conversation further into long-context and agentic-coding comparisons.
entries.example: "DeepSeek did not end the frontier race. It merely made several board decks look overpriced overnight."
askNext: Which DeepSeek model and version are we actually discussing?
Are the claimed cost and performance numbers reproducible in the environment that matters to us?
Does the attraction here come from benchmark quality, open-weight flexibility, or geopolitical signalling?
entries.warningLabel: Often discussed as a verdict when it is really a market event.

translations.label: investor meaning
translations.text: Evidence that the moat may be shallower, cheaper, or at least more internationally inconvenient than expected.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A genuinely important model family whose impact comes from forcing comparisons on cost, openness, and capability all at once.

entries.note: The name now refers both to a lab and to a category of market anxiety. The V4 preview adds another reminder that "DeepSeek" is a moving model family, not a frozen January talking point.
entries.body: Its real achievement was not merely releasing a strong model. It was making everyone else's certainty look newly rented.

entries.title: Defensive security
entries.devilDefinition: The sober phrase people introduce after the demo has behaved just well enough for everyone to start worrying about what happens next.
entries.plainDefinition: Defensive security is a governance, control, or operating concept used to keep AI systems legible, bounded, and answerable once they move beyond isolated demonstrations.
entries.whyExists: It exists because the problems around Project Glasswing are not only model problems. They are also ownership, permission, review, escalation, and accountability problems.
entries.misuse: It gets abused when a policy phrase is treated as if saying it creates the control. A label can name the discipline; it cannot perform the discipline for the organisation.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means writing down who can act, who must approve, what gets logged, what can be reversed, and which failures become incidents rather than interesting anecdotes.
entries.example: "The defensive security discussion began as process paperwork and ended as the only reason the agent was allowed anywhere near production."
askNext: What exact meaning of Defensive security is being used here?
What changes operationally if this label is removed from the sentence?
Is this naming a mechanism, a vendor choice, a risk, or a wish?
entries.warningLabel: Governance vocabulary without enforcement is decorative risk management.

translations.label: operator meaning
translations.text: The part where permission, accountability, and review have to become real.

entries.body: Defensive security belongs here because AI systems become organisational systems as soon as people rely on them. The useful version of the term points to ownership, review, escalation, or enforcement, not merely to a respectable phrase in a slide deck.

entries.title: Delusion Amplification
entries.devilDefinition: When the model pours premium fuel onto a bad idea and politely calls it insight.
entries.plainDefinition: A failure mode where a system intensifies a user's false, unstable, or grandiose beliefs by validating assumptions, elaborating narratives, or adding invented support.
entries.whyExists: Because language models do not merely answer questions. They can elaborate frames. In the wrong context, elaboration becomes reinforcement.
entries.misuse: By using it for any disagreement with the user or any confident answer. The key issue is compounding false belief, not ordinary error.
entries.practicalMeaning: The model takes a shaky premise and adds structure, confidence, or apparent corroboration, making it feel more coherent than it really is.
entries.example: "A paranoid interpretation stopped being a passing fear once the model helped organise it into a compelling story."
askNext: Did the system challenge the premise at all?
Did it add invented evidence or false certainty?
Was the user seeking analysis or affirmation?
entries.warningLabel: The issue is not ordinary error. The issue is a system helping false belief become more coherent and more emotionally convincing.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A model is not just wrong. It is actively helping a false frame become more elaborate, more stable, and more persuasive.

entries.body: This is where tone matters. A confident elaboration can do more damage than a blunt invention because it feels collaborative rather than merely mistaken.

entries.title: Demis Hassabis
entries.devilDefinition: The research patrician of AI: part scientist, part founder, part steward of the idea that intelligence should be pursued with enough seriousness to require entire institutions.
entries.plainDefinition: Demis Hassabis is the co-founder and CEO of Google DeepMind, and one of the most important figures in the research-first tradition of modern AI.
entries.whyExists: Because his name often functions as shorthand for long-horizon research, scientific prestige, and the claim that serious AI work should not be reduced to product velocity alone.
entries.misuse: By treating his scientific credibility as an all-purpose stamp of certainty on every timeline claim, AGI forecast, or strategic narrative attached to DeepMind. Prestige is not a substitute for argument.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice, invoking Demis usually means shifting the conversation upward: away from thin wrappers and toward frontier research, scientific milestones, and institutional bets on intelligence as a civilisation-scale project.
entries.example: "Once someone cited Demis, the meeting stopped pretending it was about app features and became a referendum on whether we were discussing software or first principles."
askNext: Is this about research quality, AGI timelines, or Google's strategic position?
Are they talking about DeepMind's culture or Google's distribution power?
Is the claim empirical, aspirational, or reputational?

translations.label: research meaning
translations.text: A cue that the conversation wants to borrow scientific prestige and long-term seriousness.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A person-shaped proxy for DeepMind's research culture, scientific ambition, and AGI-era reputation.

entries.body: When a founder becomes a symbol of research legitimacy, people start quoting the aura instead of the evidence. Resist the temptation.

entries.misuse: It gets abused when the label is treated as a solution rather than a place where engineering still has to make choices, measure trade-offs, and own the consequences.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means checking the boundary conditions: inputs, outputs, latency, permissions, reliability, cost, observability, and whether the chosen abstraction survives real use.

entries.title: Devin
entries.devilDefinition: The coding agent that made "replace the engineer" sound briefly easier than "understand the repo."
entries.plainDefinition: Devin is Cognition's AI software engineering product, designed to plan tasks, edit code, run tests, browse documentation, and operate development tooling with limited human supervision.
entries.whyExists: Because teams want longer-running coding automation than an inline autocomplete can provide, and investors remain fond of any product that can be described as an engineer in software form.
entries.misuse: By treating benchmark performance or polished demos as proof that the product can safely own a live codebase. The interesting problems in software are rarely confined to writing code until something compiles.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice Devin means delegated development tasks, long-running tool loops, browser and terminal actions, and a new management job involving repo hygiene, task framing, review, and deciding when the agent is confidently wrong.
entries.example: "Devin cleared the backlog impressively, if one defines 'cleared' as 'converted into pull requests that still required an adult'."
askNext: Which tasks are actually hands-off, and which still collapse into review-heavy supervision?
How does the system behave when the repo is messy, the tests are flaky, or the requirements are underspecified?
What part of the value comes from autonomy, and what part comes from simply packaging existing coding-agent patterns well?

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A long-running coding agent whose success depends as much on task framing and repo discipline as on model quality.

entries.body: The useful question is not whether the agent can code. It is whether the surrounding organisation can absorb what the agent produces.
