entries.title: DGX
entries.devilDefinition: The machine you buy when "we need serious AI infrastructure" graduates from a slogan into a capital request with cooling requirements.
entries.plainDefinition: DGX is NVIDIA's family of integrated AI systems that bundle high-end GPUs, networking, software, and reference architecture into a packaged platform for training and large-scale inference workloads.
entries.whyExists: Because many teams do not want to assemble their own AI compute stack from scratch, and NVIDIA would understandably prefer to sell the whole temple rather than only the candles.
entries.misuse: By using DGX as shorthand for any powerful GPU server, or by treating the purchase of one as proof that a company has an AI strategy rather than a newly expensive room.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means dense, vendor-shaped compute for organisations willing to pay for an integrated system, faster deployment, and closer alignment with NVIDIA's view of how serious AI infrastructure should look.
entries.example: "The announcement said the company was investing in frontier capability. The facilities team heard 'DGX' and immediately started asking about power, airflow, and delivery dates."
askNext: Do we need an integrated NVIDIA system, or just access to equivalent compute?
Is this for training, inference, experimentation, or institutional theatre?
What operational burden are we buying along with the speed and convenience?

translations.label: buyer meaning
translations.text: A bundled way to acquire serious AI compute without pretending integration work is free.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: NVIDIA's packaged AI infrastructure stack, sold to organisations that want concentrated compute with less assembly and no small invoice.

entries.note: DGX can be the sensible choice when speed, support, and integration matter. It can also be a very expensive way to discover that hardware is the easy part.
entries.body: DGX is what happens when the GPU story becomes an appliance story. It is not just more chips. It is a deliberate attempt to make the surrounding system part of the sale.

entries.title: DGX Cloud
entries.devilDefinition: DGX Cloud is the kind of vendor or product reference that turns architecture, procurement, and loyalty into the same conversation.
entries.plainDefinition: DGX Cloud is treated in this dictionary as a named vendor, product, feature, platform, or branded surface that appears in AI product, procurement, or implementation conversations.

entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means someone should ask what is being bought, integrated, routed through, depended on, or compared before the logo is allowed to stand in for the architecture.

entries.title: Digital Worker
entries.devilDefinition: Workflow software reintroduced to management as headcount, because procurement enjoys a dramatic entrance.
entries.plainDefinition: "Digital worker" is a vendor phrase for automation software, often AI-assisted, that is sold as though it were a bounded employee capable of handling routine business tasks across systems.
entries.whyExists: Because buyers understand roles more quickly than architectures, and vendors would rather sell "an accounts payable worker" than "a model-mediated process layer with brittle integrations".
entries.misuse: By speaking as if software has judgement, accountability, or broad role competence simply because it can complete a workflow. Calling it a worker does not make it adaptable, trustworthy, or employable in the human sense.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means a mix of rules, integrations, model calls, approval steps, and exception handling wrapped in labour metaphors so the budget can be compared to a salary instead of a software line item.
entries.example: "The demo promised a digital worker for claims processing. The implementation delivered an approval queue with a more theatrical org chart."
askNext: What exact tasks can this system complete without human rescue?
Where does accountability sit when the workflow makes a bad decision?
Are we buying durable automation or just renting an employee metaphor?
entries.warningLabel: If the product is introduced as a colleague, ask who will be blamed when it behaves like software.

translations.label: sales meaning
translations.text: A software budget item reframed as operational staffing.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: An automation stack with model-assisted steps and a carefully managed definition of success.

entries.body: The phrase is useful mostly as a diagnostic. When the language turns employment-shaped, someone is usually trying to smuggle software limits past a budget committee.

entries.title: Distillation
entries.devilDefinition: The art of teaching a smaller model to imitate a larger one, then introducing the result as efficiency rather than inherited opinion.
entries.plainDefinition: Distillation is a training approach in which a smaller or cheaper model learns from the outputs, preferences, or intermediate signals of a larger model so it can reproduce some of its performance at lower cost.
entries.whyExists: It exists because frontier-grade behaviour is expensive to serve everywhere. If a smaller system can absorb enough of a stronger model's behaviour, products gain speed, lower inference cost, and a better shot at running locally or at scale.
entries.misuse: It is misused when people describe the resulting model as independent genius rather than compressed imitation. Distillation can preserve capability surprisingly well, but it also preserves blind spots, style, and hidden assumptions.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means generating a large synthetic training set from a stronger model, fine-tuning a cheaper one on that data, and accepting a careful trade-off between cost, latency, and capability.
entries.example: "Their distilled model was impressively cheap, which was another way of saying they had purchased fewer surprises per request."
askNext: What behaviour was the student model trained to copy?
Which capabilities held up after distillation, and which quietly fell off?
Did the process reduce cost without merely packaging the same errors more efficiently?

translations.label: exec meaning
translations.text: Keep most of the magic, lose most of the bill.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Training a smaller model to mimic a stronger one closely enough to be cheaper and still useful.

entries.note: Distillation is often described as model compression, but the interesting part is not just size. It is which behaviours survive the squeeze.
entries.body: The smaller model may be faster, cheaper, and easier to deploy. It is still carrying somebody else's worldview in a lighter suitcase.

entries.title: Elon Musk
entries.devilDefinition: What happens when AI acquires a promoter, a distribution engine, and a permanent ability to turn product discussion into spectacle.
entries.plainDefinition: Elon Musk is the founder of xAI and one of the most polarising public figures in AI. In practice, his significance in AI discourse comes from a mix of capital, platform reach, engineering ambition, political attention, and narrative force.
entries.whyExists: Because Musk is never merely a person in these discussions. His name functions as a compressed argument about speed, attention, ecosystem control, ideological posture, and whether theatrical force is itself a strategic asset.
entries.misuse: By treating him as either pure substance or pure noise. The useful position is less flattering to everyone's instincts: the spectacle matters because attention, distribution, and organisational velocity all materially affect how AI products land.
entries.practicalMeaning: When people invoke Elon Musk in AI, they usually mean xAI's ambitions, the Grok ecosystem, or the broader reality that infrastructure, media reach, and public controversy can be bundled into one strategic instrument.
entries.example: "Once Elon entered the product discussion, it acquired three new plotlines, a stronger ideological charge, and a lower chance of remaining about the product."
askNext: Is the relevance technical, political, commercial, or cultural?
Are they talking about xAI's systems, Musk's distribution power, or both?
Is this claim about model quality, market attention, or theatre with infrastructure attached?
entries.warningLabel: Attention is not the same thing as technical evidence, but it would also be foolish to pretend attention does not matter.

translations.label: media meaning
translations.text: A guarantee that the topic will become bigger, louder, and less tidy within minutes.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A shorthand for xAI plus the surrounding machinery of audience, controversy, capital, and platform leverage.

entries.note: Date-sensitive point: xAI announced on February 2, 2026 that SpaceX had acquired xAI. For that reason, it is safer to treat Musk here as xAI's founder and dominant public force than to casually repeat older standalone-company titles without context.
entries.body: With some founders, the product explains the persona. With Musk, the persona becomes part of the product whether anyone requested that feature or not.

entries.title: Embeddings
entries.devilDefinition: The act of turning words, sentences, or images into coordinates so that "similar enough" can be calculated instead of argued about.
entries.plainDefinition: Embeddings are numerical vector representations of data, such as text or images, designed so that semantically similar items are located near one another in vector space. They are commonly used for retrieval, clustering, recommendation, and deduplication.
entries.whyExists: Exact keyword matching is often too brittle for messy human language. Embeddings exist so systems can compare meaning approximately rather than matching only literal words.
entries.misuse: They are sometimes spoken about as if they were compact containers of all meaning, truth, and business strategy. They are not. An embedding is useful geometry, not metaphysics. Its quality depends on the model, the data, the task, and the comparison method.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means encoding text into vectors, storing those vectors somewhere searchable, and using nearest-neighbour techniques to find candidates that look semantically related. They are the quiet workhorses behind a lot of so-called magical retrieval.
entries.example: "The search improved after they switched from keywords to embeddings, not because the system got wiser, but because 'refund status' and 'where is my money' finally stopped looking unrelated."
askNext: What embedding model is being used, and for which task?
How are similarity, ranking, and filtering being evaluated?
What happens when semantically similar items are still operationally irrelevant?

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A way of mapping data into numbers so retrieval can work by distance, not exact wording.

entries.body: Embeddings are one of the best examples of AI becoming less mysterious when you describe them properly. They are not memory crystals. They are useful numerical representations with clear limits.

entries.title: Enterprise agentic solutions
entries.devilDefinition: Enterprise agentic solutions is the kind of vendor or product reference that turns architecture, procurement, and loyalty into the same conversation.
entries.plainDefinition: Enterprise agentic solutions is treated in this dictionary as a named vendor, product, feature, platform, or branded surface that appears in AI product, procurement, or implementation conversations.
entries.whyExists: The term matters because discussions around Scale AI often need to separate the actual technical choice from the brand, distribution channel, contract, or ecosystem wrapped around it.
entries.misuse: It gets abused when the name is used as a substitute for specifying the exact product surface, model, API, entitlement, pricing constraint, or operational dependency under discussion.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means someone should ask what is being bought, integrated, routed through, depended on, or compared before the logo is allowed to stand in for the architecture.
entries.example: "Enterprise agentic solutions came up in the review, which was useful only after the team clarified whether they meant the product, the platform, the model family, or the vendor relationship."

entries.title: Eval Harness
entries.devilDefinition: The machinery teams build once they realise strong opinions about model quality are not, in fact, a release process.
entries.plainDefinition: An eval harness is the framework or runner used to execute repeatable evaluations against models, prompts, tools, or agent workflows and collect comparable results.
entries.whyExists: It exists because manual spot checks do not scale, model behaviour drifts, prompts regress quietly, and agent systems fail in more ways than a demo can comfortably admit.
entries.misuse: The harness gets mistaken for truth when it only reflects the cases you bothered to encode. A tidy dashboard is still compatible with spectacular blind spots.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means versioned datasets, scorers, task traces, cost accounting, regression thresholds, and a growing pile of awkward examples nobody wants to drop because they keep catching something embarrassing.
entries.example: "The eval harness did not make the agent good. It merely prevented the team from declaring victory after each plausible-looking run."
askNext: Which real failures are represented in the suite, and which remain folklore?
What metrics are being scored automatically, and what still needs human judgement?
Does the harness catch regressions before release, or just document them after the fact?

translations.label: engineering meaning
translations.text: The repeatable test rig standing between a model change and an avoidable incident.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A structured way to replay known cases often enough that quality becomes an argument with evidence.

entries.body: Every mature stack eventually invents a harness. AI teams simply get there after calling the same failure "surprising" three times.

entries.title: Eval leakage
entries.devilDefinition: What happens when the exam keeps whispering the answers into the curriculum and everyone still calls the grade objective.
entries.plainDefinition: Eval leakage is the contamination of an evaluation by training data, tuning loops, prompt iteration, or repeated rehearsal in ways that make measured performance look more general than it really is.
entries.whyExists: The term exists because AI systems are improved by repeated exposure to tasks, examples, and feedback. Without a name for leakage, teams can mistake familiarity with the test for competence on the real job.
entries.misuse: It gets invoked every time a benchmark result looks strong. Not every good score is leakage. The phrase is useful when the separation between training and evaluation has genuinely weakened, not when someone merely dislikes the number.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means guarding holdouts, rotating fresh tasks, avoiding public overexposure of narrow eval sets, and checking whether the optimisation loop has quietly learned the taste of the exam instead of the substance of the task.
entries.example: "The leaderboard rise looked impressive until the team realised their bug-fix prompt had been tuned against the very eval cases now serving as proof."
askNext: How clean is the separation between the data used to improve the system and the data used to judge it?
What parts of this eval have been exposed repeatedly to prompt tuning, red-teaming, or release review?
Do we have any fresh or time-shifted checks that the system has not already rehearsed?

translations.label: research meaning
translations.text: The measurement has stopped being as independent as the chart implies.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: The system has learned the exam too well for the exam to remain a trustworthy proxy.

entries.body: Eval leakage is not a moral category. It is a measurement problem with career consequences, which is why people keep trying to discuss it as if it were merely philosophical.

entries.title: Eval-Driven Development
entries.devilDefinition: The radical idea that if your product depends on stochastic behaviour, you should probably measure something before calling the demo a platform.
entries.plainDefinition: Eval-driven development is the practice of using targeted evaluations to guide prompt changes, model selection, tool design, and release decisions for AI systems.
entries.whyExists: Traditional software tests do not fully capture AI behaviour, while pure vibes scale poorly once money, customers, or reputations are involved. Teams need a repeatable way to detect regressions and compare alternatives.
entries.misuse: It is misused when a handful of flattering examples are promoted to an evaluation strategy, or when teams treat proxy metrics as if they were the product itself. An eval can discipline judgement. It cannot replace it.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means curated test sets, grading rubrics, automated checks, and before-after comparisons tied to real tasks. The mature version influences release gates. The immature version produces a dashboard nobody trusts but everyone screenshots.
entries.example: "Eval-driven development arrived the day they stopped arguing about prompts in Slack and started arguing about failure rates instead."
askNext: Which behaviours matter enough to measure continuously?
Do the evals reflect production tasks, or merely the tasks that are easiest to score?
What happens when the metric improves but the user experience quietly worsens?
entries.warningLabel: A useful discipline, especially when it resists becoming a PowerPoint ritual.

translations.label: team meaning
translations.text: Define the failure cases and score them before changing the system.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Replacing prompt debates with measurable arguments, then discovering the measurements also need supervision.

entries.body: The concept is sound. The danger begins when the organisation assumes that once a number exists, the thinking has been done.

entries.title: Evals
entries.devilDefinition: The discipline of finding out whether the system works before reality does it for you in public.
entries.plainDefinition: Evals are structured evaluations used to measure model or system performance against defined criteria. They may include benchmark tasks, human review, automated checks, red-team cases, regression tests, or task-specific business metrics.
entries.whyExists: Models are stochastic, context-dependent, and often impressive right up until they are deployed. Evals exist to replace anecdote with repeatable evidence and to catch regressions when prompts, models, or orchestration change.
entries.misuse: The word gets thrown around when teams have a benchmark slide and a lot of faith. Real evals are continuous, tied to the actual task, and uncomfortable enough to surface failure. If the evaluation only confirms what the team already hoped, it may be marketing in a lab coat.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means curated test sets, scored outputs, human raters, regression suites, production monitoring, and painful arguments about what "good enough" means for this use case. Good evals are part of product operations, not just research hygiene.
entries.example: "They thought the release was safe because the demo looked smoother. The evals later showed the new prompt quietly doubled the rate of invented citations."
askNext: What failure modes does this eval suite actually test?
How often is it run, and what changes trigger regression checks?
Are we measuring benchmark elegance or real task performance?

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A disciplined way of checking whether the system still deserves confidence after you changed something.

entries.body: The most revealing evals are usually the least glamorous ones: domain cases, nasty edge conditions, and boring regression checks that stop the product from drifting into fiction.

entries.title: Fallback policy
entries.devilDefinition: The sober phrase people introduce after the demo has behaved just well enough for everyone to start worrying about what happens next.
entries.plainDefinition: Fallback policy is a governance, control, or operating concept used to keep AI systems legible, bounded, and answerable once they move beyond isolated demonstrations.
entries.whyExists: It exists because the problems around Model Router are not only model problems. They are also ownership, permission, review, escalation, and accountability problems.
entries.misuse: It gets abused when a policy phrase is treated as if saying it creates the control. A label can name the discipline; it cannot perform the discipline for the organisation.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means writing down who can act, who must approve, what gets logged, what can be reversed, and which failures become incidents rather than interesting anecdotes.
entries.example: "The fallback policy discussion began as process paperwork and ended as the only reason the agent was allowed anywhere near production."
askNext: What exact meaning of Fallback policy is being used here?
What changes operationally if this label is removed from the sentence?
Is this naming a mechanism, a vendor choice, a risk, or a wish?
entries.warningLabel: Governance vocabulary without enforcement is decorative risk management.

translations.label: operator meaning
translations.text: The part where permission, accountability, and review have to become real.

entries.body: Fallback policy belongs here because AI systems become organisational systems as soon as people rely on them. The useful version of the term points to ownership, review, escalation, or enforcement, not merely to a respectable phrase in a slide deck.

entries.title: Figma
entries.devilDefinition: The design file everyone agrees is the source of truth until code, product, and reality each file a competing claim.
entries.plainDefinition: Figma is a collaborative design platform used for interface design, prototyping, design systems, developer handoff, and increasingly AI-assisted design-to-code workflows through Dev Mode and MCP integrations.
entries.whyExists: It exists because teams need a shared place to design, discuss, inspect, and hand off product interfaces without passing static files around like office furniture.
entries.misuse: It gets treated as if a neat Figma file guarantees a neat product. Dev Mode, component metadata, and MCP context can improve implementation, but they do not settle product ambiguity, missing states, accessibility, or the difference between a component library and a living app.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice Figma means design-system source material, inspectable component context, comments, prototypes, and a growing bridge into coding agents that can read design intent before writing UI.
entries.example: "The agent implemented the Figma screen accurately, including the part where nobody had designed the error state."
askNext: Is the Figma file current enough to guide implementation?
Which design tokens, components, and states are actually represented?
Does the agent receive structured design context, or just a screenshot with confidence?
entries.warningLabel: Design context helps agents. It does not absolve anyone from checking the interface.

translations.label: designer meaning
translations.text: The shared design surface where intent, comments, components, and unresolved arguments gather.

translations.label: engineer meaning
translations.text: Useful structured context, as long as the design file has not become a historical document.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A strong handoff and collaboration platform that agents can now query more directly, not a guarantee that the app will make sense.

entries.body: Figma's AI-era importance is not just that designs can become code. It is that design context can become part of the agent's working memory before the first component is written.

entries.title: Fine-tuning
entries.devilDefinition: Taking a large model and sending it to finishing school so it stops answering like the open internet and starts answering like your use case.
entries.plainDefinition: Fine-tuning is the process of further training a pre-existing model on a narrower dataset so that its behaviour shifts toward a specific domain, style, format, or task.
entries.whyExists: General-purpose models are broad, not bespoke. Fine-tuning exists for cases where prompting alone is too unstable, too expensive, or too weak to reliably shape behaviour.
entries.misuse: It is often sold as the universal answer whenever prompts disappoint. In reality, many problems blamed on a lack of fine-tuning are retrieval problems, product design problems, or evaluation problems. Fine-tuning can help, but it can also overfit and create fresh failure modes.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means curating a task-specific dataset, deciding how much of the base model to adapt, training on that smaller dataset, and checking whether the resulting behaviour improved enough to justify the effort and operational complexity.
entries.example: "They did not fine-tune because the team loved custom models; they did it because the required output format had to be boringly consistent."
askNext: What behaviour are we trying to change that prompting cannot hold steady?
What data will teach that behaviour without teaching unwanted shortcuts?
How will we know the tuned model is better, not just narrower?

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A targeted attempt to move model behaviour with additional training rather than endless prompt cajoling.

entries.body: Good fine-tuning is usually boring in the best way. It removes repeated prompt hacks and makes behaviour steadier. Bad fine-tuning makes a model narrower, more brittle, and harder to reason about.

entries.title: Frontier Model
entries.devilDefinition: A model said to be at the frontier, which often means "very capable, very expensive, and deeply useful to mention in a fundraising narrative."
entries.plainDefinition: A frontier model is generally understood to mean one of the most capable and resource-intensive models available at a given time, usually near the top end of performance on a range of tasks. It is not a sharply defined scientific category.
entries.whyExists: The term exists because the field needed a way to talk about the leading edge of capability without listing every model individually. It can be useful shorthand when discussing high-end systems, their risks, and their costs.
entries.misuse: It is often treated as a formal badge rather than a loose ranking label. Vendors, commentators, and investors may use it to imply inevitability, superiority, or strategic importance beyond what the actual use case warrants.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means a large, expensive model family with broad capability, strong benchmarks, and significant operational or policy attention. For many products, the question is not whether a model is frontier. The question is whether it is necessary.
entries.example: "The company pitched a frontier-model strategy. Operations translated that into a calmer sentence about high cost, external dependency, and unclear product fit."
askNext: Frontier by which metric: benchmark spread, multimodal breadth, tool use, or cost-to-quality ratio?
Does the use case actually need a top-tier model, or just a reliable one?
What trade-offs come with using the most capable model available?
entries.warningLabel: Useful shorthand, not a formal category. Often as much status signal as technical description.

translations.label: investor meaning
translations.text: This company is close enough to the top of the capability race to sound strategically important.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A high-end model near the current performance frontier, whether or not your workload needs that much firepower.

entries.body: The term becomes most useful when followed immediately by the phrase "for what task?" Otherwise it tends to drift into status perfume.

entries.title: Function Calling
entries.devilDefinition: Giving the model permission to request an action, while reminding everyone that requesting an action is not the same as doing it safely.
entries.plainDefinition: Function calling is a pattern where a model is asked to produce structured arguments for a predefined tool or function, and the surrounding application then executes that tool in ordinary software.
entries.whyExists: Models are good at interpreting language but bad at directly mutating real systems without control. Function calling exists to separate decision-making about which action to take from the actual execution of that action.
entries.misuse: It gets described as if the model itself is performing the external action. Usually it is not. The model proposes the call. Your software validates it, executes it, and handles the consequences. If that surrounding code is sloppy, the elegance of the schema will not save you.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means JSON schemas, argument validation, permission controls, retries, and post-tool reasoning. Useful systems treat function calling as structured intent, not an excuse to trust a model near production infrastructure.
entries.example: "The assistant did not refund the customer by itself; it produced a structured request, the service checked policy, and only then did the refund tool run."
askNext: Who validates the arguments before execution?
What tools is the model allowed to call, and under what conditions?
How are failures, partial successes, and risky actions surfaced?

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: The model emits a structured request; the real system still has to decide whether to trust, reject, or execute it.

entries.body: Function calling is one of the clearest places where AI product language can mislead. The impressive part is not merely that the model chose a tool. The important part is whether the surrounding system kept that choice legible and safe.

entries.title: GAIC
entries.devilDefinition: A local AI community, which is what happens when "the ecosystem" stops being a slide and starts needing a venue, a WhatsApp thread, and someone to bring people into the room.
entries.plainDefinition: GAIC stands for Gauteng AI Community: a local community label for people in and around Gauteng who are learning, building, discussing, and applying AI in practical contexts.
entries.whyExists: AI adoption is local long before it is national. Communities like this give practitioners, founders, students, and curious operators a place to compare tools, share context, find collaborators, and translate global AI noise into regional practice.
entries.misuse: By treating the acronym as proof that an ecosystem already exists at scale. A community is only real if people keep showing up, learning from each other, and turning contact into work.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means meetups, demos, informal support, founder conversations, learning loops, and a local signal that AI is being worked through by people close enough to share constraints.
entries.example: "GAIC mattered less as an acronym than as proof that the AI conversation in Gauteng had somewhere to land between LinkedIn enthusiasm and actual projects."
askNext: Who is the community for: builders, founders, students, operators, or all of them?
What does participation produce beyond attendance?
Which local constraints does the community help people solve?

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A local AI learning and builder community, useful when it turns enthusiasm into relationships, projects, and shared practice.

entries.body: Communities are infrastructure with worse accounting. When they work, they quietly reduce the cost of starting.

entries.title: Gemini
entries.devilDefinition: A name used for models, apps, and ecosystem positioning, which keeps everyone slightly unsure whether they are comparing software, model families, or brand architecture.
entries.plainDefinition: Gemini is Google's brand used across both AI models and user-facing products. Depending on context, it may refer to a model family, an assistant experience, or a broader product layer within Google's ecosystem.
entries.whyExists: The term matters because Google uses Gemini across several surfaces. Without context, claims about Gemini can blur model behaviour, app experience, workspace integration, and vendor strategy.
entries.misuse: It gets treated as if a result seen in one Google surface automatically describes every Gemini model or deployment context. That is rarely true. Tooling, integration, context, and product constraints all change the experience.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means either a Google model family or a Google product experience using those models. Serious comparison requires pinning down which one.
entries.example: "The team said Gemini handled the task well. After some digging, this meant a specific workspace flow, not a portable judgement about every Gemini model."
askNext: Do you mean the Gemini app, a Gemini model family, or a Google product using Gemini behind the scenes?
Which version or surface produced the result?
What tool access or integration context shaped the outcome?
entries.warningLabel: The brand spans models and products. Ask which one is being discussed.
entries.note: Gemini conversations often confuse integrated product behaviour with base model ability. Those are related, but not identical, claims.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A Google-branded AI surface whose exact meaning depends on which product or model someone is referring to.

entries.body: The safest follow-up sentence is short: "Which Gemini?" It rescues a surprising number of meetings.

entries.title: Gemini 3.1 Flash Live
entries.devilDefinition: The voice model meant to make real-time conversation feel less like alternating voicemail and more like software that noticed you were still speaking.
entries.plainDefinition: Gemini 3.1 Flash Live is Google's real-time audio and voice model for live dialogue, voice-first agents, and conversational experiences that need lower latency and stronger turn-taking.
entries.whyExists: Spoken AI feels broken the moment latency, interruptions, or tonal comprehension fall behind human patience. A live audio model exists because real-time interaction is less forgiving than text and far less polite about failure.
entries.misuse: It gets marketed as natural conversation when many deployments are still glorified phone trees with better diction. Smoother audio helps. It does not automatically produce better reasoning, better policies, or a better answer.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means voice agents, customer-support flows, Search Live, and assistant products where handling interruption, noisy environments, and conversational rhythm matters as much as the content of the reply.
entries.example: "Flash Live made the assistant sound far more human, which only sharpened the need for the underlying judgement to improve as well."
askNext: Does it handle interruptions and background noise reliably?
Which gains come from the model itself, and which come from surrounding orchestration?
Are we building a useful voice agent, or simply a smoother queue?
entries.warningLabel: A pleasant voice is not the same thing as good judgement.

translations.label: developer meaning
translations.text: A faster, more robust live audio model for building voice-first experiences.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: The same assistant ambition, but with less lag between misunderstanding and sound.

entries.body: Realtime AI is a harsher examiner than chat. The pauses are shorter, the mistakes are louder, and the illusion of competence has less time to hide.

entries.title: Gemini 3.1 Flash-Lite
entries.devilDefinition: The cheaper model tier whose chief virtue is letting ambitious product managers scale their optimism by the million tokens.
entries.plainDefinition: Gemini 3.1 Flash-Lite is Google's lower-cost, lower-latency Gemini 3.1 model for high-volume workloads where throughput and unit economics matter more than maximal frontier capability.
entries.whyExists: Most production AI systems are constrained less by grand theory than by latency budgets, API spend, and how much traffic the business intends to survive. A cheaper fast model tier exists because the invoice also attends the architecture review.
entries.misuse: It gets framed as though cheaper automatically means strategically sufficient. Sometimes it does. Sometimes it simply spreads weaker judgement across more requests and gives procurement a pleasant afternoon.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means translation, moderation, UI generation, bulk inference, and the usual industrial workloads where modest capability improvements are less important than response speed and cost discipline.
entries.example: "Flash-Lite was exactly the right model until someone quietly expanded the use case from 'classify this' to 'think for us'."
askNext: Is the bottleneck latency, token cost, or answer quality?
Are we choosing the smallest model that clears the bar, or the cheapest one that lets the slide deck survive?
What errors become acceptable once the per-request price drops?

translations.label: platform meaning
translations.text: A cost-efficient model tier meant for workloads where volume matters more than dramatic cleverness.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A sensible default for many production calls, right up to the moment someone asks it to be profound.

entries.body: Model progress now arrives in at least two currencies: capability and affordability. Flash-Lite is the reminder that the second one decides far more deployments than the keynote usually admits.

entries.title: Gemma
entries.devilDefinition: Google's way of demonstrating that it, too, can participate in the open-model conversation without misplacing the rest of the product empire.
entries.plainDefinition: Gemma is Google's family of lighter-weight models released for developers to run, fine-tune, or adapt outside the company's main hosted product surfaces.
entries.whyExists: Many teams want smaller models for local use, private deployment, research, or fine-tuning. Google also benefits from having a developer-facing model family that can circulate beyond closed API products and keep it visible in the open-model ecosystem.
entries.misuse: It gets confused with Gemini, treated as a single model, or described as if "small" automatically means cheap, safe, or easy to deploy. As usual, the useful details live in the specific variant, size, licence terms, and workload.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice Gemma usually means a family of relatively lightweight Google models for experimentation, local inference, edge deployment, and fine-tuning work where a giant hosted model would be excessive, costly, or simply inconvenient.
entries.example: "They chose Gemma for the local prototype because the requirement was responsiveness on-device, not a spiritual commitment to the largest model available."
askNext: Which Gemma variant and size are we evaluating?
Does the workload actually benefit from a smaller local model, or are we just allergic to APIs?
How do quality, latency, and privacy trade-offs compare with hosted alternatives on the real task?
entries.warningLabel: Model family, not a single stable answer to the question.

entries.title: GEO
entries.devilDefinition: SEO after a rebrand, a haircut, and a fresh promise to investors.
entries.plainDefinition: An emerging term for trying to increase a brand's visibility inside AI-generated answers, summaries, and recommendation layers rather than only in traditional search engine rankings.
entries.whyExists: Because people noticed that users increasingly ask chat systems instead of typing ten blue links into a search box.
entries.misuse: By pretending it is a wholly new discipline rather than a messy blend of discoverability, authority, structured content, citations, technical hygiene, and brand presence.
entries.practicalMeaning: Creating clear, well-structured, cited, machine-legible content that AI systems and retrieval layers can understand, quote, or prefer.
entries.example: "The agency sold GEO as a revolution, then quietly delivered schema, better copy, stronger pages, and clearer source material."
askNext: Which engines or assistants do they mean?
Are they measuring citations, referral traffic, mentions, or conversions?
Is this genuinely new work, or renamed SEO plus PR?
entries.warningLabel: Often a rebranding exercise with some real technical hygiene hiding underneath.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Make your content clearer, more structured, more citeable, and easier for retrieval layers to find without pretending the entire discipline was born yesterday.

entries.body: The suspicion is usually justified. Much of GEO is indeed old work wearing new shoes. That does not make it fake. It makes it familiar.

entries.title: GLM
entries.devilDefinition: A Chinese model family that keeps appearing in agent benchmarks to remind the usual shortlist it is not a shortlist by divine right.
entries.plainDefinition: GLM is a model family associated with Z.AI, including flagship long-context and agent-oriented models such as GLM-5.1.
entries.whyExists: It matters because the model market is increasingly global, and GLM sits in the same practical conversation as Qwen, DeepSeek, and other non-US model families competing on coding, agents, context, and serving economics.
entries.misuse: It gets treated either as a single model or as geopolitical shorthand. The useful question is more boring: which GLM version, which deployment path, which workload, and which failure modes?
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means evaluating Z.AI's models for long-horizon coding, agent workflows, tool use, context length, and cost-performance against the better-known frontier suppliers.
entries.example: "The GLM trial was not ideological; it was an attempt to see whether the agent could work longer without turning the repo into theatre."
askNext: Which GLM version is being evaluated, and under which provider or hosting path?
Is the appeal coding, long-context work, agent reliability, price, or regional availability?
Do our own evals confirm the benchmark story?
entries.warningLabel: Model-family name. Not enough information by itself.

translations.label: vendor meaning
translations.text: Another serious non-US model family to test before pretending the market is tidy.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A Z.AI model line whose relevance depends on the exact version and workload.

entries.title: Google Antigravity
entries.devilDefinition: Google's answer to the question of whether vibe coding needed a launch name with more lift.
entries.plainDefinition: Google Antigravity is Google's agentic development surface for building and editing apps from prompts, with deeper project understanding and integrations for backend, secrets, and deployment-adjacent work.
entries.whyExists: Prompt-to-app demos stop being charming once users want authentication, databases, API keys, multiplayer state, and software that can survive contact with actual requirements. Antigravity exists to move that promise closer to full-stack work.
entries.misuse: It is pitched as though software difficulty now consists mainly of choosing a framework and describing the vibes with conviction. The agent can scaffold more of the stack. It cannot repeal complexity, ownership, or future maintenance.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means Google AI Studio getting more serious about app generation: Firebase wiring, tool use, framework selection, project memory, and a product surface where the agent is meant to feel like a teammate rather than a chat box with keyboard privileges.
entries.example: "Antigravity got the prototype airborne quickly, then left the team to rediscover that landing is its own discipline."
askNext: Is it generating maintainable software or just compressing the prototype stage?
How much of the workflow is genuinely agentic rather than pre-scripted scaffolding?
What happens once the app needs architecture rather than acceleration?

translations.text: A Google surface for turning prompts into real apps with more backend seriousness than a weekend demo.

translations.text: A stronger prompt-to-app workflow that still leaves the long-term engineering bill intact.

entries.title: Google DeepMind
entries.devilDefinition: The research empire formed when Google looked at one of the most storied AI labs in the world and decided it would also like the rest of the machine under the same roof.
entries.plainDefinition: Google DeepMind is Google's central AI research and frontier-model organisation. It combines the original DeepMind organisation with key Google AI research efforts, and sits behind research programmes and products including Gemini, AlphaFold, robotics work, safety research, and broader frontier-model development.
entries.whyExists: Because people still say "DeepMind" as if it were a free-floating research monastery. It is now a major unit inside Google, with both scientific prestige and the platform power that comes from sitting inside one of the largest technology companies on earth.
entries.misuse: By treating Google DeepMind as either a pure research institution or merely another product division. It is both more interesting and more awkward than that: a frontier lab nested inside a company that wants research breakthroughs to become products at scale.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means Google's long-horizon AI engine: the part of the company responsible for major research advances, frontier models, safety work, and many of the technical foundations that later surface through Gemini and other Google products.
entries.example: "The pitch started with Gemini, but the strategic argument was really about Google DeepMind and whether one company could still dominate both research legitimacy and product distribution."
askNext: Do they mean Google DeepMind the lab, Gemini the model family, or the Gemini app?
Is this a research claim, a product claim, or a capability claim?
Are we discussing scientific output, safety posture, or Google's platform strategy?
entries.warningLabel: Research lab, product engine, and corporate strategy node. Ask which layer the speaker means.

translations.label: research meaning
translations.text: The place people point when they want the conversation to sound more scientific than commercial.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Google's frontier AI organisation, where research prestige and product ambition share the same building.

entries.note: Google's own framing emphasises responsible AI for broad human benefit, but the practical significance of Google DeepMind is that it is both a frontier research lab and a core engine of product advantage across Google's ecosystem.
entries.body: The old mental model of DeepMind as a separate mythic lab is now mostly nostalgia. The current reality is institutional power with far shorter distance to product.

entries.title: Governance posture
entries.devilDefinition: The sober phrase people introduce after the demo has behaved just well enough for everyone to start worrying about what happens next.
entries.plainDefinition: Governance posture is a governance, control, or operating concept used to keep AI systems legible, bounded, and answerable once they move beyond isolated demonstrations.
entries.whyExists: It exists because the problems around Safe Superintelligence are not only model problems. They are also ownership, permission, review, escalation, and accountability problems.
entries.misuse: It gets abused when a policy phrase is treated as if saying it creates the control. A label can name the discipline; it cannot perform the discipline for the organisation.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means writing down who can act, who must approve, what gets logged, what can be reversed, and which failures become incidents rather than interesting anecdotes.
entries.example: "The governance posture discussion began as process paperwork and ended as the only reason the agent was allowed anywhere near production."
askNext: What exact meaning of Governance posture is being used here?
What changes operationally if this label is removed from the sentence?
Is this naming a mechanism, a vendor choice, a risk, or a wish?
entries.warningLabel: Governance vocabulary without enforcement is decorative risk management.

translations.label: operator meaning
translations.text: The part where permission, accountability, and review have to become real.

entries.body: Governance posture belongs here because AI systems become organisational systems as soon as people rely on them. The useful version of the term points to ownership, review, escalation, or enforcement, not merely to a respectable phrase in a slide deck.

entries.devilDefinition: The sober phrase people introduce after the demo has behaved just well enough for everyone to start worrying about what happens next.
entries.plainDefinition: Governance theatre is a governance, control, or operating concept used to keep AI systems legible, bounded, and answerable once they move beyond isolated demonstrations.
entries.whyExists: It exists because the problems around Safety Washing are not only model problems. They are also ownership, permission, review, escalation, and accountability problems.
entries.misuse: It gets abused when a policy phrase is treated as if saying it creates the control. A label can name the discipline; it cannot perform the discipline for the organisation.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means writing down who can act, who must approve, what gets logged, what can be reversed, and which failures become incidents rather than interesting anecdotes.
entries.example: "The governance theatre discussion began as process paperwork and ended as the only reason the agent was allowed anywhere near production."

entries.title: GPT
entries.devilDefinition: The acronym that began as a model architecture and graduated into a public synonym for whatever the chatbot did this morning.
entries.plainDefinition: GPT stands for Generative Pre-trained Transformer. In current usage it usually refers to transformer-based language models and, more specifically, to OpenAI's GPT-branded model families.
entries.whyExists: The term matters because it names a lineage of models built around pre-training and transformer architectures, but it also became a consumer-facing brand fragment attached to products, APIs, and model tiers.
entries.misuse: It gets used as a generic word for any AI assistant, any large language model, or any answer that arrived in a chat box. That makes comparisons sloppy and lets vendor branding smuggle itself into technical conversation.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means someone is talking about a language model, often an OpenAI model, without yet being precise about product surface, model version, context window, tool access, or pricing.
entries.example: "The procurement note said GPT. Engineering had to ask whether that meant ChatGPT, the OpenAI API, a particular model, or merely the reassuring sound of three capital letters."
askNext: Do we mean GPT as an architecture label, an OpenAI model family, or a product feature?
Which model version, context size, tool environment, and price tier are actually in scope?
Are we comparing a model to a model, or a full product experience to a bare API call?
entries.warningLabel: Often used as a brand-shaped synonym for the whole model category.

translations.label: meeting meaning
translations.text: The AI thing, said with enough confidence to postpone specificity.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A GPT-branded or GPT-like language model whose actual behaviour depends on the exact version and product wrapper.

entries.note: Mini and lightweight GPT-branded variants are usually better treated as model-sizing or pricing choices, not as a separate concept from GPT itself.
entries.body: The acronym is useful when it is precise. It becomes less useful the moment it is asked to cover architecture, brand, product, and folklore in one breath.

entries.title: GPT Image 2
entries.devilDefinition: OpenAI's current image model for people who would like better picture generation, better editing, and one more meeting about whether prompt writing now counts as art direction.
entries.plainDefinition: GPT Image 2 is OpenAI's current image-generation and image-editing model. It accepts text plus image inputs, returns image outputs, and can be used through the Image API or through the Responses API image generation tool.
entries.whyExists: Image systems are now expected to do more than spit out pretty approximations. They need stronger prompt adherence, better editing, higher-fidelity preservation of input images, and a smoother path from one-off generation to iterative product workflows.
entries.misuse: It gets described as though a new image model automatically solves taste, brand judgement, and production readiness. It does not. It mostly means OpenAI has improved the engine while leaving humans in charge of deciding whether the result deserves civilisation.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means OpenAI's latest image model for generation and edits, with flexible sizing and stronger image-input handling. The operational wrinkle is that the model itself sits behind two different patterns: direct image endpoints such as `v1/images/generations` and `v1/images/edits`, or a mainline model in the Responses API using the `image_generation` tool for multi-turn editing and prompt revision.
entries.example: "They said they had integrated OpenAI's new images v2, which turned out to mean GPT Image 2 plus a mainline model quietly rewriting prompts before the pixels arrived."
askNext: Are you calling the Image API directly, or using the Responses API image generation tool through a mainline model?
Do you need high-fidelity editing of an input image, or just one-shot generation?
Will the workflow break if someone asks for a transparent background, which GPT Image 2 does not currently support?
entries.warningLabel: Useful image model. Still not a substitute for taste or production judgement.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: OpenAI's newest image engine, plus a fresh round of people discovering that better generation does not remove the need for judgement.

entries.note: OpenAI's current docs describe GPT Image 2 as the state-of-the-art image generation model, with the snapshot alias `gpt-image-2-2026-04-21`. The docs also note that when you use image generation through the Responses API, a mainline model such as `gpt-5.4` can revise the prompt and decide whether to generate or edit.
entries.body: The important distinction is not merely new versus old. It is whether you are buying a standalone image endpoint, a conversational editing loop, or simply a shinier excuse to postpone hiring someone with visual judgement.

entries.title: GPU
entries.devilDefinition: The rectangle of silicon that turns abstract AI ambition into rack diagrams, queueing pain, and a line item large enough to sober up strategy decks.
entries.plainDefinition: A GPU is a highly parallel processor originally built for graphics work that is now widely used to train and serve AI models because it handles the underlying matrix computations efficiently.
entries.whyExists: Because modern AI workloads need a great deal of parallel numerical work, and GPUs became the practical engine for that before most organisations had learned how physical their software plans were.
entries.misuse: By using "GPU" to mean the whole AI stack, or by assuming that access to GPUs automatically solves latency, data quality, product design, or cost discipline. Hardware helps. It does not finish the argument.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means a constraint on training speed, inference cost, memory limits, model size, procurement timing, and whether your supposedly simple launch now depends on specialist infrastructure.
entries.example: "The roadmap called it an AI feature. Finance called it six more GPUs and several uncomfortable follow-up questions."
askNext: Is the bottleneck raw compute, memory, networking, or utilisation?
Do we need premium GPU capacity, or are we overbuying for the workload?
What part of the user value actually depends on this level of hardware?

translations.label: operator meaning
translations.text: The compute unit that decides whether your AI ambitions are shipping, waiting, or becoming procurement folklore.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A parallel processor that underwrites much of modern AI training and inference, along with a large share of the bill.

entries.note: The important distinction is not only which GPU you have, but whether the surrounding system can keep it fed, cooled, scheduled, and worth the money.
entries.body: A GPU is often described as the engine of AI. More usefully, it is the point where the conversation stops being metaphor and starts being capacity planning.

entries.title: GPU cloud
entries.devilDefinition: A useful technical label that becomes less useful the moment it is dropped into a meeting as proof that the hard part has already been solved.
entries.plainDefinition: GPU cloud names a technical pattern, component, capability, or implementation concern that affects how AI systems are built, connected, evaluated, or operated.
entries.whyExists: It exists because conversations around Neocloud need sharper language than "the AI bit" when the real issue is interfaces, constraints, failure modes, cost, or system behaviour.
entries.misuse: It gets abused when the label is treated as a solution rather than a place where engineering still has to make choices, measure trade-offs, and own the consequences.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means checking the boundary conditions: inputs, outputs, latency, permissions, reliability, cost, observability, and whether the chosen abstraction survives real use.
entries.example: "The team said gpu cloud as though it closed the matter; the postmortem revealed it was where the matter had been hiding."

entries.title: Grok
entries.devilDefinition: The assistant brand that insists it is not merely an AI product but a personality, a posture, and occasionally a geopolitical plot device.
entries.plainDefinition: Grok is xAI's assistant brand, covering both end-user chatbot experiences and model offerings exposed through xAI products and APIs. Depending on context, people may mean the Grok app, Grok on X, a specific Grok model, or the broader branded assistant experience.
entries.whyExists: Because the name "Grok" is used across product surfaces, model releases, consumer interfaces, and enterprise offerings. Without separating those layers, people end up comparing a brand impression to a technical system and calling it analysis.
entries.misuse: By treating Grok as one stable thing. It can mean the app, the model family, the social-media-integrated assistant, or an API-accessible model with specific tools and constraints. Those are related, but not interchangeable.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means xAI's consumer and developer-facing assistant stack: real-time search posture, distinct brand tone, and a growing family of models and tools wrapped in one loud identity.
entries.example: "They said Grok was better, which turned out to mean they liked the product's tone, real-time feel, and distribution inside familiar surfaces, not that they had run a clean model evaluation."
askNext: Do you mean the Grok product, a specific Grok model, or the experience inside X or grok.com?
Is the appeal about capability, personality, distribution, or real-time data access?
What part of the result came from the model versus the surrounding product scaffolding?

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A branded xAI assistant experience spanning multiple surfaces, models, tools, and moods.

entries.note: xAI's current product pages frame Grok as a truth-seeking companion with strong reasoning, coding, and multimodal capabilities. As of February 2, 2026, the surrounding corporate context also changed when xAI announced it had joined SpaceX, so the Grok brand now sits inside an even larger machinery of distribution and spectacle.

entries.whyExists: The term matters because discussions around Grok often need to separate the actual technical choice from the brand, distribution channel, contract, or ecosystem wrapped around it.
entries.misuse: It gets abused when the name is used as a substitute for specifying the exact product surface, model, API, entitlement, pricing constraint, or operational dependency under discussion.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means someone should ask what is being bought, integrated, routed through, depended on, or compared before the logo is allowed to stand in for the architecture.
entries.example: "Grok Enterprise came up in the review, which was useful only after the team clarified whether they meant the product, the platform, the model family, or the vendor relationship."

entries.title: Grounding
entries.devilDefinition: The effort to keep a model's answer attached to evidence rather than to whatever polished sentence was statistically easiest to generate.
entries.plainDefinition: Grounding refers to tying a model's output to external evidence, context, or real-world signals so that the answer is based on something more reliable than the model's prior patterns alone.
entries.whyExists: Language models can sound certain even when they are improvising. Grounding exists to improve faithfulness by connecting answers to documents, databases, observations, or tool results that bear on the question.
entries.misuse: It gets used as a prestige synonym for "we attached some documents". That is not enough. If the evidence is irrelevant, stale, contradictory, or ignored by the prompt design, the system is not meaningfully grounded.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means retrieval, citation, tool outputs, or structured data being incorporated in a way that materially constrains the answer. Good grounding is less about adding more text and more about adding the right evidence with clear priority.
entries.example: "The grounded version of the assistant did not just answer more cautiously; it cited the actual policy entry it used and admitted when retrieval came back thin."
askNext: What source is the answer grounded in?
How is the system encouraged or required to use that evidence faithfully?
What happens when the evidence is missing, conflicting, or low quality?
entries.note: In some research contexts, grounding can refer more broadly to connecting model symbols to the world. In product discussions, it usually means anchoring outputs to evidence or tool results.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: The answer has some evidentiary anchor outside the model's own priors, assuming the system uses that anchor properly.

entries.body: Grounding is one of the most practical words in the field when used well. It is also one of the easiest to fake with decorative citations.

entries.title: gstack
entries.devilDefinition: The proposition that one founder, one agent shell, and enough specialised slash commands can now impersonate a small product org with alarming speed.
entries.plainDefinition: gstack is Garry Tan's open-source workflow and skill stack for Claude Code and related agent hosts, packaged as a large set of opinionated roles and commands for planning, coding, review, QA, security, release, and retrospectives.
entries.whyExists: Because agent users do not just want a model. They want a repeatable operating system for product work: who reviews, who tests, who ships, and what sequence keeps the chaos looking intentional.
entries.misuse: By talking as if the stack itself is a substitute for product judgement, engineering taste, or organisational memory. A thick layer of specialist prompts can accelerate work. It cannot abolish the need to know what good looks like or who is accountable when it is not.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means turning an agent shell into a highly structured software-factory workflow, with preset roles for founder review, engineering review, design critique, browser QA, security audit, release management, and post-mortems.
entries.example: "The impressive part of gstack was not that it made one person look like a team. The impressive part was how much of the team's ceremony it compressed into commands."
askNext: Which parts are genuinely workflow discipline, and which parts are elaborate prompt theatre?
Does the stack improve outcomes on a real repo, or merely increase the amount of agentic motion?
Who still owns the decisions once the virtual org chart starts speaking?
entries.warningLabel: Useful when it enforces discipline. Less useful when it merely industrialises confidence.

translations.label: founder meaning
translations.text: A way to turn one competent builder into something that looks suspiciously like a staffed startup.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A strongly opinionated operating layer for AI-assisted software work, built out of reusable skills and process choreography.

entries.note: Garry Tan positions gstack as his personal answer to shipping with agent tooling: a public software-factory kit with role-specific commands, browser QA, review loops, and release mechanics. The interesting question is not whether it feels powerful. The interesting question is whether the structure holds up once the repo gets messy, the product gets real, and the novelty wears off.
entries.body: gstack is notable because it stops pretending the agent is just "helping with code". It formalises the wider ambition: compressing a startup's rituals into reusable command surfaces and seeing how much of the organisation can be simulated before management notices.

entries.title: Guardrails
entries.devilDefinition: The collection of fences, checks, filters, and sternly worded hopes designed to stop a model from wandering into places the product team cannot afford.
entries.plainDefinition: Guardrails are mechanisms used to constrain or monitor model behaviour, such as policy filters, tool permissions, schema validation, prompt rules, moderation systems, human approvals, or output checks.
entries.whyExists: Models are flexible in exactly the ways that make product owners nervous. Guardrails exist to narrow behaviour into something safe, compliant, or at least operationally manageable.
entries.misuse: The term gets invoked as though one moderation layer solves the entire problem. Real guardrails are layered, task-specific, and never total. A vague promise of guardrails often means someone has remembered risk late in the process and is trying to sound calmer than they feel.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means a stack of constraints before, during, and after the model call: restricted tools, validation logic, refusal policies, monitoring, and human review for higher-risk actions. The strongest guardrail is often reducing what the system is allowed to do in the first place.
entries.example: "The useful guardrail was not the disclaimer. It was the rule that stopped the assistant from sending anything externally without a human sign-off."
askNext: What specific failure mode is each guardrail intended to catch?
Which constraints happen before generation, during generation, and after generation?
What evidence shows the current guardrails work on real edge cases?
entries.warningLabel: A vague promise of guardrails is not a safety strategy.

translations.label: compliance meaning
translations.text: The controls we hope will keep the system inside acceptable boundaries.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A layered set of restrictions and checks, only as good as their coverage and enforcement.

entries.body: Guardrails work best when paired with scope discipline. If you keep widening the task, you are also widening the space the guardrails must cover.

entries.title: Hallucination
entries.devilDefinition: The moment a model says something false with the calm, polished confidence of a consultant who will never have to implement it.
entries.plainDefinition: In AI, a hallucination is an output that is incorrect, fabricated, unsupported, or inconsistent with the source material or task constraints. For language models, this can include made-up facts, invented citations, false summaries, or bogus tool arguments.
entries.whyExists: The term exists because generative models produce plausible sequences, not guaranteed truth. They can sound coherent while being wrong, especially when asked beyond their evidence, given poor context, or rewarded for fluent completion rather than faithful reporting.
entries.misuse: It gets used too loosely for every kind of model failure. Not every bad answer is a hallucination. Some outputs are simply outdated, misclassified, poorly retrieved, ambiguously prompted, or badly evaluated. Precision helps because the mitigation often depends on the failure mode.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means you need better task design, stronger grounding, narrower permissions, tighter output constraints, or human review. It also means you should stop treating eloquence as a proxy for truth.
entries.example: "The model produced a five-paragraph answer, three invented legal references, and the exact tone of someone who hoped nobody would check."
askNext: Was the output unsupported by evidence, or was the evidence retrieval itself broken?
What level of factual reliability does this workflow actually require?
What safeguards exist before the answer reaches a customer, regulator, or executive?

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: The model generated something that sounds acceptable but does not survive contact with evidence.

entries.body: Hallucinations are not shocking side effects of consciousness. They are ordinary failure modes of systems built to generate likely continuations. Treat them like engineering problems, not supernatural events.

entries.title: Hermes Agent
entries.devilDefinition: A self-hosted agent for people who thought OpenClaw was a good start and then asked whether the assistant could remember, delegate, schedule, browse, and live on a server.
entries.plainDefinition: Hermes Agent is an open-source, MIT-licensed autonomous server agent from Nous Research, positioned around persistent memory, auto-generated skills, scheduled automations, subagents, sandboxing, browser control, and multi-channel access.
entries.whyExists: It exists because the agent category is moving from chat windows toward always-on systems that can remember projects, run unattended jobs, delegate work, and operate across communication surfaces.
entries.misuse: It gets tempting to describe the whole package as a digital worker and stop there. The useful conversation is less romantic: what permissions it has, where it runs, which sandboxes apply, what it remembers, and how unattended work is audited.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means a self-hosted agent stack for users who want persistent automation, multi-channel interaction, browser and tool access, parallel subagents, and more control than a purely hosted chat product allows.
entries.example: "Hermes Agent was attractive because it lived on their server; the security team then asked the traditional follow-up question of what, exactly, had been invited to live there."
askNext: Which channels, tools, sandboxes, and memories are enabled?
What can it do unattended, and how are approvals, logs, and rollbacks handled?
Is self-hosting reducing risk, or merely moving the risk into a room you now own?
entries.warningLabel: Self-hosted does not mean self-governing.

translations.label: operator meaning
translations.text: An always-on agent stack that wants server privileges, memory, and a proper adult permission model.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: An OpenClaw-adjacent self-hosted agent with persistent memory, skills, subagents, automations, and browser control.

entries.body: Hermes is interesting because it makes the agent argument concrete. Once the assistant has memory, schedules, channels, sandboxes, and a browser, the debate leaves the keynote and enters operations.

entries.title: High-volume inference
entries.devilDefinition: A useful technical label that becomes less useful the moment it is dropped into a meeting as proof that the hard part has already been solved.
entries.plainDefinition: High-volume inference names a technical pattern, component, capability, or implementation concern that affects how AI systems are built, connected, evaluated, or operated.
entries.whyExists: It exists because conversations around Gemini 3.1 Flash-Lite need sharper language than "the AI bit" when the real issue is interfaces, constraints, failure modes, cost, or system behaviour.
entries.misuse: It gets abused when the label is treated as a solution rather than a place where engineering still has to make choices, measure trade-offs, and own the consequences.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means checking the boundary conditions: inputs, outputs, latency, permissions, reliability, cost, observability, and whether the chosen abstraction survives real use.
entries.example: "The team said high-volume inference as though it closed the matter; the postmortem revealed it was where the matter had been hiding."
askNext: What exact meaning of High-volume inference is being used here?
What changes operationally if this label is removed from the sentence?
Is this naming a mechanism, a vendor choice, a risk, or a wish?
entries.warningLabel: Technical vocabulary is not implementation evidence.

translations.text: A concrete implementation concern wearing a compact label.

entries.body: High-volume inference belongs in the dictionary because implementation detail changes the product conversation. The useful question is not whether the term sounds modern, but what constraint, cost, interface, or failure mode it names.

entries.title: Human evaluation
entries.devilDefinition: A phrase that sounds self-explanatory until someone asks it to survive a budget meeting, a product review, or a mildly hostile question.
entries.plainDefinition: Human evaluation names the nearby AI idea that shows up when LLM-as-a-Judge needs a more specific operational handle.
entries.whyExists: It exists because the main term alone is often too blunt. The surrounding conversation needs a way to name the neighbouring idea without pretending it is the same thing.
entries.misuse: It gets abused when people use the phrase as a mood, a shortcut, or a status signal instead of explaining the actual mechanism, incentive, or trade-off they mean.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means slowing the conversation down enough to ask whether the label points to a real system property, a commercial incentive, a user behaviour, or just a familiar rhetorical flourish.
entries.example: "Once human evaluation entered the discussion, the team finally had a name for the thing everyone had been gesturing at with less precision."
askNext: What exact meaning of Human evaluation is being used here?
What changes operationally if this label is removed from the sentence?
Is this naming a mechanism, a vendor choice, a risk, or a wish?

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A useful nearby term, provided someone explains the mechanism instead of using it as scenery.

entries.body: Human evaluation gets its own page because the surrounding discussion around LLM-as-a-Judge keeps needing this sharper distinction. Used well, it turns a vague AI-adjacent gesture into a claim someone can test, budget for, or push back on.

entries.title: Human in the Loop
entries.devilDefinition: The employee retained to absorb liability after management announces that the future requires fewer employees.
entries.plainDefinition: A workflow design in which a person reviews, approves, corrects, or intervenes in an automated system's output before or during action.
entries.whyExists: Because some decisions remain too costly, risky, or ambiguous to automate blindly. Human review is how organisations keep accountability attached to a named person rather than a press release.
entries.misuse: By invoking it as a magic safety phrase when the human is undertrained, overloaded, or expected to rubber-stamp machine output at impossible speed.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means queues, review fatigue, awkward staffing maths, and the discovery that "just have a human check it" is not a design but a labour budget.
entries.example: "They said the workflow was safe because it kept a human in the loop. The human, regrettably, was checking six hundred cases a day."
askNext: What authority does the reviewer actually have to stop or change the system's output?
How many cases can one person review before judgement degrades into gesture?
Is the human improving quality, or merely carrying blame?

translations.label: compliance meaning
translations.text: A reassuring phrase that sounds better than 'we still need staff to catch the machine's mistakes'.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A human checkpoint whose quality depends on staffing, authority, and time.

entries.body: If the loop is moving too quickly for thought, the human is decorative.
