entries.title: Human Taste
entries.devilDefinition: The increasingly expensive ability to notice that the machine has technically produced something and practically produced nothing.
entries.plainDefinition: Human judgement applied to selecting, editing, rejecting, sequencing, refining, or contextualising generated output.
entries.whyExists: Because automation scales production faster than it scales discernment.
entries.misuse: By mystifying it. Human taste is not just artistic instinct. It can be operational, commercial, ethical, strategic, or technical.
entries.practicalMeaning: A person knows when the answer is wrong, the image is tacky, the copy is off-brand, the feature is unnecessary, or the code should be rewritten rather than patched.
entries.example: "The workflow was mostly automated. The value was still in the person who knew which three outputs were worth keeping."
askNext: Whose taste?
Against what objective?
Is the person choosing better outputs, or merely choosing familiar ones?

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A person notices where automation has produced output without producing quality, relevance, or judgement.

entries.title: Humane
entries.devilDefinition: A beautifully marketed warning that futuristic hardware demos and durable product-market fit are not the same thing.
entries.plainDefinition: Humane was the startup behind the AI Pin, a wearable AI device that aimed to replace or reduce dependence on the smartphone. The product drew major attention, but Humane later wound down the consumer AI Pin service, and HP announced in February 2025 that it was acquiring key Humane AI capabilities, including the Cosmos platform, talent, and intellectual property.
entries.whyExists: Because Humane became shorthand for both the ambition and the fragility of early AI-device optimism.
entries.misuse: By treating Humane as proof that all AI hardware is doomed, rather than evidence that weak utility, awkward execution, pricing friction, and overhype can kill even well-funded products.
entries.practicalMeaning: A case study in what happens when storytelling outruns product usefulness. Also a reminder that hardware does not get to be vague for long. Consumers carry the thing, charge the thing, and notice when the thing does not justify itself.
entries.example: "The pitch was post-phone computing. The reality was a lesson in how little patience consumers have for expensive inconvenience."
askNext: Was the failure about AI itself, hardware design, pricing, latency, or practical utility?
Did Humane fail because the idea was wrong, or because the implementation was weak?
What, if anything, survives from the vision?
entries.warningLabel: Humane is now more useful as a cautionary reference than as a live consumer platform.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A beautifully framed product vision that did not survive the ordinary cruelty of consumer reality.

entries.note: Humane's consumer support materials said AI Pin cloud-connected features would stop working on February 28, 2025. HP separately announced the acquisition of key Humane capabilities on February 18, 2025.
entries.body: Humane deserves precision because it is too easy to turn into a morality play. It did not prove that AI devices are impossible. It proved that theatrical ambition does not excuse weak everyday utility.

entries.title: Industrial policy
entries.devilDefinition: The sober phrase people introduce after the demo has behaved just well enough for everyone to start worrying about what happens next.
entries.plainDefinition: Industrial policy is a governance, control, or operating concept used to keep AI systems legible, bounded, and answerable once they move beyond isolated demonstrations.
entries.whyExists: It exists because the problems around Sovereign AI are not only model problems. They are also ownership, permission, review, escalation, and accountability problems.
entries.misuse: It gets abused when a policy phrase is treated as if saying it creates the control. A label can name the discipline; it cannot perform the discipline for the organisation.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means writing down who can act, who must approve, what gets logged, what can be reversed, and which failures become incidents rather than interesting anecdotes.
entries.example: "The industrial policy discussion began as process paperwork and ended as the only reason the agent was allowed anywhere near production."

entries.title: Inference
entries.devilDefinition: The part where the model stops being a research paper and starts being an invoice.
entries.plainDefinition: Inference is the process of running a trained model on new input to produce an output. For language models, that usually means taking tokens in, computing the next-token probabilities, and generating a response token by token.
entries.whyExists: The term distinguishes using a model from building one. Training changes weights. Inference uses those weights to do work on fresh data. The distinction matters because the technical constraints, costs, and risks are very different.
entries.misuse: People often use "inference" as a grand-sounding synonym for "the AI bit". That hides the practical questions. Inference is not magic reasoning dust. It is a serving workload with latency, throughput, reliability, and cost characteristics.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means API calls, GPU time, queueing, prompt assembly, caching, streaming, rate limits, and a finance person eventually asking why the bot became expensive after launch. If your AI feature is live, inference is the meter running.
entries.example: "Training happened once in the background; inference happens every time a customer opens the feature and expects an answer in under two seconds."

Is the bottleneck latency, throughput, quality, or context assembly?
Can some of this workload be cached, batched, or offloaded to a smaller model?

translations.label: founder meaning
translations.text: The part customers can actually feel.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A performance budget attached to compute, prompts, and product expectations.

entries.body: If someone says a feature is "AI-powered", inference is usually the operational question hiding underneath. Not whether the model is clever in principle, but whether the system can answer quickly, cheaply, and often enough to survive contact with users.

entries.title: Inference Arbitrage
entries.devilDefinition: Buying intelligence wholesale from one provider, retailing it through a cleaner interface, and hoping the margin survives long enough to become a strategy.
entries.plainDefinition: Inference arbitrage is the practice of profiting from differences in model price, latency, quality, or access by routing demand across providers and packaging the result as a product or service.
entries.whyExists: Model markets are uneven. Providers price differently, regions behave differently, and many users would rather pay one intermediary than personally compare latency tables like a day trader with a prompt addiction.
entries.misuse: The term is stretched when any wrapper, gateway, or comparison site is described as though routing alone were a durable moat. Arbitrage can be real. It is also famously allergic to permanence.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means mixing models behind the scenes, swapping suppliers when pricing shifts, and capturing some spread through UX, bundling, reliability, or customer ignorance. The better businesses add real workflow value. The thinner ones are mostly tariffs with branding.
entries.example: "Their moat turned out to be inference arbitrage with a charming UI and a very aggressive newsletter."
askNext: What part of the margin comes from routing skill, and what part from customer convenience?
How quickly does the advantage disappear if providers cut prices or copy the feature?
Is there product substance here, or mainly a spread between wholesale tokens and retail confidence?

translations.label: operator meaning
translations.text: Capture value from differences between model suppliers instead of betting on a single one.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Someone else is shopping the model market so the customer can pretend not to notice.

entries.body: Arbitrage is not shameful. It is merely less mythic than many decks would prefer.

entries.title: Inference Budget
entries.devilDefinition: The line item where intelligence becomes less abstract and more payable within thirty days.
entries.plainDefinition: An inference budget is the planned or enforced limit on the cost, latency, tokens, model calls, compute, or provider spend used to run AI features in production.
entries.whyExists: Model calls have variable cost and performance characteristics. Without a budget, a successful feature can become a financial incident, and an agent can discover new forms of curiosity at the company's expense.
entries.misuse: It gets reduced to token counting. Tokens matter, but so do model choice, retries, tool calls, reasoning effort, cache hits, latency targets, and the number of times a workflow politely tries again.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means per-user limits, per-task ceilings, model routing policies, cache strategy, timeout rules, alerting, and deciding which requests deserve the expensive model before the expensive model decides for itself.
entries.example: "The inference budget survived launch only after the agent was stopped from spending premium reasoning on password reset emails."
askNext: What is the maximum acceptable cost for this user action?
Which requests can use a cheaper model, cache, or shorter reasoning path?
Are retries and tool calls counted in the same budget as the visible model response?

translations.label: finance meaning
translations.text: Set a ceiling on how much intelligence a workflow is allowed to buy.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A practical argument between quality, speed, and the bill.

entries.body: Inference budgets are not anti-intelligence. They are the moment the system admits that cleverness has a marginal cost.

entries.title: Inference Provider
entries.devilDefinition: The company renting you access to intelligence by the token, with latency graphs where the soul used to be.
entries.plainDefinition: An inference provider runs and serves AI models through APIs or managed infrastructure so customers can send inputs and receive outputs without operating the full model stack themselves.
entries.whyExists: Because reliable model serving requires GPUs, batching, scaling, observability, routing, and a tolerance for operational pain that many product teams would rather outsource.
entries.misuse: By blurring the line between building a model and hosting one, or by describing a rented endpoint as proprietary intelligence simply because the invoice arrives under a different logo.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice the choice of inference provider shapes latency, price, regional availability, data handling, model access, and how much of your application's reliability now depends on somebody else's serving layer.
entries.example: "They said they had their own AI platform, which turned out to mean an inference provider, a neat dashboard, and a heroic willingness to rename dependency as strategy."
askNext: Who actually trains the model, and who merely serves it?
What happens to cost and performance when traffic scales?
Which data-handling guarantees survive the extra layer?

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Someone else is handling the GPU headaches, and now they sit in your critical path.

entries.body: Inference providers matter because very few teams want to run model serving properly, and a surprising number still want to sound as if they do.

entries.title: Inference Stack
entries.devilDefinition: The collection of expensive plumbing discovered immediately after somebody says, "It's just one API call."
entries.plainDefinition: An inference stack is the set of systems used to run model requests in production, including providers, routing, caching, prompt assembly, guardrails, observability, evaluation, and cost control.
entries.whyExists: Because shipping AI features requires more than model access. Real applications need reliability, latency control, policy enforcement, failover, measurement, and a defensible explanation for the monthly bill.
entries.misuse: By calling every thin wrapper a stack. A real inference stack handles production constraints. A dropdown of model names and a vibes-based retry loop do not amount to infrastructure.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means SDKs, proxies, model routers, rate limits, prompt templates, redaction, logging, eval pipelines, cache policies, billing dashboards, and repeated arguments over whether quality improved enough to justify the spend.
entries.example: "The inference stack matured rapidly once finance started reading the token invoices with feeling."
askNext: Which parts of the stack protect reliability, and which merely create another place for failure to hide?
How are routing, caching, and fallback policies affecting quality as well as cost?
Who can explain the per-request economics without opening six dashboards and a prayer?

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A layered serving system where cost, latency, safety, and uptime argue with one another on every request.

entries.title: Integration governance
entries.devilDefinition: The sober phrase people introduce after the demo has behaved just well enough for everyone to start worrying about what happens next.
entries.plainDefinition: Integration governance is a governance, control, or operating concept used to keep AI systems legible, bounded, and answerable once they move beyond isolated demonstrations.
entries.whyExists: It exists because the problems around Tool Registry are not only model problems. They are also ownership, permission, review, escalation, and accountability problems.
entries.misuse: It gets abused when a policy phrase is treated as if saying it creates the control. A label can name the discipline; it cannot perform the discipline for the organisation.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means writing down who can act, who must approve, what gets logged, what can be reversed, and which failures become incidents rather than interesting anecdotes.
entries.example: "The integration governance discussion began as process paperwork and ended as the only reason the agent was allowed anywhere near production."

entries.title: Jailbreak
entries.devilDefinition: The art of persuading a safety system that the rules were meant for someone else.
entries.plainDefinition: A jailbreak is a prompt or interaction pattern intended to bypass a model's safety rules, system instructions, or refusal policies and elicit behaviour it is supposed to restrict.
entries.whyExists: It exists because instruction-following systems are probabilistic, layered defences are imperfect, and many users are curious, mischievous, adversarial, or simply unwilling to accept "no" from a machine.
entries.misuse: The term gets stretched to cover any successful prompt tweak. More precisely, a jailbreak is about overriding the intended boundary, not merely asking the question more clearly.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means red-team exercises, defence updates, moderation tuning, prompt hardening, and a permanent reminder that a polite interface does not imply a secure one, especially once tools and external systems are attached.
entries.example: "The jailbreak was funny right up to the moment someone remembered the model also had access to tools."
askNext: What boundary was bypassed: policy, system prompt, tool permission, or all three?

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Someone found a conversational way around safeguards that were always less absolute than the marketing implied.

entries.note: Not every refusal workaround is a serious security event. The risk changes sharply when the model can call tools, access data, or operate in regulated workflows.
entries.body: The most interesting part is rarely the clever prompt. It is the quiet assumption that the model should have been tougher in the first place.

entries.title: Jensen Huang
entries.devilDefinition: The man who sold shovels to the gold rush, then noticed that the shovels were now the kingdom.
entries.plainDefinition: Jensen Huang is the founder and CEO of NVIDIA, the company most closely associated with the compute layer underneath the current AI boom.
entries.whyExists: Because AI eventually becomes a hardware conversation whether the software people like it or not. Jensen's name now stands for compute supply, pricing power, chip strategy, and the awkward fact that ambitious models run on physical constraints, not vibes.
entries.misuse: By speaking as if NVIDIA is AI itself rather than a dominant infrastructure layer beneath much of it. That overstates the company and understates the rest of the stack, but it does capture where the leverage currently sits.
entries.practicalMeaning: When people say "Jensen", they usually mean GPU access, training economics, inference cost, procurement pain, or the broader truth that model ambition can be throttled by silicon and supply chains.
entries.example: "The startup imagined it was building software until it met Jensen and discovered that its roadmap was now partially a conversation about lead times."

Is NVIDIA enabling the market, pricing the market, or quietly defining it?

entries.title: Knowledge Cutoff
entries.devilDefinition: The calendar line after which the model becomes a gifted historical novelist.
entries.plainDefinition: A knowledge cutoff is the latest point in time reflected in a model's internal knowledge without fresh retrieval or live access to newer information.
entries.whyExists: Models are built from data gathered during a finite period. However broad that period was, it still ends somewhere, which means the model's default knowledge eventually becomes stale.
entries.misuse: By treating the cutoff as either a precise magical boundary or as something that no longer matters because the interface looks current. A model can sound up to date long after its internal map stopped being refreshed.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means any question about recent events, changing laws, product releases, prices, or people who change jobs needs fresh sources instead of trusting the model's memory and posture.
entries.example: "The answer sounded current until someone checked the date. It was confidence wearing a knowledge cutoff."
askNext: What information in this workflow changes often enough to outrun the model's built-in knowledge?
Is the system using fresh retrieval, or merely sounding well informed about the past?
How would we notice if the answer stopped at last year's world?

translations.label: user meaning
translations.text: The moment where the system's memory stops and its style keeps going.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A built-in date limit on what the model can know without outside help.

entries.body: The problem is not that models have a past. The problem is that they rarely announce it at the exact moment you most need them to.

entries.title: Latency
entries.devilDefinition: The awkward silence between "look how magical this is" and the user deciding they no longer care.
entries.plainDefinition: Latency is the elapsed time between making a request to a system and receiving a usable response.
entries.whyExists: AI systems do not merely need to be correct enough. They need to respond quickly enough for the surrounding workflow, interface, and patience of the person waiting.
entries.misuse: By treating latency as a minor engineering detail that can be apologised away with a loading spinner. People are far more forgiving of imperfection than of delay that breaks the rhythm of work.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means the difference between a feature that feels helpful and one that feels like a committee meeting. Model size, retrieval, tool calls, routing, and output length all conspire to make waiting expensive.
entries.example: "The model was accurate, elegant, and two seconds too slow, which is another way of saying it was losing."
askNext: What is the maximum delay this workflow can tolerate before people route around it?
Which part of the pipeline is slow: model time, retrieval, tools, or post-processing?
Are we paying for higher capability when the real problem is response speed?

entries.title: Liar's Dividend
entries.devilDefinition: The social profit earned when fake media becomes plausible enough that real evidence can be waved away as synthetic inconvenience.
entries.plainDefinition: The liar's dividend is the benefit people gain when the existence of convincing AI-generated or manipulated media lets them deny authentic evidence as fake.
entries.whyExists: AI has lowered the cost of producing persuasive synthetic audio, images, and video. That does not only create fake evidence. It also weakens confidence in real evidence by giving bad actors a ready-made excuse.
entries.misuse: The phrase can be used too theatrically, as though trust has already collapsed beyond recovery. Verification, provenance, and forensic methods still matter. The problem is not total epistemic ruin. It is a higher and more expensive burden of proof.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means journalists, courts, platforms, and the public need stronger provenance checks while opportunists claim every inconvenient recording is probably AI. The immediate damage is often delay, confusion, and public doubt rather than perfect deception.
entries.example: "The clip was real, but the liar's dividend arrived on schedule and turned basic verification into a partisan hobby."
askNext: What provenance or verification evidence accompanies this media?
Who benefits from uncertainty around its authenticity?
Are we discussing actual forensic doubts, or opportunistic denial dressed as prudence?

translations.label: policy meaning
translations.text: Synthetic media makes it easier to dispute authentic evidence.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Once fakes are cheap, denial gets cheaper too.

entries.body: The liar's dividend is one of the more serious AI terms because it describes a failure mode with no product demo. It arrives as a shrug, a doubt, and a very convenient excuse.
entries.searchText:
Liar's Dividend liar dividend synthetic deniability deepfake alibi Cultural terms Safety and evaluation deepfakes trust media verification The social profit earned when fake media becomes plausible enough that real evidence can be waved away as synthetic inconvenience.
 The liar's dividend is the benefit people gain when the existence of convincing AI-generated or manipulated media lets them deny authentic evidence as fake.
 AI has lowered the cost of producing persuasive synthetic audio, images, and video. That does not only create fake evidence. It also weakens confidence in real evidence by giving bad actors a ready-made excuse.
 The phrase can be used too theatrically, as though trust has already collapsed beyond recovery. Verification, provenance, and forensic methods still matter. The problem is not total epistemic ruin. It is a higher and more expensive burden of proof.
 In practice it usually means journalists, courts, platforms, and the public need stronger provenance checks while opportunists claim every inconvenient recording is probably AI. The immediate damage is often delay, confusion, and public doubt rather than perfect deception.
 "The clip was real, but the liar's dividend arrived on schedule and turned basic verification into a partisan hobby."

entries.title: Llama
entries.devilDefinition: The model family that made "open" a competitive move, a developer habit, and a licensing argument in one convenient package.
entries.plainDefinition: Llama is Meta's family of foundation models, released in multiple versions and sizes for research, development, and commercial use under terms that made the models widely deployable without making the conversation any less political.
entries.whyExists: Developers wanted strong deployable models outside closed APIs, and Meta wanted broad ecosystem influence, research visibility, and distribution through everyone else's hardware budget.
entries.misuse: It gets treated as a single model, as synonymous with open source, or as proof that downloading weights is the same thing as having deployment solved. "We use Llama" is usually the beginning of the sentence, not the useful part.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means evaluating a specific Llama variant for self-hosting, fine-tuning, or cost control, with all the usual adult questions about licences, throughput, memory, moderation, and whether the chosen size is actually fit for purpose.
entries.example: "They said the stack ran on Llama, which turned out to mean one particular checkpoint, two compatibility caveats, and a spirited debate about memory."
askNext: Which Llama version, size, and licence terms are we talking about?
What problem does self-hosting solve here: cost, privacy, latency, control, or ideology?
Do we have the infrastructure and evaluation discipline to run it well?
entries.warningLabel: Model family, not a monolithic capability.

translations.label: ecosystem meaning
translations.text: A major open-model family that helped turn self-hosting from hobbyism into a default strategic option.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A family of deployable Meta models whose usefulness depends on the exact checkpoint and the operator's competence.

entries.note: Llama's significance is as much strategic as technical. It changed what many teams assumed was possible outside closed-provider APIs.
entries.body: Some products use Llama because they believe in openness. Many more use it because a downloadable model is easier to budget than a permanently rising surprise.

entries.title: LLM
entries.devilDefinition: A statistical text engine promoted from autocomplete to corporate metaphysics.
entries.plainDefinition: LLM stands for large language model: a model trained to predict and generate language, often across many tasks involving text, code, and increasingly tools and multimodal inputs.
entries.whyExists: The field needed a short label for the class of large generative models that sit underneath much of the current AI boom. The acronym is easier to say than the architecture, which is part of its success.
entries.misuse: By using LLM as a synonym for AI, intelligence, or product value. An LLM can be the engine inside a useful system, but the acronym by itself explains very little about whether the result is accurate, safe, or worth paying for.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means a flexible language-and-code engine that can draft, classify, summarise, translate, search, and bluff, depending on how it is prompted, constrained, and surrounded by everything else.
entries.example: "They said the product was powered by an LLM as though naming the engine answered the question of whether the vehicle was heading anywhere sensible."
askNext: What specific model behaviour matters here beyond the presence of an LLM?
What surrounding retrieval, tooling, or controls make the system useful?
Is the acronym explaining the product, or merely decorating it?

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A broad class of models for generating and manipulating language, not a guarantee of product merit.

entries.note: The acronym is useful as shorthand. It becomes perfume when treated as an explanation rather than a category.

entries.title: LLM application security
entries.devilDefinition: The sober phrase people introduce after the demo has behaved just well enough for everyone to start worrying about what happens next.
entries.plainDefinition: LLM application security is a governance, control, or operating concept used to keep AI systems legible, bounded, and answerable once they move beyond isolated demonstrations.
entries.whyExists: It exists because the problems around Prompt Firewall are not only model problems. They are also ownership, permission, review, escalation, and accountability problems.
entries.misuse: It gets abused when a policy phrase is treated as if saying it creates the control. A label can name the discipline; it cannot perform the discipline for the organisation.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means writing down who can act, who must approve, what gets logged, what can be reversed, and which failures become incidents rather than interesting anecdotes.
entries.example: "The llm application security discussion began as process paperwork and ended as the only reason the agent was allowed anywhere near production."

entries.title: LLM-as-a-Judge
entries.devilDefinition: The practice of asking one stochastic witness whether another stochastic witness deserves a trophy.
entries.plainDefinition: LLM-as-a-judge is an evaluation pattern in which one language model scores, ranks, or compares outputs from another model against a rubric, preference signal, or pairwise choice.
entries.whyExists: Because human evaluation is expensive, slow, and inconveniently unwilling to scale at the pace product teams prefer.
entries.misuse: By treating the judge as neutral, objective, or magically aligned with user value. A model grader can inherit stylistic bias, provider bias, verbosity bias, and a remarkable affection for whatever sounds polished.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it is useful for triage, regression testing, and broad ranking, provided the rubric is clear and the results are anchored to human review on the cases that actually matter.
entries.example: "The model won the internal evals until an actual customer arrived and demonstrated that the judge had confused confidence with usefulness."
askNext: What rubric is the judge following, and who wrote it?
How often do human reviewers disagree with the model's preferences?
Are we using the judge for cheap sorting or pretending it is ground truth?
entries.warningLabel: Helpful for ranking. Dangerous when mistaken for reality.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A scalable but imperfect grading shortcut that still needs humans to calibrate the definition of good.

entries.body: It is a sensible tool if used with modesty. The trouble begins when teams treat automated judgement as the cheaper cousin of judgement itself.

entries.title: Lovable
entries.devilDefinition: A chat-driven builder for turning "I have an idea" into a working app before anyone asks who will inherit the code.
entries.plainDefinition: Lovable is a product for generating and iterating on web applications from natural-language instructions, with modes for planning, editing code, and increasingly agent-like execution across a project.
entries.whyExists: It exists because many people want software outcomes without local setup, framework literacy, or the traditional rite of discovering three build errors before lunch. Products like this collapse prototyping, editing, and deployment into one highly persuasive loop.
entries.misuse: It gets sold as though describing software and owning software are the same discipline. The tool can absolutely shorten the road to a useful prototype. It cannot repeal the maintenance burden or make architecture an optional hobby.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice Lovable usually means faster internal tools, MVPs, marketing sites, and founder demos, plus a fresh opportunity to confuse visible progress with durable engineering.
entries.example: "Lovable got them from idea to live demo in a weekend, which was excellent right up to the moment the product had to become a system."
askNext: Is this a prototype, an internal tool, or something expected to survive real users?
Who understands the resulting code once the initial chat session is over?
Did the tool remove engineering work, or merely defer it to a less festive day?

translations.label: founder meaning
translations.text: A short route from idea to demo, ideally before caution schedules a meeting.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A product that makes app creation more accessible while leaving long-term code ownership very much alive.

entries.body: The category is real. So is the temptation to treat software as finished the moment it becomes presentable.

entries.title: Mac mini
entries.devilDefinition: The little silver box people buy when they want "serious AI infrastructure" without admitting they mostly mean "a neat machine that sits quietly on a desk."
entries.plainDefinition: Mac mini is Apple's compact desktop computer line. In AI conversations, it usually comes up as a relatively tidy, efficient machine for local inference, lightweight automation, development, and always-on orchestration workloads rather than frontier-scale model training. The current M4 and M4 Pro generation is especially relevant because Apple positions it for on-device AI workloads, and the M4 Pro configuration pushes unified memory and I/O far enough to make it a credible local workhorse within limits.
entries.whyExists: Because not every AI workload needs a server rack, and not every ambitious workflow deserves one either.
entries.misuse: By talking about it as if it were either a toy or a supercomputer. It is neither. It is a practical desktop that can be surprisingly capable within its lane and very ordinary outside it.
entries.practicalMeaning: A good box for coding, running local tools, hosting lightweight agents, testing smaller models, or acting as a stable automation node. It is especially attractive when low noise, power efficiency, and desk-friendly deployment matter more than maximum accelerator firepower.
entries.example: "He did not need a DGX. He needed a Mac mini, some discipline, and a better sense of what counted as production."
askNext: Is the workload inference, orchestration, development, or training?
Will it be running one assistant or many workflows?
Is the constraint compute, RAM, storage, or simply operational clarity?

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A practical always-on box for local AI work when you want something more serious than a laptop and much less dramatic than data-centre cosplay.

entries.note: Apple's 2024 Mac mini line pairs M4 or M4 Pro with unified memory, and Apple specifically highlighted on-device AI speed, Thunderbolt 5 on M4 Pro, and higher display and bandwidth ceilings. None of that turns it into a training cluster. It does make it a respectable local node.
entries.body: The Mac mini has become a kind of anti-theatrical AI machine. It is small, quiet, and far more useful when treated as infrastructure than when narrated as destiny.

entries.title: Managed inference
entries.devilDefinition: A useful technical label that becomes less useful the moment it is dropped into a meeting as proof that the hard part has already been solved.
entries.plainDefinition: Managed inference names a technical pattern, component, capability, or implementation concern that affects how AI systems are built, connected, evaluated, or operated.
entries.whyExists: It exists because conversations around Model Garden need sharper language than "the AI bit" when the real issue is interfaces, constraints, failure modes, cost, or system behaviour.
entries.misuse: It gets abused when the label is treated as a solution rather than a place where engineering still has to make choices, measure trade-offs, and own the consequences.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means checking the boundary conditions: inputs, outputs, latency, permissions, reliability, cost, observability, and whether the chosen abstraction survives real use.
entries.example: "The team said managed inference as though it closed the matter; the postmortem revealed it was where the matter had been hiding."

entries.title: Manifest
entries.devilDefinition: A model router for agents whose first autonomous act was apparently choosing the most expensive model available.
entries.plainDefinition: Manifest is an open-source LLM routing layer for personal AI agents, designed to sit behind OpenAI-compatible endpoints and route requests to cheaper or more suitable models based on task complexity and policy.
entries.whyExists: It exists because many agents send every request to the same large model, even when the task is tiny, repetitive, or cheap enough to deserve a less theatrical engine.
entries.misuse: It gets mistaken for model intelligence rather than routing policy. A router can reduce waste and add fallback behaviour, but it cannot make a weak task definition or poor evaluation strategy suddenly competent.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means model-tiering, fallback chains, budget controls, provider abstraction, local or cloud routing, and a sharper view of what each agent request actually costs.
entries.example: "Manifest saved money by routing simple agent chores away from the heroic model, which was a cruel blow to the heroic invoice."
askNext: What signals decide whether a task is simple, complex, or reasoning-heavy?
How are budgets, fallbacks, and provider failures handled?
Does cheaper routing preserve quality on the workloads that actually matter?

translations.label: operator meaning
translations.text: Stop paying frontier-model prices for every agent cough.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A routing and budget layer that makes agent spending less mystical and more accountable.

entries.body: The funny thing about autonomy is how quickly it needs accounting. Manifest is what happens when agent ambition meets a spreadsheet that can still feel pain.

entries.title: Manual approval
entries.devilDefinition: The sober phrase people introduce after the demo has behaved just well enough for everyone to start worrying about what happens next.
entries.plainDefinition: Manual approval is a governance, control, or operating concept used to keep AI systems legible, bounded, and answerable once they move beyond isolated demonstrations.
entries.whyExists: It exists because the problems around Human in the Loop and Rubber-Stamp Review are not only model problems. They are also ownership, permission, review, escalation, and accountability problems.
entries.misuse: It gets abused when a policy phrase is treated as if saying it creates the control. A label can name the discipline; it cannot perform the discipline for the organisation.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means writing down who can act, who must approve, what gets logged, what can be reversed, and which failures become incidents rather than interesting anecdotes.
entries.example: "The manual approval discussion began as process paperwork and ended as the only reason the agent was allowed anywhere near production."
askNext: What exact meaning of Manual approval is being used here?
What changes operationally if this label is removed from the sentence?
Is this naming a mechanism, a vendor choice, a risk, or a wish?
entries.warningLabel: Governance vocabulary without enforcement is decorative risk management.

translations.label: operator meaning
translations.text: The part where permission, accountability, and review have to become real.

entries.body: Manual approval belongs here because AI systems become organisational systems as soon as people rely on them. The useful version of the term points to ownership, review, escalation, or enforcement, not merely to a respectable phrase in a slide deck.

entries.title: Manus
entries.devilDefinition: An invite-only demonstration that autonomy feels much more plausible when scarcity does some of the marketing.
entries.plainDefinition: Manus is an agent product associated with multi-step task execution across browsing, research, and software tools, marketed as a system that can carry out longer chains of work than a standard chatbot.
entries.whyExists: The appeal is obvious: people do not merely want better answers, they want finished tasks. A product that promises to browse, compare, draft, click, and return with the work already done sits directly on that desire.
entries.misuse: It gets treated as evidence that general-purpose autonomous agents are effectively solved. In reality, a polished demo reel, a waitlist, and a few successful runs do not abolish brittleness, supervision, or task selection.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means a carefully packaged long-running agent workflow with browser actions, tool calls, and enough narrative polish to make ordinary automation feel like a historical turning point.
entries.example: "Manus looked like a digital chief of staff until the workflow left the happy path and became a queue with ambition."

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A credible but constrained automation product whose strongest feature may be how vividly it dramatises the future.

entries.note: The term often refers as much to the reaction around the product as to the product itself.

entries.title: MCP
entries.devilDefinition: A way of making tools and resources speak a common dialect so the assistant stops needing a bespoke adapter for every cupboard in the house.
entries.plainDefinition: MCP stands for Model Context Protocol. It is a protocol for exposing tools, resources, and related capabilities to AI clients in a standardised way, so assistants can discover and use external systems without each integration being entirely custom.
entries.whyExists: AI applications increasingly need access to files, APIs, documentation, databases, prompts, and tools. Without a shared protocol, every client-server pairing becomes its own integration project. MCP exists to reduce that fragmentation.
entries.misuse: It gets spoken about as though a protocol itself makes systems intelligent, secure, or interoperable by magic. It does not. Standardisation helps, but permissions, trust boundaries, authentication, and product design still matter more than the acronym.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means exposing capabilities through a consistent interface so a client can list resources, call tools, and work with external context more systematically. The real value is reducing bespoke glue and making integrations more legible.
entries.example: "MCP did not solve the assistant's judgement problem. It solved the quieter and more useful problem of not writing one-off connectors forever."
askNext: Which tools and resources are exposed through the protocol?
How are authentication, permissions, and auditability handled?
What part of the integration problem is standardised, and what still remains application-specific?

translations.label: engineer meaning
translations.text: A standard way to expose capabilities so clients do not need a fresh bespoke contract every time.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Less adapter sprawl, provided someone still does the hard work on security and product boundaries.

entries.body: Protocols are boring in the most productive possible sense. They do not remove the need for judgement. They remove some of the repetitive integration labour that distracts from the judgement problem.

entries.title: Memory Policy
entries.devilDefinition: The quiet constitution deciding what the system remembers, for how long, and which future mistake inherits the privilege.
entries.plainDefinition: Memory policy is the set of rules governing what an AI system stores from interactions, how long it keeps that information, when it retrieves it, and when it must forget or redact it.
entries.whyExists: It exists because continuity is useful and indiscriminate memory is dangerous. Long-running systems can become more helpful by remembering preferences, facts, and prior decisions, but they can also become more wrong, more invasive, and more difficult to debug.
entries.misuse: The term gets reduced to "we have memory now", as if any retained context were inherently intelligent. It also gets used to dress up ordinary logging or vector storage without answering the harder questions about consent, correction, deletion, and sensitive data.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means retention windows, summarisation rules, provenance, retrieval thresholds, redaction filters, user controls, and clear logic for when stale memory should be ignored rather than honoured out of misplaced loyalty.
entries.example: "The assistant felt personal right up until its memory policy allowed one outdated preference to haunt every recommendation for the next three months."
askNext: What information qualifies for memory, and what must never be stored?
How are stale or incorrect memories corrected, deleted, or superseded?
When memory is retrieved, how does the system distinguish relevance from sentimental hoarding?
entries.warningLabel: Useful until it starts remembering the wrong thing with perfect confidence.

translations.label: product meaning
translations.text: The rules that make continuity feel helpful instead of mildly creepy.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A set of retention, retrieval, and deletion decisions nobody can safely leave to improvisation.

entries.body: Memory policy matters because forgetting is part of quality control. A system that never forgets is often just a system that never learned restraint.

entries.title: Microsoft AI
entries.devilDefinition: The part of Microsoft tasked with making "Copilot everywhere" sound less like a slogan and more like the natural order of software.
entries.plainDefinition: Microsoft AI is the organisation Microsoft formed in 2024 to advance Copilot and its consumer AI products and research. It is distinct from Microsoft as a whole, and also distinct from Azure's infrastructure role or OpenAI's position as a model partner.
entries.whyExists: Because Microsoft needed a named unit for the part of its AI strategy focused on product experience, consumer interfaces, and turning model access into habitual software use.
entries.misuse: By using "Microsoft AI" to mean Microsoft generally, Azure specifically, or OpenAI by implication. The company has several overlapping AI layers; this term refers to the product-and-research organisation built around Copilot and related consumer experiences.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means the organisational machinery behind Microsoft's bid to make AI a persistent interface layer across its consumer products, with Copilot as the flagship expression of that strategy.
entries.example: "They said Microsoft AI as if that explained the architecture. In reality it explained the product thesis: put a companion layer in front of as much software as possible."
askNext: Do they mean Microsoft AI, Azure AI infrastructure, or Microsoft's OpenAI partnership?
Is the topic model capability, product interface, or distribution?
How much of the value comes from Copilot branding versus actual workflow integration?
entries.warningLabel: Organisation name. Not a synonym for Azure, OpenAI, or every AI feature Microsoft ships.

translations.label: enterprise meaning
translations.text: The part of Microsoft trying to turn AI into a default interface, not merely an optional feature.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Microsoft's dedicated organisation for turning model capability into sticky products people actually use.

entries.note: Microsoft announced the organisation on March 19, 2024, framing it around Copilot and other consumer AI products and research. That matters because "Microsoft AI" is a specific organisational bet, not a vague brand halo.
entries.body: If OpenAI is the supplier in Microsoft's story, Microsoft AI is the packaging department with imperial ambitions.

entries.title: Microsoft Copilot
entries.devilDefinition: Software sold as an assistant and deployed as a new layer of management: supervising the machine that was supposed to save you time.
entries.plainDefinition: Microsoft Copilot is Microsoft's umbrella brand for AI assistants and generative features across workplace and consumer products, including Microsoft 365, Windows, developer tools, and business software.
entries.whyExists: Because Microsoft wants AI to become the default interface layer across the software people already pay for, turning model access into subscription expansion and habitual workflow presence.
entries.misuse: By using "Copilot" as if it named one coherent product or guaranteed productivity. In reality it is a family resemblance: similar branding spread across many surfaces with uneven usefulness.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means an AI sidebar, a drafting assistant, a meeting-summary machine, a permissions headache, and a fresh round of arguments about whether the licence justifies itself.
entries.example: "The rollout promised a copilot for every employee. What most people got was a new button and one more thing to evaluate before sending an email."
askNext: Which Copilot are we discussing, in which product, with which permissions and data access?
What measurable work improved after adoption rather than merely feeling more modern?
Is this replacing friction, or adding a branded detour to familiar tasks?
entries.warningLabel: Umbrella product label. Always ask which Copilot and in which software surface.

translations.label: board meaning
translations.text: Proof that the office suite now has an AI strategy attached to it.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A branded assistant layer whose value depends heavily on product surface, permissions, and user patience.

entries.note: The important question is rarely whether Copilot exists. It is whether a specific Copilot meaningfully improves a specific workflow enough to survive the invoice.
entries.body: The branding suggests a tireless co-worker. The operating reality is closer to a software committee member.

entries.title: Midjourney
entries.devilDefinition: A picture factory for turning "I have no visual taste" into "I have a campaign."
entries.plainDefinition: Midjourney is an image-generation product known for stylised visual output, rapid iteration, and strong adoption among designers, marketers, and hobbyists.
entries.whyExists: Because visual ideation is expensive, taste is unevenly distributed, and many people would like concept art, campaign imagery, or mood boards before hiring anyone who can draw.
entries.misuse: By treating it as either the death of design or the same thing as design. It is neither. It is a very powerful image engine whose output still requires selection, direction, and legal and brand judgement.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means mood boards at industrial speed, more options than anyone needed, and a sudden premium on the human ability to reject what merely looks expensive.
entries.example: "Midjourney gave the team one hundred glorious directions, which unfortunately left them needing a designer more than they had an hour earlier."
askNext: Is the goal ideation, final artwork, or merely spectacle for a pitch deck?
Who is making the taste decisions after the model floods the room with options?
What rights, brand constraints, and production steps still sit outside the image generator?

translations.label: marketing meaning
translations.text: A way to make the brand look visionary before the brand has decided what it believes.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: An image tool that dramatically lowers the cost of visual exploration while raising the need for editorial restraint.

entries.body: The machine can flood the page with possibility. Someone still has to decide which possibility deserves civilisation.

entries.title: Mini Apps
entries.devilDefinition: A phrase that sounds self-explanatory until someone asks it to survive a budget meeting, a product review, or a mildly hostile question.
entries.plainDefinition: Mini Apps names the nearby AI idea that shows up when Telegram needs a more specific operational handle.
entries.whyExists: It exists because the main term alone is often too blunt. The surrounding conversation needs a way to name the neighbouring idea without pretending it is the same thing.
entries.misuse: It gets abused when people use the phrase as a mood, a shortcut, or a status signal instead of explaining the actual mechanism, incentive, or trade-off they mean.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means slowing the conversation down enough to ask whether the label points to a real system property, a commercial incentive, a user behaviour, or just a familiar rhetorical flourish.
entries.example: "Once mini apps entered the discussion, the team finally had a name for the thing everyone had been gesturing at with less precision."

entries.title: MiniMax
entries.devilDefinition: A model vendor whose product surface suggests the future will be multimodal, agentic, and administratively impossible to describe in one sentence.
entries.plainDefinition: MiniMax is an AI company and model platform offering text, speech, video, image, music, file, and agent-facing API capabilities, including official MCP server implementations for some modalities.
entries.whyExists: It exists because model platforms are becoming bundles of capabilities rather than single chat endpoints. Teams increasingly buy text, voice, media generation, tool calling, and integrations from the same vendor shelf.
entries.misuse: It gets reduced to another model name when the operational question is broader: which modality, which API, which latency, which rights, and which integration surface are actually being used?
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means a vendor option for multimodal generation, voice and media workflows, agent integrations, and OpenAI or Anthropic SDK-compatible access paths.
entries.example: "MiniMax entered the architecture review as a text model and left carrying voice, video, MCP, and three new procurement questions."
askNext: Which MiniMax capability is in scope: text, speech, video, image, music, or MCP integration?
What are the latency, licensing, data, and moderation constraints for that modality?
Does the unified vendor surface reduce complexity, or merely collect it in one account?

translations.label: platform meaning
translations.text: One vendor trying to cover more of the generative stack than plain chat.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A multimodal API supplier whose exact relevance depends on the workload, not the brand.

entries.body: The vendor map gets less neat every month. MiniMax is one of the reminders that "model provider" now often means "small department store".

entries.title: Mistral AI
entries.devilDefinition: The French company that managed to make "European frontier AI" sound like a commercial category rather than a wistful policy memo.
entries.plainDefinition: Mistral AI is a French AI company building models, products, and platform services, with a public identity built around efficient frontier systems, enterprise deployment, and a more open posture than many large closed-model competitors.
entries.whyExists: Because the market wanted proof that serious model-building could happen outside the dominant US labs, and because Mistral made strategic use of openness, efficiency, and European identity to become that proof.
entries.misuse: By describing Mistral AI as either purely open-source or purely a geopolitical symbol. It is a commercial model company making selective openness part of its strategy, not a charity for developer ideals.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means a vendor choice for teams that want competitive models, deployment flexibility, open-weight options, and some combination of cost discipline, control, and distance from the biggest US platforms.
entries.example: "They said they wanted Mistral for openness. What they really wanted was optionality, speed, and a way to avoid handing every strategic decision to the usual American suspects."
askNext: Do they want Mistral for model quality, deployment control, openness, or pricing?
Which product do they mean: API models, Le Chat, enterprise platform, or open-weight releases?
Is the attraction technical fit or strategic independence?
entries.warningLabel: Do not confuse a selectively open commercial strategy with pure open-source idealism.

translations.label: founder meaning
translations.text: A way to say we want frontier capability without sounding resigned to the usual stack.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A serious model vendor using efficiency, optionality, and selective openness as competitive weapons.

entries.note: Mistral's own language emphasises accessible frontier AI, and the Mistral 3 line makes the selective-open strategy more explicit: large frontier models, smaller deployment-friendly models, and enough openness to be strategically useful without becoming simple charity.
entries.body: It is one of the clearest examples of how "open" in AI rarely means simple purity. It usually means a strategy, a pricing posture, and a market wedge wearing noble clothes.

 In practice it usually means a vendor choice for teams that want competitive models, deployment flexibility, open-weight options, and some combination of cost discipline, control, and distance from the biggest US platforms.

 Do they want Mistral for model quality, deployment control, openness, or pricing? Which product do they mean: API models, Le Chat, enterprise platform, or open-weight releases? Is the attraction technical fit or strategic independence? Arthur Mensch Open Weights Open Source AI Frontier Model Mistral's own language emphasises accessible frontier AI, and the Mistral 3 line makes the selective-open strategy more explicit: large frontier models, smaller deployment-friendly models, and enough openness to be strategically useful without becoming simple charity.

entries.title: Mixture of Experts
entries.devilDefinition: An architecture for having enormous parameter counts without insisting every token use the whole cathedral.
entries.plainDefinition: A mixture-of-experts model is a neural architecture in which a routing mechanism activates only a subset of specialised sub-networks for each token or input, allowing higher total capacity without using all parameters on every pass.
entries.whyExists: Because model builders want more capacity and performance without paying the full compute cost of dense models at every step. Sparse activation offers a way to scale capability more economically, at least in theory.
entries.misuse: By treating "experts" as if the model contains little professors with stable domains of knowledge. It is an optimisation strategy, not a panel discussion. The routing can help a great deal, but it does not grant wisdom by committee.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means better parameter-efficiency on paper, more complex training and serving behaviour, routing overhead, and a fresh set of engineering questions about load balancing, latency spikes, and uneven quality.
entries.example: "The model was a mixture of experts, which sounded philosophical until the serving team had to explain the routing bottlenecks."
askNext: How many experts exist, and how many are active per token?
What did sparse routing improve relative to a dense baseline at the same cost?
Did the architecture move the bottleneck from compute to systems complexity?

translations.label: conference meaning
translations.text: A way to say the model is huge without admitting every request does not touch most of it.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Sparse activation traded some compute cost for substantially more systems complexity.

entries.body: The name flatters the machinery. What matters is not that there are experts, but whether the router sends work to the right places often enough to justify the complications.

entries.title: Model Collapse
entries.devilDefinition: The fear that if the machines keep learning from one another's homework, the class average will eventually become the curriculum.
entries.plainDefinition: Model collapse is the hypothesised degradation that can occur when models are trained repeatedly on synthetic or model-generated data without enough fresh, high-quality human data to preserve diversity and fidelity.
entries.whyExists: Because synthetic data is useful, cheap, and increasingly tempting at scale, but feedback loops can distort distributions, wash out edge cases, and quietly teach systems to imitate their own errors.
entries.misuse: By treating it as a guaranteed apocalypse or as proof that synthetic data is worthless. Synthetic data can be valuable. The concern is about unmanaged feedback loops, weak provenance, and replacing judgement with volume.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means teams need stronger data lineage, better curation, and honest evaluation before they let generated material dominate the training mix just because it is abundant and conveniently machine-readable.
entries.example: "The lab was not worried about synthetic data in principle. It was worried about model collapse from feeding the next system a buffet of the previous system's bad habits."
askNext: How much of the training mix is synthetic, and how is its quality measured?
What prevents generated noise from becoming self-confirming doctrine?
Are we preserving human-created edge cases or optimising them out of existence?

translations.label: internet meaning
translations.text: The machines are eating too much of their own content and may start to resemble it.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Poorly managed recursive data pipelines can flatten quality faster than the dashboards admit.

entries.body: The phrase has become a general anxiety container, which is understandable. Still, the useful question is not whether collapse is coming in the abstract, but whether your data pipeline has the manners to notice contamination before it institutionalises it.

entries.title: Model drift
entries.devilDefinition: The moment a system that used to behave one way quietly acquires new manners, new vices, or a fresh interpretation of your instructions, and no dashboard feels personally responsible.
entries.plainDefinition: Model drift is the change in a model system's behaviour over time, whether because the underlying model changed, surrounding prompts and tools changed, traffic changed, or the environment stopped being the one the original evaluation had in mind.
entries.whyExists: AI systems are not static artefacts. Providers update models, teams revise prompts, tools change their schemas, and user behaviour wanders off. The term exists because quality can move even when nobody thinks they touched the main lever.
entries.misuse: It gets used as a polite synonym for "something got worse". Sometimes the issue is genuine behaviour drift. Sometimes it is bad retrieval, a broken tool, a prompt edit, or a team discovering that last month's benchmark did not survive contact with this month's traffic.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means running comparisons over time, holding out stable evaluation sets, watching sensitive slices rather than averages, and keeping enough version history to identify whether the model changed or the system around it did.
entries.example: "The team called it model drift because that sounded dignified. The incident report later revealed a prompt change, a tool timeout, and optimism."
askNext: Which part of the system changed: the base model, the prompt stack, the tools, the routing, or the traffic?
What stable evaluation set shows the behaviour actually moved rather than merely encountering a harder week?
Can we roll back or replay the previous configuration to isolate the change?

translations.label: operator meaning
translations.text: The system changed somewhere, and now you must prove where before someone blames the wrong component.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A behaviour change whose cause may live in the model, the scaffolding, or the surrounding workflow.

entries.body: Model drift deserves a score of five because teams confuse the label with the diagnosis. The phrase is useful only if it narrows the search rather than sanctifying the shrug.

entries.title: Model Garden
entries.devilDefinition: A cloud menu where every model is arranged like a tasteful orchard and billed like enterprise software.
entries.plainDefinition: Model garden is a vendor term for a managed catalogue of models that customers can browse, provision, fine-tune, and call through a cloud platform.
entries.whyExists: Because buyers want more than one model, clouds want to be the place where that choice gets made, and nobody enjoys integrating a fresh API every time the leaderboard twitches.
entries.misuse: By making model access sound like curation solved the hard part. A catalogue is useful. It does not answer which model is trustworthy, economical, regionally available, or appropriate for a real workload.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means a procurement-and-deployment layer with billing, quotas, IAM, regions, logging, and just enough abstraction to turn model shopping into infrastructure policy.
entries.example: "The model garden looked abundant until the architecture review reduced it to the three options legal, security, and latency could tolerate."
askNext: Which models are actually production-ready for our constraints?
What vendor lock-in sits behind the pleasant catalogue language?
Are we choosing a model, or quietly choosing an entire cloud relationship?

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A cloud-controlled shelf of models that turns experimentation into something procurement can invoice.

entries.body: The garden metaphor is doing heavy work here. It suggests abundance, order, and gentle stewardship. The operational reality is still quotas, contracts, and somebody arguing about egress.

entries.title: Model Router
entries.devilDefinition: The switchboard that promises the right model for every job and occasionally adds a thrilling new place for policy mistakes to hide.
entries.plainDefinition: A model router is a software layer that selects among multiple models or providers for a given request based on rules or signals such as task type, latency, cost, availability, policy, or fallback behaviour.
entries.whyExists: Because no single model is best at everything, outages happen, prices move, and product teams would prefer not to hard-code every bet into the application layer.
entries.misuse: By marketing routing itself as intelligence, or by implying that a multi-model stack is automatically well evaluated. A router can express good operational judgement. It can also just spread uncertainty across more vendors.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means heuristics, policy rules, retries, provider abstractions, observability, and a permanent negotiation between quality, speed, and the monthly bill.
entries.example: "The model router kept the app online during the outage, then introduced a fresh round of debugging because two providers interpreted the same tool schema differently."
askNext: What signals drive routing decisions, and were they benchmarked on real workloads?
What is the fallback policy when the preferred model fails or drifts?
Does the router reduce operational risk, or merely distribute it?

translations.label: platform meaning
translations.text: One control plane to decide whose model invoice gets the request.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: An operational policy engine standing between product promises and vendor behaviour.

entries.note: Vendors often sell model routers as a neutral convenience layer. In practice the interesting part is what routing policy the customer can inspect, override, and trust.
entries.body: The appeal is obvious. So is the trap. Once routing becomes strategic, the application has acquired a small foreign policy department.

entries.title: Model spec
entries.devilDefinition: A public document describing how the model ought to behave, which is at least more honest than pretending the behaviour emerged from pure mathematics and good breeding.
entries.plainDefinition: A model spec is a provider-authored behavioural specification that describes the intended priorities, refusal patterns, boundaries, and instruction-handling rules for a model or model family.
entries.whyExists: Modern models are expected to be useful, safe, steerable, and publicly defensible at once. A specification exists so providers can state what behaviour they are trying to produce and give developers a reference for the rules sitting behind the pleasant interface.
entries.misuse: It gets treated as a guarantee, a safety proof, or a substitute for evaluation. A spec can clarify intent. It cannot ensure that every model version, product surface, or edge case will obey the text as faithfully as legal and marketing might prefer.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means reading the provider's stated priorities, understanding how instruction hierarchy and refusals are supposed to work, and designing prompts, product expectations, and escalation paths around the fact that the model has house rules you did not personally write.
entries.example: "The product team called it a bug. The model spec called it a refusal. The user called it a waste of time."
askNext: Which behaviours in this product come from our prompt stack, and which come from the provider's published rules?
How closely does the live model match the current spec in the cases we actually care about?
What happens operationally when the provider updates the spec before we update our expectations?

translations.label: developer meaning
translations.text: The provider's declared behavioural contract, minus the comforting fantasy that declarations enforce themselves.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A document you should read before accusing the model of breaking rules that were never yours to set.

entries.body: Model specs matter because the era of purely generic models is over. The model arrives with prior instructions, prior politics, and prior notions of what counts as responsible behaviour.

entries.title: Multimodal
entries.devilDefinition: Able to juggle text, images, audio, or video, which sounds far more coherent than the reality of several data types sharing one product team.
entries.plainDefinition: Multimodal refers to models or systems that can process, generate, or jointly reason over more than one data modality, such as text, image, audio, or video.
entries.whyExists: Human tasks rarely arrive as clean text alone. Multimodal systems exist because many useful applications involve documents, screenshots, spoken language, visual context, or combinations of these.
entries.misuse: It gets waved around as a prestige adjective even when a product merely accepts an image upload and produces a caption. True multimodal capability is not just accepting multiple inputs. It is being able to use them meaningfully for the task at hand.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means a model or stack that can ingest several types of data, map them into compatible internal representations, and produce outputs that reflect those combined signals. The hard part is often not modality support itself but maintaining reliability across them.
entries.example: "The feature was multimodal in the useful sense: it could read the chart, listen to the spoken explanation, and answer questions about the mismatch."
askNext: Which modalities can the system actually handle in production?
Does the model merely accept multiple formats, or does it use them jointly to improve the outcome?
How has performance been evaluated across the different input types?
entries.note: Multimodal can describe both input and output capabilities. A system that reads images and writes text is multimodal, even if it does not generate images in return.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: The system works across more than one type of data, with all the messy evaluation problems that implies.

entries.body: The adjective matters less than the task. The real question is whether the extra modality materially improves the outcome or simply decorates the feature list.

entries.title: Mustafa Suleyman
entries.devilDefinition: The fluent translator of machine intelligence into product language for people who would rather adopt a companion than study a model card.
entries.plainDefinition: Mustafa Suleyman is the CEO of Microsoft AI and a prominent founder-operator associated with productising advanced AI into mass-market interfaces, assistants, and companion-style experiences.
entries.whyExists: Because AI is not only a research contest. It is also a packaging contest, an interface contest, and a trust contest. Suleyman's name often marks the point where the conversation shifts from raw capability to how intelligence is turned into habit.
entries.misuse: By treating "companion" language as either obviously profound or obviously absurd. It is better understood as a serious product thesis about persistence, personalisation, convenience, and software that wants to sit between the user and everything else.
entries.practicalMeaning: When people invoke Mustafa Suleyman, they usually mean AI as a persistent product layer: more assistant, less query box; more relationship, less transaction.
entries.example: "The roadmap had gone full Mustafa: less tool, more companion, with enough memory and polish to make dependence sound like convenience."
askNext: Is this about interface design, product strategy, or model capability?
Does the vision depend on trust, habit, or lock-in?
Are we building a workflow tool, a companion, or a polite way to blur the line?

translations.label: product meaning
translations.text: A sign that the discussion is moving from model performance to user relationship design.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A shorthand for the thesis that AI should feel less like software you visit and more like software that stays with you.

entries.body: It is easier to mock the companion idea than to notice how many major products are inching toward it anyway.

entries.title: Nemotron 3
entries.devilDefinition: NVIDIA's model family for anyone who suspected the GPU company might also enjoy owning more of what runs on the GPUs.
entries.plainDefinition: Nemotron 3 is NVIDIA's open model family with Nano, Super, and Ultra variants positioned for agentic, reasoning, coding, and conversational workloads.
entries.whyExists: It exists because model vendors increasingly want families that span different cost and performance points, especially for agent workloads where serving efficiency and deployment control matter as much as leaderboard theatre.
entries.misuse: It gets flattened into "NVIDIA has a model" as though all variants, licences, serving choices, and agent benchmarks collapse into one tidy procurement noun.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means evaluating an NVIDIA-backed open model family for agentic workloads, often alongside questions about hardware fit, NIM deployment, open-weight hosting, and price-performance.
entries.example: "Nemotron 3 entered the shortlist because the team wanted an open model that made sense on the hardware strategy already approved by finance."
askNext: Which Nemotron 3 variant are we testing: Nano, Super, or Ultra?
Are we choosing it for agent capability, serving economics, openness, or NVIDIA stack alignment?
How does it perform on our own workflows rather than the family brochure?

translations.label: infrastructure meaning
translations.text: A model family designed to make NVIDIA's agent story less dependent on other people's models.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A family of model-size and capability trade-offs wrapped in the NVIDIA deployment universe.

entries.body: The more agent workloads become real workloads, the more model families start sounding like infrastructure portfolios.

entries.title: Neocloud
entries.devilDefinition: A cloud provider whose most disruptive feature is often discovering that scarce Nvidia hardware can be resold with excellent posture.
entries.plainDefinition: "Neocloud" is an informal label for newer cloud providers focused on AI infrastructure, especially GPU capacity for training and inference, rather than broad general-purpose enterprise cloud offerings.
entries.whyExists: The term exists because demand for accelerators outran easy supply, while many teams wanted alternatives to the hyperscalers on price, access, support, geography, or simple willingness to answer the phone.
entries.misuse: It is misused whenever specialised capacity is treated as a permanent moat. Owning or brokering GPUs can be valuable. It does not automatically solve networking, reliability, data governance, procurement, or the small inconvenience of debt.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means a provider that can get you machines faster, sometimes cheaper, sometimes with better human support, in exchange for narrower platform breadth and a closer relationship with the physics of hardware finance.
entries.example: "The neocloud pitch was compelling right up to the moment finance noticed the business model involved both GPUs and optimism."
askNext: Are we choosing this provider for price, access, support, location, or all four?
What happens to reliability, networking, and compliance once we leave the big clouds?
Is this a real platform advantage or an opportunistic position in a supply shortage?

translations.label: operator meaning
translations.text: A cloud that may treat AI workloads as its first-class citizen instead of an unusually demanding tenant.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A specialist compute provider offering real advantages in some cases and a very concentrated exposure to hardware economics in others.

entries.note: The term describes a market position more than a technical architecture. Some neoclouds are serious platforms. Some are very earnest forwarding businesses with GPUs.
entries.body: The honest version of the pitch is simple: the big clouds were busy, so a new class of landlord arrived.
