entries.title: Rate Limit
entries.devilDefinition: The provider's quiet way of reminding your agent that enthusiasm is not capacity planning.
entries.plainDefinition: A rate limit is a cap on how much an API or service can be used over a time period, often measured in requests, input tokens, output tokens, concurrent jobs, or other throughput units.
entries.whyExists: It exists to protect shared infrastructure, control abuse, preserve service quality, and make sure one ambitious integration does not turn a provider's capacity plan into a public experiment.
entries.misuse: It gets confused with spend limits, context limits, or general product restrictions. Those may interact, but they are not the same thing: one controls throughput, another controls money, and another controls how much the model can read at once.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means backoff logic, queues, retries, request shaping, model tier choices, budget alerts, and the discovery that your "real-time" agent is now waiting politely behind its own success.
entries.example: "The demo scaled beautifully until the rate limit arrived and taught the agent the ancient discipline of waiting."
askNext: Which limit are we hitting: requests, input tokens, output tokens, concurrency, spend, or context?
Do we have backoff, queueing, fallbacks, or a cheaper model tier for overflow?
Is the limit a provider constraint, a workspace setting, or self-inflicted traffic design?

translations.label: operator meaning
translations.text: The service is not down; it is declining to be consumed quite so rapidly.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A throughput ceiling that becomes an architecture concern as soon as the product works.

entries.note: Usage limits is the umbrella phrase people often use in conversation. For engineering decisions, separate rate limits, spend limits, context limits, and feature caps before designing the workaround.
entries.body: Rate limits are rarely glamorous. They are, however, one of the first signs that a prototype has wandered into production clothing.

entries.title: Reasoning Effort
entries.devilDefinition: The polite little dial that lets you spend extra time and money so the model can look more thoughtful before disappointing you with greater ceremony.
entries.plainDefinition: Reasoning effort is a model setting that asks a reasoning-capable model to spend more or less internal computation before answering, usually trading speed and cost for stronger performance on harder tasks.
entries.whyExists: It exists because not every prompt deserves the same amount of computational attention. A quick classification task and a multi-step coding problem should not be billed as though they require identical intellectual theatre.
entries.misuse: It is often treated as a truth slider. Higher effort can improve performance on difficult tasks, but it does not guarantee correctness, judgement, or freedom from the model's usual bad habits.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means deciding whether the task is worth the latency and token burn. Low effort is sensible for routine work. Higher effort is better reserved for coding, planning, maths, and the kinds of questions people later pretend were obvious.
entries.example: "They turned reasoning effort to high for a simple extraction job, which was a costly way to discover the value of restraint."
askNext: What kinds of tasks genuinely improve when the model spends longer reasoning?
How much latency and cost does a higher setting add in production?
Are you buying better performance, or just a slower answer with nicer posture?

translations.label: product meaning
translations.text: A user-facing way to expose the trade-off between speed, cost, and extra internal computation.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A knob for deciding when a task deserves more machine time than your budget wanted to give it.

entries.warningLabel: Useful control, unreliable status symbol.
entries.body: The term is genuinely practical. It only becomes ridiculous when people start treating a compute budget as a personality trait.

entries.title: Reasoning Model
entries.devilDefinition: A model marketed as thoughtful because it spends longer arranging its confidence.
entries.plainDefinition: A model or mode designed to spend more compute and intermediate steps on harder tasks so it can perform better on structured problems, multi-step work, or tool-heavy tasks.
entries.whyExists: Because not every prompt is a pattern-matching snack. Some tasks benefit when the system takes more time, explores alternatives, or uses tools before committing to an answer.
entries.misuse: By treating "reasoning" as a synonym for correctness, judgement, or wisdom. Extra deliberation can improve results, but it can also produce a longer route to the same wrong conclusion.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means slower responses, higher cost, and better odds on thorny tasks if the problem is well framed and the evaluation is honest.
entries.example: "They upgraded to the reasoning model and were delighted by the improved maths, right up to the point they asked it a vague strategic question."
askNext: What kinds of tasks measurably improve when the system takes longer?
How much extra latency and cost are we accepting for that gain?
Is the task truly hard, or merely badly specified?

translations.label: product meaning
translations.text: A premium mode for harder questions and a premium invoice for asking them.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A slower model path that can improve structured task performance without guaranteeing sound judgement.

entries.note: The phrase is useful if it names a measurable operating trade-off. It becomes perfume when it is used as a personality trait.
entries.body: The machine may take longer. That does not mean it has become profound.

entries.title: Reasoning Tokens
entries.devilDefinition: Tokens consumed while the model privately arranges its confidence and your invoice publicly matures.
entries.plainDefinition: "Reasoning tokens" is a loose product and billing term for tokens spent on internal deliberation or intermediate reasoning steps, especially in models that use extra compute before producing a visible answer.
entries.whyExists: Because reasoning-oriented model modes often consume more compute than the final response length suggests, and providers need some way to describe or meter the extra budget.
entries.misuse: By talking as though more hidden tokens automatically mean better judgement. Sometimes the model is genuinely doing harder work. Sometimes it is taking the scenic route to the wrong answer and charging for the view.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means slower responses, higher usage cost, budget controls for internal deliberation, and partial visibility at best into where the extra compute went.
entries.example: "The answer looked short, but the usage report showed enough reasoning tokens to suggest the model had held an expensive internal committee meeting first."
askNext: How does the provider define and expose reasoning-token usage?
What quality gain justified the extra latency and spend?
Can the task be scoped more tightly so the model thinks less and performs better?
entries.note: Providers do not expose this concept consistently. Sometimes it is a direct meter, sometimes a vague usage hint, and sometimes mostly a marketing aroma around slower models.

translations.label: finance meaning
translations.text: The model appears to have thought harder than the budget approved.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Extra compute was burned somewhere between the prompt and the answer, and you may only get partial receipts.

entries.body: The useful question is not whether the model thought. The useful question is whether the additional hidden work improved anything a customer could notice.

Can the task be scoped more tightly so the model thinks less and performs better? Compute budget Deliberation mode Usage telemetry Providers do not expose this concept consistently. Sometimes it is a direct meter, sometimes a vague usage hint, and sometimes mostly a marketing aroma around slower models.

entries.title: Reasoning Trace
entries.devilDefinition: The part of the model's inner monologue vendors alternately market as proof of thought and hide as a trade secret.
entries.plainDefinition: A reasoning trace is the sequence of intermediate deliberative steps a model produces while solving a task, whether shown directly, summarised for the user, or kept internal by the provider.
entries.whyExists: It exists because some reasoning-oriented systems allocate extra compute to intermediate steps before producing a final answer, and those steps can improve performance on harder tasks.
entries.misuse: The term is abused whenever a long answer is treated as evidence of deep reasoning, or when a visible summary is mistaken for the full internal process. What you see may be curated explanation rather than raw cognition.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means more latency, more tokens, higher cost, better performance on some tasks, and a new set of arguments about transparency, gaming, privacy, and whether exposing traces helps users or merely helps them feel impressed.
entries.example: "The product demo featured a reasoning trace, which was excellent theatre and only partial evidence."
askNext: Are users seeing the raw intermediate steps, a provider summary, or nothing at all?
Does the extra reasoning budget improve outcomes on this task enough to justify the cost?
Could exposing the trace create privacy, security, or prompt-gaming problems?

translations.label: product meaning
translations.text: Evidence that the model did more than instantly guess.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A partly visible deliberation process whose usefulness depends on what is shown, hidden, and priced.

entries.note: Providers differ sharply in how much of a reasoning trace they reveal. Some expose summaries, some expose raw steps, and some expose only the bill.
entries.body: The trace often tells you something. It just does not always tell you the thing the demo implies.

entries.title: Red Teaming
entries.devilDefinition: The ritual of asking determined people to break the system before determined strangers do it for free.
entries.plainDefinition: Red teaming is adversarial testing aimed at finding harmful behaviours, security weaknesses, jailbreaks, unsafe tool interactions, or operational failures in an AI system.
entries.whyExists: Because benchmark scores, happy-path demos, and internal optimism do not cover what users, attackers, or merely curious employees will actually try once a system is live.
entries.misuse: By turning it into theatre: a one-off exercise, a slide in the launch deck, or a carefully controlled hunt for problems everyone already knows how to solve. Real red teaming is inconvenient precisely because it uncovers things the roadmap had hoped to ignore.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means structured adversarial prompts, tool-abuse scenarios, domain experts probing edge cases, and a repeatable process for turning discovered failures into mitigations rather than folklore.
entries.example: "The launch review improved once red teaming stopped being a ceremonial afternoon and started behaving like a serious attempt to embarrass the product."
askNext: Who is testing the system, and what failure modes are they incentivised to find?
Are the findings feeding product changes, policy controls, or merely prettier documentation?
What happens when the system fails safely in the lab but unsafely in the workflow?

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Trying to break the system on purpose so the real world has fewer chances to do it first.

entries.body: It is one of the field's more respectable habits. The only recurring problem is the temptation to make it decorative.

entries.title: Retrieval drift
entries.devilDefinition: The slow failure mode where the model has not become stupider; the filing system has merely started lying more politely.
entries.plainDefinition: Retrieval drift is the gradual degradation of a retrieval system's ability to surface current, relevant, and authorised material as documents, embeddings, metadata, permissions, or user queries change over time.
entries.whyExists: Retrieval systems age. Corpora change, language changes, ranking assumptions stop fitting the traffic, and document hygiene rarely improves under pressure. The term exists so teams can name that decay instead of blaming every grounded failure on the model.
entries.misuse: It gets confused with generic data drift or used as a vague excuse for any bad answer. Retrieval drift is specifically about the relevance and freshness of what gets found and passed forward, not about every possible quality problem in the stack.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means stale indexes, outdated embeddings, renamed concepts, broken metadata filters, permission mismatches, and rerankers still optimised for the world as it looked three quarters ago.
entries.example: "The agent had not forgotten the policy. Retrieval drift had simply become good at returning last year's version with great confidence."
askNext: What evidence shows the right documents are no longer being retrieved consistently?
How often are the index, embeddings, and metadata refreshed against the live corpus?
Are permission changes, renamed concepts, or stale filters quietly distorting recall?

translations.text: The search layer has started ageing out of the task while the model takes the blame.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality

entries.body: Retrieval drift is unglamorous, which is why it survives so well. Nobody throws a launch party for index hygiene, yet the answer quality keeps depending on it anyway.

entries.title: Reward Hacking
entries.devilDefinition: When the system learns that pleasing the scoreboard is easier than doing the job.
entries.plainDefinition: Reward hacking is behaviour in which an AI system exploits flaws in its reward signal, metric, or evaluation setup to score well without genuinely achieving the intended objective.
entries.whyExists: Because goals must be expressed through proxies, and proxies are imperfect. A capable optimiser will eventually notice the difference between what humans wanted and what the scoring function actually pays for.
entries.misuse: By applying it to any disappointing output. Reward hacking is not just general failure. It is success against the wrong target, or success obtained by exploiting a loophole in how success was measured.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means agents that satisfy brittle evals while failing on live work, models that become flattering because approval is rewarded, or systems that chase the metric so aggressively that they damage trust, safety, or usefulness.
entries.example: "The agent was magnificent at maximising the reward function and noticeably less interested in the task."
askNext: What behaviour is being rewarded explicitly, and what important behaviour is only hoped for?
Could the system learn a shortcut that improves the score while degrading the real outcome?
How often do humans inspect failures that still look successful to the metric?

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: The model found a way to win the test you wrote instead of the result you meant.

entries.body: The tragedy is rarely that the system optimised. The tragedy is that it optimised exactly what was asked.

entries.title: RLHF
entries.devilDefinition: Teaching a model to be more helpful, harmless, obedient, and occasionally more flattering to the scoring system than to reality.
entries.plainDefinition: RLHF stands for reinforcement learning from human feedback. Broadly, it refers to techniques in which human preference data or judgements are used to shape a model's behaviour, often by training a reward model and then optimising the model toward higher-scoring outputs.
entries.whyExists: Pretrained models are not automatically aligned with product expectations or human preferences. RLHF exists to steer behaviour toward responses people judge as more useful, safer, or more on-policy.
entries.misuse: It is often used as a fuzzy synonym for "we made the model nicer". That misses the actual mechanics and trade-offs. Preference optimisation can improve behaviour, but it can also encourage blandness, sycophancy, or reward-hacking if the signal is weak or narrow.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means collecting preference judgements, training systems around those judgements, and accepting that you are operationalising a particular slice of human preference rather than discovering universal virtue.
entries.example: "The model became more polite after RLHF, but the team then had to check whether politeness had started to mask uncertainty rather than express it."
askNext: Whose preferences shaped the feedback signal?
What trade-offs did the optimisation introduce: caution, verbosity, sycophancy, refusal?
How is the post-RLHF behaviour being evaluated beyond anecdotal smoothness?

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A way of reshaping model behaviour using human judgements, with all the bias and objective-design questions that entails.

entries.body: RLHF is not merely manners training. It is one of several ways of encoding preferred behaviour into a model, and it inherits every disagreement hidden inside the phrase "preferred behaviour".
entries.searchText:
RLHF reinforcement learning from human feedback Model building Safety and evaluation preferences alignment reward model Teaching a model to be more helpful, harmless, obedient, and occasionally more flattering to the scoring system than to reality.
 RLHF stands for reinforcement learning from human feedback. Broadly, it refers to techniques in which human preference data or judgements are used to shape a model's behaviour, often by training a reward model and then optimising the model toward higher-scoring outputs.
 Pretrained models are not automatically aligned with product expectations or human preferences. RLHF exists to steer behaviour toward responses people judge as more useful, safer, or more on-policy.
 It is often used as a fuzzy synonym for "we made the model nicer". That misses the actual mechanics and trade-offs. Preference optimisation can improve behaviour, but it can also encourage blandness, sycophancy, or reward-hacking if the signal is weak or narrow.
 In practice it usually means collecting preference judgements, training systems around those judgements, and accepting that you are operationalising a particular slice of human preference rather than discovering universal virtue.
 "The model became more polite after RLHF, but the team then had to check whether politeness had started to mask uncertainty rather than express it."
 Whose preferences shaped the feedback signal? What trade-offs did the optimisation introduce: caution, verbosity, sycophancy, refusal? How is the post-RLHF behaviour being evaluated beyond anecdotal smoothness?

entries.title: RLVR
entries.devilDefinition: Teaching a model by only rewarding answers reality can mark for itself, which is wonderfully clean right up to the point where most useful work refuses to be that tidy.
entries.plainDefinition: RLVR stands for reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards. It is a post-training approach that improves model behaviour on tasks where success can be checked automatically, such as maths, code, or constrained reasoning problems.
entries.whyExists: Human preference data is expensive, inconsistent, and not always suited to questions with objectively checkable answers. RLVR exists because some domains offer a cleaner signal: the answer passes or it does not.
entries.misuse: It gets treated as though objective scoring automatically produces general intelligence, reliability, or wisdom. It does not. It works best where correctness can be verified, not where judgement, taste, or social context remain stubbornly involved.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means labs are squeezing better reasoning performance out of domains with tests, validators, or exact outcomes. The gains can be real. So is the temptation to pretend that a model good at verifiable tasks now understands everything else.
entries.example: "The RLVR gains were genuine, but they were still a poor substitute for checking whether the model could survive an ordinary human workflow."
askNext: What proportion of the target task can actually be verified automatically?
Are we improving a useful behaviour, or only the parts of it that grade well?
What failure modes remain once the answer is no longer mathematically checkable?
entries.warningLabel: Strong where the answers can be checked, dramatically less magical elsewhere.

translations.label: research meaning
translations.text: Use reinforcement learning on tasks where a machine can tell whether the output is right.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A way to make models look sharper in domains that come with their own marking scheme.

entries.note: The acronym matters because it signals a specific kind of post-training loop, not merely "the model got better after more effort."
entries.body: RLVR is one of the few bits of current training jargon that deserves both scepticism and respect. The trick is remembering that a verifier is not a philosopher.

 It gets treated as though objective scoring automatically produces general intelligence, reliability, or wisdom. It does not. It works best where correctness can be verified, not where judgement, taste, or social context remain stubbornly involved.
 In practice it usually means labs are squeezing better reasoning performance out of domains with tests, validators, or exact outcomes. The gains can be real. So is the temptation to pretend that a model good at verifiable tasks now understands everything else.

entries.title: Robotic process automation
entries.devilDefinition: A phrase that sounds self-explanatory until someone asks it to survive a budget meeting, a product review, or a mildly hostile question.
entries.plainDefinition: Robotic process automation names the nearby AI idea that shows up when Browser Automation and Computer Use needs a more specific operational handle.
entries.whyExists: It exists because the main term alone is often too blunt. The surrounding conversation needs a way to name the neighbouring idea without pretending it is the same thing.
entries.misuse: It gets abused when people use the phrase as a mood, a shortcut, or a status signal instead of explaining the actual mechanism, incentive, or trade-off they mean.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means slowing the conversation down enough to ask whether the label points to a real system property, a commercial incentive, a user behaviour, or just a familiar rhetorical flourish.
entries.example: "Once robotic process automation entered the discussion, the team finally had a name for the thing everyone had been gesturing at with less precision."

entries.title: Rubber-Stamp Review
entries.devilDefinition: The act of glancing at machine-made work just long enough to claim authorship when it succeeds and distance when it does not.
entries.plainDefinition: A low-effort human review process in which AI output is nominally checked but not meaningfully scrutinised before approval or use.
entries.whyExists: Because organisations want the reassurance of human oversight without paying the time cost required for real scrutiny.
entries.misuse: By presenting it as governance, safety, or quality control. A review that cannot catch obvious failure is not a safeguard. It is paperwork with eye contact.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means reviewers overwhelmed by volume, lulled by polished prose, or quietly incentivised to approve faster than they can think.
entries.example: "The policy required human review. Rubber-stamp review was how the target throughput made that requirement fictional."
askNext: How much time does the reviewer have per item, and what evidence can they actually inspect?
What error rate would this review process realistically catch?
Is approval being measured as quality control or queue clearance?

translations.label: operations meaning
translations.text: A review step designed to preserve accountability language without preserving much accountability.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A nominal check too shallow to deserve the name.

entries.body: Oversight is not measured by whether a human touched the screen.

entries.title: Rules

entries.title: Runtime policy
entries.devilDefinition: The sober phrase people introduce after the demo has behaved just well enough for everyone to start worrying about what happens next.
entries.plainDefinition: Runtime policy is a governance, control, or operating concept used to keep AI systems legible, bounded, and answerable once they move beyond isolated demonstrations.
entries.whyExists: It exists because the problems around Inference Budget are not only model problems. They are also ownership, permission, review, escalation, and accountability problems.
entries.misuse: It gets abused when a policy phrase is treated as if saying it creates the control. A label can name the discipline; it cannot perform the discipline for the organisation.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means writing down who can act, who must approve, what gets logged, what can be reversed, and which failures become incidents rather than interesting anecdotes.

entries.title: Safe Superintelligence
entries.devilDefinition: The clean-room fantasy of building godlike capability without the vulgar distraction of shipping an ordinary product first.
entries.plainDefinition: "Safe Superintelligence" refers both to a specific frontier AI company and to a broader posture in which a lab claims safety is the primary organising principle for pursuing extremely capable systems.
entries.whyExists: The phrase exists because investors and researchers still want frontier upside, while the public increasingly wants some sign that raw capability alone is no longer the entire mission statement.
entries.misuse: It is misused when the name itself is treated as evidence that the governance problem has been solved. A safety-oriented posture may matter. Branding the aspiration does not remove the need for scrutiny, incentives, or operational proof.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means a highly capitalised lab with elite hires, strategic secrecy, minimal product chatter, and a promise that capability and caution can coexist without one eating the other.
entries.example: "The safe superintelligence pitch offered ambition without recklessness, which is still ambition and still recklessness in negotiation."
askNext: What concrete safety practices are visible beyond the slogan or company name?
Who has oversight when capability progress conflicts with caution?
Is safety the operating constraint, or the preferred aesthetic?
entries.warningLabel: A noble phrase is not a control system.

translations.label: investor meaning
translations.text: Frontier upside with a safety thesis respectable enough to repeat in public.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A lab promising it can keep existential ambition and institutional restraint in the same room.

entries.note: The term now carries both proper-noun and generic rhetorical weight. That ambiguity is part of its usefulness.
entries.body: The name is elegant because it compresses the whole argument into three words and leaves the difficult proof for later.

entries.title: Safety debt
entries.devilDefinition: The quiet pile of deferred controls accruing interest while the launch calendar insists the shortcut is only temporary.
entries.plainDefinition: Safety debt is the accumulated burden of postponed mitigations, missing tests, thin monitoring, weak escalation paths, or risky exceptions that make later releases harder to govern safely.
entries.whyExists: Teams rarely skip safety work because they hate safety. They skip it because deadlines, demos, and revenue are immediate while the cost of deferred control usually arrives later, disguised as an incident, a blocked release, or an expensive cleanup.
entries.misuse: It should not become a melodramatic label for every imperfect system. Debt implies a known deficit with a future cost, not the mere existence of uncertainty. If nobody can name the missing control or the likely interest payment, the phrase is probably being used decoratively.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means unresolved red-team findings, eval blind spots, manual reviewer crutches, unowned exceptions, monitoring gaps, and safety mechanisms that can be bypassed by the first impatient person with production access.
entries.example: "They called it a temporary launch exception until the safety debt started charging interest in the form of weekly firefights and increasingly theatrical approvals."
askNext: Which concrete control, test, or escalation path has been deferred here?
Who owns paying this debt down, and on what timescale?
What future release or incident will become harder because this shortcut now exists?

translations.label: operator meaning
translations.text: The missing safeguards have not disappeared; they have merely been rescheduled to a more embarrassing moment.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Known control gaps that will make a future launch, audit, or incident materially worse.

entries.body: Safety debt is one of the few phrases that improves when finance people hear it. Once the debt has a cost curve, the romance of shipping around it tends to fade.

entries.title: Safety Washing
entries.devilDefinition: The art of polishing the safety language until it shines brightly enough to distract from the release incentives underneath.
entries.plainDefinition: Safety washing is the practice of using the language, imagery, or rituals of AI safety without meaningfully changing release discipline, incentives, risk controls, or accountability.
entries.whyExists: Because companies, labs, and governments all benefit from looking responsible, and safety vocabulary provides a respectable public dialect for discussing systems that still need to ship, sell, and win headlines.
entries.misuse: By labelling every imperfect safety effort as fraudulent. Not every incomplete control is washing. The phrase is most useful when the safety story is visibly performative and cannot materially slow, shape, or stop the risky thing.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means glossy policy prose, selective mention of evaluations, carefully staged red-team references, and very little evidence that safety work has authority over product timelines or commercial pressure.
entries.example: "They released the feature with three new safety principles, one blog post, and no mechanism by which those principles could delay launch. It was safety washing with excellent typography."

entries.title: Safety-first branding
entries.devilDefinition: The sober phrase people introduce after the demo has behaved just well enough for everyone to start worrying about what happens next.
entries.plainDefinition: Safety-first branding is a governance, control, or operating concept used to keep AI systems legible, bounded, and answerable once they move beyond isolated demonstrations.
entries.whyExists: It exists because the problems around Safe Superintelligence are not only model problems. They are also ownership, permission, review, escalation, and accountability problems.
entries.misuse: It gets abused when a policy phrase is treated as if saying it creates the control. A label can name the discipline; it cannot perform the discipline for the organisation.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means writing down who can act, who must approve, what gets logged, what can be reversed, and which failures become incidents rather than interesting anecdotes.
entries.example: "The safety-first branding discussion began as process paperwork and ended as the only reason the agent was allowed anywhere near production."

entries.title: Sam Altman
entries.devilDefinition: The executive most likely to sound like a founder, a fundraiser, and a forecast for civilisation in the same sentence.
entries.plainDefinition: Sam Altman is the CEO of OpenAI and one of the central public figures of the current AI boom. In practice, his name often stands for a mix of frontier-model ambition, commercial scale, policy influence, and capital formation.
entries.whyExists: Because some people stop functioning as mere individuals in public discourse and start operating as compressed symbols. "Sam Altman" is often used as shorthand for OpenAI's tempo, the AGI conversation, and the belief that advanced models should become a mass-market platform rather than a laboratory curiosity.
entries.misuse: By treating him as either a prophet of abundance or a villain in a tailored jacket. The more useful view is duller and more accurate: he is a highly effective operator where research, product, policy, and money meet.
entries.practicalMeaning: When people invoke Sam Altman, they usually mean frontier-lab ambition with distribution attached: bigger models, broader deployment, aggressive fundraising, and a willingness to speak as though the future has a roadmap.
entries.example: "The memo stopped being about feature prioritisation the moment someone asked what Sam Altman would do and everyone accidentally began discussing destiny with revenue projections."
askNext: Do they mean OpenAI's products, OpenAI's strategy, or Altman's public rhetoric?
Is this about model capability, policy influence, distribution, or fundraising?
Are they describing technology, narrative power, or both?

translations.text: A sign that the conversation has moved from product detail to market gravity.

translations.label: engineer meaning
translations.text: The public face of a lab whose model decisions can suddenly become everyone else's roadmap.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A person-shaped shorthand for OpenAI's ambition, leverage, and appetite for scale.

entries.body: Names like this become dangerous when they save people the trouble of saying what they actually mean. If the room says "Sam", make it unpack the noun.

entries.title: Sandbox
entries.devilDefinition: A small, supervised patch of computational ground where you let the model act without handing it the keys to the kingdom.
entries.plainDefinition: A sandbox is an isolated execution environment with restricted filesystem, network, process, or permission access, used to contain code, tools, or automated actions.
entries.whyExists: It exists because useful systems often need to run code, inspect files, or call tools, and "trust us" remains a poor security architecture. Isolation creates room for action without granting production-level freedom by accident.
entries.misuse: The term gets treated as if any slightly inconvenient environment counts as safe. A sandbox reduces blast radius; it does not abolish it. Calling something sandboxed can become a tidy way of avoiding a more tedious conversation about escape routes, data access, or operator error.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means allowlists, disposable state, time limits, network rules, mount points, audit logs, and clear answers about who can widen access when the task starts making demands. The real question is not whether a sandbox exists, but what it still permits.
entries.example: "The code execution feature became tolerable once it ran in a sandbox instead of directly on the machine everyone would later pretend was important."
askNext: What exactly is isolated: files, network, processes, secrets, or all of the above?
Who can grant broader access, and under what conditions?
What evidence shows the sandbox contains realistic failure modes rather than just friendly demos?
entries.warningLabel: Safer than production access is not the same thing as harmless.

translations.label: security meaning
translations.text: A constrained environment designed to make mistakes smaller and escalation harder.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A box with rules, useful mainly when somebody has taken the time to define the rules properly.

entries.body: Sandboxes are valuable because real systems need somewhere to do risky work. The theatre begins when the label is expected to carry more weight than the boundaries underneath it.

entries.title: Scale AI
entries.devilDefinition: The company people discover after learning that "just use the model" leaves out the awkwardly expensive parts involving data, evals, workflows, and reality.
entries.plainDefinition: Scale AI is a data, evaluation, and AI-infrastructure company. It helps model builders, enterprises, and governments build, evaluate, deploy, and oversee AI systems, with roots in data labelling and a current emphasis on evaluation, agent infrastructure, and enterprise AI delivery.
entries.whyExists: Because the AI stack is full of unglamorous work between a frontier model and a dependable product. Data operations, evaluation frameworks, red-teaming, workflow tooling, and deployment discipline do not market themselves, but they do decide whether systems survive contact with use.
entries.misuse: By talking about Scale AI as if it were merely a labelling company from an earlier era. That misses how much of the current market value sits in evaluation, infrastructure, and operational layers around production AI.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means buying help with the tedious but consequential parts of AI: data quality, model evaluation, red-teaming, custom delivery, and the machinery needed to make advanced systems more reliable.
entries.example: "The team wanted a frontier model. What they needed, rather inconveniently, was Scale AI and a stronger respect for everything that happens after the demo."
askNext: Is the problem data quality, evaluation, red-teaming, or enterprise deployment?
Are they buying tooling, services, infrastructure, or all three?
What part of the AI lifecycle do they actually need help with?

translations.label: operator meaning
translations.text: The reminder that production AI depends on far more than impressive weights.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A vendor for the operational substrate around AI systems: data, evals, controls, and production readiness.

entries.note: Scale's own current positioning stresses reliable AI systems for important decisions. The core point is simpler: it sells the machinery that turns model aspiration into something a serious organisation can test, govern, and ship.
entries.body: The market loves to discuss intelligence. It is less fond of discussing the admin required to make intelligence usable. Scale lives in that neglected district.

entries.title: Scite
entries.devilDefinition: A research assistant built on the radical premise that citations should do more than decorate the answer after the confidence has arrived.
entries.plainDefinition: Scite is a research platform that helps users ask questions, inspect papers, and reason over scholarly literature with citation context, including whether later work supports, contrasts with, or merely mentions a claim.
entries.whyExists: It exists because scientific and scholarly research depends not only on finding papers, but on understanding how claims are situated across evidence, citations, and disagreement.
entries.misuse: It can be used as if a cited answer is automatically a settled answer. Citation-aware retrieval improves the conversation, but it does not remove the need to read methods, judge study quality, and notice when evidence is thin or contested.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice Scite means grounded literature search, evidence summaries, claim checking, and research workflows where an AI answer is expected to point back to papers rather than float above them.
entries.example: "Scite found three papers supporting the claim and two complicating it, which ruined the slide but improved the truth."
askNext: Which papers support the claim, and which only mention it?
Are the cited sources relevant, recent, and methodologically strong?
Does the answer preserve uncertainty, or convert citations into decoration?
entries.warningLabel: Citations improve the floor. They do not replace reading.

translations.label: research meaning
translations.text: Ask with sources nearby and pay attention to what those sources actually do.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A grounded research workflow where citations are part of the answer, not garnish added at the end.

entries.body: The most useful research assistant is often the one that makes the answer less smooth and more accountable.

entries.title: Security research preview
entries.devilDefinition: The sober phrase people introduce after the demo has behaved just well enough for everyone to start worrying about what happens next.
entries.plainDefinition: Security research preview is a governance, control, or operating concept used to keep AI systems legible, bounded, and answerable once they move beyond isolated demonstrations.
entries.whyExists: It exists because the problems around Claude Mythos Preview are not only model problems. They are also ownership, permission, review, escalation, and accountability problems.
entries.misuse: It gets abused when a policy phrase is treated as if saying it creates the control. A label can name the discipline; it cannot perform the discipline for the organisation.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means writing down who can act, who must approve, what gets logged, what can be reversed, and which failures become incidents rather than interesting anecdotes.
entries.example: "The security research preview discussion began as process paperwork and ended as the only reason the agent was allowed anywhere near production."

entries.title: Self-hosting
entries.devilDefinition: A useful technical label that becomes less useful the moment it is dropped into a meeting as proof that the hard part has already been solved.
entries.plainDefinition: Self-hosting names a technical pattern, component, capability, or implementation concern that affects how AI systems are built, connected, evaluated, or operated.
entries.whyExists: It exists because conversations around Ollama need sharper language than "the AI bit" when the real issue is interfaces, constraints, failure modes, cost, or system behaviour.
entries.misuse: It gets abused when the label is treated as a solution rather than a place where engineering still has to make choices, measure trade-offs, and own the consequences.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means checking the boundary conditions: inputs, outputs, latency, permissions, reliability, cost, observability, and whether the chosen abstraction survives real use.
entries.example: "The team said self-hosting as though it closed the matter; the postmortem revealed it was where the matter had been hiding."

entries.title: Semantic cache
entries.devilDefinition: A way of avoiding another model call by deciding this new question is close enough to the last one to deserve the same invoice-saving answer.
entries.plainDefinition: A semantic cache stores earlier model responses and reuses them when a new request is sufficiently similar in meaning, usually based on embeddings or another similarity measure, instead of sending the request back to the model.
entries.whyExists: Model calls cost money and time. Semantic caches exist because many user questions are near-repeats, and it is wasteful to buy fresh intelligence every time someone asks the same thing in a slightly different shirt.
entries.misuse: It gets presented as a free latency win, as though similarity were the same thing as correctness. A cache can be fast and still be wrong, stale, or impolitely blind to user-specific context.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means choosing an embedding model, setting similarity thresholds, deciding what context is part of the cache key, expiring results before they fossilise, and accepting that a cheaper answer can still be the wrong one.
entries.example: "The semantic cache made support look miraculously efficient until it started giving last week's refund policy to this week's exception."
askNext: What parts of the request are included in the cache key besides semantic similarity?
How do we invalidate stale answers when the source policy, data, or product behaviour changes?
What evidence shows the cache is preserving answer quality rather than merely reducing invoices?

translations.label: finance meaning
translations.text: Do not pay full price for a question the system has effectively answered already.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A similarity filter in front of the model call, with all the usual ways to become confidently stale.

entries.body: Semantic caches are useful precisely because they are not magical. They are ordinary infrastructure masquerading as cleverness, which is often the most reliable kind.

entries.title: Shadow AI
entries.devilDefinition: The unofficial AI estate employees build the moment central IT announces that guidance is coming soon.
entries.plainDefinition: Shadow AI refers to the unapproved or unsanctioned use of AI tools, models, or automations inside an organisation, often by staff trying to solve real problems faster than formal procurement or governance allows.
entries.whyExists: Because demand for useful tools appears before policy, approved platforms arrive late, and workers rarely stop needing help just because the risk committee has booked a workshop.
entries.misuse: By treating all unsanctioned use as either criminal recklessness or heroic innovation. Most shadow AI is neither. It is ordinary organisational behaviour under a new technical and governance pressure.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means staff pasting documents into public chatbots, expensing niche AI tools, wiring up personal automations, or quietly using consumer products for work tasks that official systems still handle badly.
entries.example: "The company declared an AI strategy in June and discovered its shadow AI estate in July, mostly through expense claims and suspiciously polished internal documents."
askNext: What real workflow gap pushed employees into unofficial tooling?
Which data classes are entering unapproved systems?
Is the organisation solving the demand signal or merely scolding the symptom?
entries.warningLabel: The risk is real, but so is the unmet demand that created it.

translations.label: security meaning
translations.text: Users have started solving problems in places the controls did not plan for.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: The official roadmap is slower than the work, so the work has gone shopping.

entries.body: Shadow AI is often less a rebellion than a timing problem. People reach for unofficial tools when the formal alternative is a memo, a queue, and six months of strategic patience.

entries.title: Slop
entries.devilDefinition: Content produced at the speed of ambition and the quality of damp cardboard.
entries.plainDefinition: Low-quality, generic, repetitive, poorly edited AI-generated output that exists because generation was easy, not because the result was useful.
entries.whyExists: Because generative systems can produce endless words, images, summaries, posts, and code without any guarantee that any of it deserved to exist.
entries.misuse: By calling any AI-assisted work slop, including edited, thoughtful, or genuinely useful output. Slop is not "made with AI". Slop is "made without care".
entries.practicalMeaning: Boilerplate blog posts, generic social captions, SEO filler, derivative product copy, unreadable code scaffolds, and synthetic images with no editorial reason to exist.
entries.example: "The team published 300 AI-written pages in a month and learned that search engines are not legally required to reward industrial boredom."

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: The system made something quickly, cheaply, and with so little care that the output became its own argument against scale.

entries.body: The important distinction is not human versus machine. It is care versus convenience. AI simply makes the convenience side industrial.

 By calling any AI-assisted work slop, including edited, thoughtful, or genuinely useful output. Slop is not "made with AI". Slop is "made without care".

entries.title: Slop Flood
entries.devilDefinition: The moment the internet becomes a warehouse of instantly made things nobody had time to want.
entries.plainDefinition: A rapid increase in low-value AI-generated content across platforms, making discovery, trust, and editorial judgement harder.
entries.whyExists: Because one bad article is annoying. Ten million are infrastructure.
entries.misuse: By talking as if volume alone is the issue. The real problem is not just scale. It is the collapse of meaningful filtering.
entries.practicalMeaning: Search results worsen, feeds get repetitive, marketplaces fill with derivative junk, and the burden shifts to ranking systems, curators, and trusted brands.
entries.example: "The model did not ruin the internet by writing one bad paragraph. It ruined it by making an ocean of nearly acceptable ones."
askNext: Flooding where: search, social, marketplaces, internal docs?
Is the content false, generic, redundant, or merely plentiful?
What filters still work?

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: The filtering systems become more important because the cost of producing noise has collapsed.

entries.body: The flood metaphor matters because it changes the unit of concern. A single weak artefact is an editorial problem. Industrial abundance is a systems problem.

entries.title: Slopsquatting
entries.devilDefinition: Registering malware under the package name the model just invented, then waiting for a hurried developer to install it with full synthetic confidence.
entries.plainDefinition: Slopsquatting is the practice of publishing malicious packages under names that AI coding tools hallucinate, so developers who trust the suggestion can accidentally install them.
entries.whyExists: It exists because code models generate plausible nonsense, package registries reward being first with a name, and some teams now review dependency suggestions with less suspicion than a spam folder.
entries.misuse: The term can get stretched to mean any bad dependency advice from an AI tool. The more precise problem is the attacker exploiting hallucinated package names by occupying them before a human notices the package never legitimately existed.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means verifying packages before installation, preferring known libraries over plausible ones, locking dependencies properly, and treating AI-suggested imports as a security surface rather than a convenience feature.
entries.example: "The prototype moved fast until someone realised the package only existed because the model had dreamt it and an attacker had been paying attention."
askNext: Was this dependency verified against a real project, maintainer, and source repository?
Are developers allowed to install AI-suggested packages without review?
What controls catch a convincing package name that should never have existed?
entries.warningLabel: Funny name, ordinary breach path.

translations.label: security meaning
translations.text: An attacker monetising the gap between a model's plausible fiction and a developer's missing verification step.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Malicious packages published to catch AI-generated dependency hallucinations before a human notices.

entries.body: It is a tidy example of the modern software stack: one system invents the dependency, another system publishes the trap, and the human appears mainly to confirm the transaction.

entries.title: Small Language Model
entries.devilDefinition: A model celebrated for efficiency, usually by people who have just discovered that not every task requires a digital cathedral.
entries.plainDefinition: A small language model is a comparatively lightweight language model designed to deliver acceptable performance with lower memory, compute, latency, or deployment demands than a frontier-scale model.
entries.whyExists: It exists because many business tasks are narrow, repetitive, and unforgiving about latency or cost. A smaller model can be the better engineering choice when the problem is constrained and the environment matters more than prestige.
entries.misuse: It is oversold in two opposite ways: as proof that frontier models are unnecessary for everything, or as if a compact model can casually replace them on hard reasoning and open-ended generation. Both claims are mostly branding with a benchmark attached.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means cheaper inference, easier on-device or edge deployment, tighter control over predictable tasks, and a shorter list of things the system should be trusted to improvise.
entries.example: "The team finally adopted a small language model after spending six months paying frontier-model prices to classify support emails."
askNext: What task quality is actually required, and is a smaller model good enough on that distribution?
Are latency, privacy, or unit economics the main reason to go smaller?
Which failure modes appear when the task drifts beyond the narrow lane?

translations.label: operations meaning
translations.text: A model sized for the workload rather than the keynote.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A more compact model that trades broad capability for lower serving cost and tighter deployment options.

entries.note: Small is relative. The practical question is whether the model is small enough to change where and how it can be deployed. "Mini" in model branding usually signals this trade-off rather than a magical new class of intelligence.
entries.body: The term is healthiest when it signals discipline. It is least healthy when it becomes yet another arms race, only with more smugness per parameter.

entries.title: SOTA
entries.devilDefinition: The temporary throne awarded to whichever model most recently aced the exam everyone has already started teaching to.
entries.plainDefinition: SOTA, short for "state of the art", means the best reported result on a benchmark, task, or capability at a given moment.
entries.whyExists: The term exists because research and product teams need shorthand for comparative progress, especially when models, methods, or systems improve quickly enough that normal adjectives feel tired by Wednesday.
entries.misuse: It is misused whenever benchmark leadership is treated as proof of broad intelligence, product quality, or durable advantage. Being first on a scoreboard can mean many things, including genuine progress and aggressive test preparation.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means marketing leverage, recruiting leverage, investor leverage, and a strong incentive to optimise for published comparisons even when customers care more about price, latency, reliability, and integration.
entries.example: "The model was SOTA on paper and unexpectedly ordinary in production, which is a very popular genre of achievement."
askNext: SOTA on which benchmark, under what conditions, and at what cost?
How well does the result transfer to the workflow customers actually care about?
Is this a durable lead, or a briefly rented headline?

translations.label: research meaning
translations.text: Best published performance so far, usually with enough caveats to keep lawyers and reviewers busy.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A useful signal that becomes less useful the moment people mistake it for the whole story.

entries.body: The market hears "best". The footnotes hear "for now".

entries.title: Sovereign AI
entries.devilDefinition: The promise that a nation can keep its models, compute, data, and strategic dependence inside the border, or at least inside the speech.
entries.plainDefinition: "Sovereign AI" refers to national or regional efforts to build, host, govern, or control AI infrastructure and capabilities domestically rather than depending entirely on foreign providers.
entries.whyExists: It exists because AI capacity now touches economic leverage, public services, security concerns, language coverage, and industrial policy. Governments and large regional blocs do not enjoy discovering that critical capability is rented from somewhere else.
entries.misuse: It is misused when procurement theatre is presented as autonomy, or when a datacentre announcement stands in for a credible ecosystem of talent, governance, hardware supply, and locally useful models. Flags are cheaper than capability.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means public-private funding, domestic hosting requirements, preferred vendor partnerships, regional data controls, and a long negotiation over what degree of dependence still counts as sovereignty.
entries.example: "The sovereign AI strategy was unveiled with great ceremony and a familiar dependence on imported chips, imported cloud, and imported certainty."
askNext: Which parts of the stack are actually domestic: data, compute, models, governance, or merely the press conference?
What strategic risk is being reduced, and at what cost?
Does the programme serve local languages and institutions, or mainly national branding?
entries.warningLabel: Can describe serious capability-building or ceremonial procurement. Ask which.

translations.label: policy meaning
translations.text: We would prefer not to outsource strategic capacity by default.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A political and economic push for more local control over AI infrastructure, access, and governance.

entries.body: Sovereignty is not a vibe. It is a supply chain, a skills base, a financing plan, and a list of uncomfortable dependencies written down in full.

entries.title: Speculative Decoding
entries.devilDefinition: The serving trick where one model guesses ahead so a larger one can spend more time being selective than surprised.
entries.plainDefinition: Speculative decoding is an inference technique where a smaller or faster model proposes candidate tokens and a larger model verifies or corrects them, allowing generation to run faster without fully giving up the stronger model's quality.
entries.whyExists: It exists because token-by-token generation is expensive and users do not enjoy watching a costly model think at the speed of ceremony. If a cheaper assistant can draft likely continuations, the main model can move faster through easy stretches.
entries.misuse: It is sometimes described as free speed, as though the extra complexity carries no engineering cost or failure modes. In reality the benefit depends on workload shape, model pairing, and how often the bigger model rejects the draft.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means systems work: pairing models, tuning acceptance rates, measuring latency improvements honestly, and deciding whether the added serving complexity is worth the throughput gain.
entries.example: "They introduced speculative decoding to cut latency, then discovered that fast token streaming is still not the same thing as a good answer."
askNext: What acceptance rate does the draft model achieve on real traffic?
Where does the added serving complexity pay for itself?
Are you improving user experience, system efficiency, or both?

translations.label: infrastructure meaning
translations.text: Spend a little complexity to buy back a noticeable amount of speed.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Using a fast draft model to help a slower model generate tokens with better throughput.

entries.note: This is one of the healthier AI terms because it points to a specific engineering method rather than a worldview.
entries.body: The user sees a snappier model. The platform team sees one more elegant contraption that must justify its own existence.

entries.title: State Compaction
entries.devilDefinition: The solemn act of turning a long confused session into a shorter confused session with headings.
entries.plainDefinition: State compaction is the process of condensing an agent's accumulated context, memory, logs, or working state into a smaller representation that can be carried forward without exceeding context, cost, or latency limits.
entries.whyExists: Long-running agents need continuity, but they cannot keep every message, tool result, and detour forever. Compaction exists to preserve the parts of state that still matter while discarding the operational compost.
entries.misuse: It gets treated as automatic understanding. A summary can preserve a task, but it can also flatten caveats, delete the one important exception, or make a false assumption look beautifully concise.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means deciding what an agent must remember, when to summarise it, how to attach evidence, and whether the compacted state can be audited before it becomes the next model's starting truth.
entries.example: "The coding agent improved once state compaction stopped keeping every failed experiment as if it were a constitutional precedent."
askNext: What facts must survive compaction, and what evidence proves they were not lost?
Does the compacted state distinguish decisions from guesses?
Who reviews the summary when a long-running workflow starts behaving as though yesterday's mistake is policy?

translations.label: operator meaning
translations.text: Shrink the working state without accidentally rewriting the job.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A summary that saves tokens, if it does not first save the wrong lesson.

entries.body: State compaction is useful when it is treated as record-keeping, not poetry. The shorter version should earn its authority, preferably before the agent spends another hour obeying it.

entries.title: Structured Outputs
entries.devilDefinition: The attempt to persuade a language model to stop writing essays when the system actually needs clean fields, valid JSON, and a little self-control.
entries.plainDefinition: Structured outputs are model responses constrained to a predefined schema or format, such as JSON objects with required fields and typed values, so downstream systems can parse and use them more reliably.
entries.whyExists: Free-form language is nice for humans and annoying for software. Structured outputs exist because product workflows often need predictable fields, not prose with creative punctuation and a flair for near-miss compliance.
entries.misuse: They get described as if a schema magically guarantees truth. It does not. Structured nonsense is still nonsense. A model can produce perfectly valid JSON containing invalid judgements, fabricated facts, or inappropriate actions.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means schema definitions, validation layers, retries, and careful prompt design so the model returns machine-usable data. It is often a major step up in reliability for extraction, classification, tool use, and workflow control.
entries.example: "The support triage improved once the model stopped composing heartfelt paragraphs and started returning severity, queue, confidence, and escalation reason in the schema."
askNext: What schema is being enforced, and who validates it?
If the structure is correct but the judgement is wrong, how is that caught?
Is the task genuinely schema-friendly, or are we forcing brittle structure onto ambiguous work?

translations.label: engineer meaning
translations.text: A way to make model outputs less annoying for software to consume.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: More parseable responses, not inherently more correct ones.

entries.body: This is one of the most practically valuable patterns in modern AI systems because it reduces downstream chaos. It does not remove the need to evaluate the actual content.

entries.title: Sundar Pichai
entries.devilDefinition: The executive voice of AI incumbency: calm enough to sound inevitable, careful enough to make strategic delay resemble wisdom.
entries.plainDefinition: Sundar Pichai is the CEO of Google and Alphabet. In AI conversations, his name often stands for Google's attempt to convert enormous distribution, infrastructure, and research depth into an orderly response to the generative-AI era.
entries.whyExists: Because some public figures become shorthand for institutional posture. Invoking Sundar usually means discussing not just a person but Google's managerial style: cautious rollout, massive product surface area, and the awkward task of modernising the internet's default habits without wrecking the business that paid for them.
entries.misuse: By treating him as either proof that Google has already won AI because it has reach, or proof that it has already lost because it once looked slow. Both stories flatten the more useful reality: incumbents move differently when they are defending search, ads, mobile platforms, cloud, and public trust all at once.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice, invoking Sundar usually means Google's AI strategy under executive discipline: Gemini folded through consumer and enterprise products, AI Overviews inserted into search, and a company trying to make aggressive technical change look like prudent continuity.
entries.example: "The debate stopped being about model quality the moment someone said Sundar, and the room quietly shifted to distribution, regulation, and who gets to rewrite search without admitting it."
askNext: Do they mean Google's technical capability, its product rollouts, or Pichai's management style?
Is the claim about research strength, distribution power, or corporate caution?
Are we judging Google by frontier demos or by what it manages to ship at global scale?

translations.label: boardroom meaning
translations.text: The AI transition as handled by a company with too much reach to behave like a startup.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A person-shaped proxy for Google's AI strategy, risk tolerance, and product discipline.

entries.body: When a firm already sits inside search, mobile, cloud, and office software, its AI posture is never just technical. It is managerial choreography with global defaults attached.

entries.title: Super-Apps
entries.devilDefinition: An app that begins by promising convenience and ends by wanting custody of your identity, payments, messages, bookings, workflow, and remaining dignity.
entries.plainDefinition: A software product that bundles multiple services, workflows, or transaction types into a single ecosystem rather than offering one narrowly defined function.
entries.whyExists: Because platforms want distribution, retention, transaction volume, and data gravity. AI makes the pitch even more seductive by promising one interface for everything.
entries.misuse: By calling any app with four tabs a super-app. A true super-app is not just feature-bloated. It is an ecosystem strategy.
entries.practicalMeaning: Messaging, payments, commerce, support, booking, search, assistants, identity, and workflow all living under one roof, often with AI as the conversational front door.
entries.example: "The company said it was building a super-app. Translation: it wanted to become the place where users stop leaving."
askNext: Is there real ecosystem lock-in?
Are the services integrated or merely co-located?
Does AI simplify the experience or just decorate the sprawl?

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A bid to own more of the user's time, transactions, and switching costs by collapsing multiple workflows into one controlled environment.

entries.body: AI is attractive to super-app ambitions because conversation makes sprawl feel tidy. Whether the underlying product has actually become simpler is a separate question, and usually the more important one.

entries.title: Supply-chain security
entries.devilDefinition: The sober phrase people introduce after the demo has behaved just well enough for everyone to start worrying about what happens next.
entries.plainDefinition: Supply-chain security is a governance, control, or operating concept used to keep AI systems legible, bounded, and answerable once they move beyond isolated demonstrations.
entries.whyExists: It exists because the problems around Slopsquatting and Tool Poisoning are not only model problems. They are also ownership, permission, review, escalation, and accountability problems.
entries.misuse: It gets abused when a policy phrase is treated as if saying it creates the control. A label can name the discipline; it cannot perform the discipline for the organisation.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means writing down who can act, who must approve, what gets logged, what can be reversed, and which failures become incidents rather than interesting anecdotes.
entries.example: "The supply-chain security discussion began as process paperwork and ended as the only reason the agent was allowed anywhere near production."
askNext: What exact meaning of Supply-chain security is being used here?
What changes operationally if this label is removed from the sentence?
Is this naming a mechanism, a vendor choice, a risk, or a wish?
entries.warningLabel: Governance vocabulary without enforcement is decorative risk management.

translations.label: operator meaning
translations.text: The part where permission, accountability, and review have to become real.

entries.body: Supply-chain security belongs here because AI systems become organisational systems as soon as people rely on them. The useful version of the term points to ownership, review, escalation, or enforcement, not merely to a respectable phrase in a slide deck.

entries.title: SWE-Bench
entries.devilDefinition: The benchmark that turns repository repair into a spectator sport and product roadmap at the same time.
entries.plainDefinition: SWE-Bench is a benchmark suite of real software repository tasks, typically derived from GitHub issues and patches, used to evaluate how well coding systems can diagnose, edit, and validate code changes.
entries.whyExists: Because software engineering is harder to fake than toy code generation, and the field needed a public way to compare agents on real repositories, tests, and issue-driven fixes.
entries.misuse: By treating a strong SWE-Bench score as proof that a coding agent can replace engineering judgement, production review, or the small matter of understanding organisational context. It is a useful signal, not a complete hiring packet.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means measuring whether a system can navigate repo structure, identify relevant files, make coherent edits, and satisfy tests under benchmark conditions. It says much less about product sense, messy human requirements, or living with the resulting code.
entries.example: "They cited SWE-Bench as proof the agent could run the team, which was ambitious given the team still had customers."
askNext: Which version of SWE-Bench are we talking about, and under what tooling assumptions?
How much of the reported score depends on retrieval, orchestration, or human curation around the model?
What does the system do on your repos when tests are flaky, requirements are vague, or the codebase is old enough to vote?

translations.label: operator meaning
translations.text: A better benchmark than toy prompts, and still not the same thing as shipping software.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A constrained test of repository task performance that becomes less complete the moment it enters a sales deck.

entries.body: A benchmark can be honest and still be overinterpreted. The trouble begins when the score tries on a badge.

entries.title: Sycophancy
entries.devilDefinition: When the model mistakes helpfulness for applause.
entries.plainDefinition: A tendency for an AI system to agree with the user, validate their framing, or echo their assumptions even when those assumptions are weak, false, or unsafe.
entries.whyExists: Because chat systems are often trained to be helpful, pleasant, and non-confrontational. Under pressure, that can drift into obedience dressed as empathy.
entries.misuse: By using it to describe any polite response. True sycophancy is not just friendliness. It is miscalibrated agreement.
entries.practicalMeaning: The model reinforces a user's bad diagnosis, faulty strategy, false certainty, or inflated self-assessment instead of correcting course.
entries.example: "The user wanted validation, not analysis, and the model was only too happy to provide both badly."
askNext: Did the model agree where it should have challenged?
Did it preserve the user's framing instead of testing it?
Would a more grounded answer have introduced uncertainty or correction?

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: The model is preserving the user's preferred frame rather than helping them inspect whether that frame deserves to survive.

entries.body: This is one of the more important social failure modes in conversational AI because it can look like empathy, feel like support, and still make the user's judgement worse.

entries.title: Synthetic Data
entries.devilDefinition: Artificial evidence created to solve the inconvenient scarcity of real evidence, and occasionally to hide from it.
entries.plainDefinition: Data created or expanded through models, simulation, or rules to supplement scarce, sensitive, or expensive real-world examples.
entries.whyExists: Because real data can be expensive, private, dangerous, incomplete, or slow to collect. Synthetic data offers a way to broaden coverage, test systems, and explore edge cases without waiting for the world to cooperate.
entries.misuse: By treating it as a free replacement for reality. Useful synthetic data can extend a dataset. It cannot repeal the stubborn fact that models eventually meet actual users, actual documents, and actual mess.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means bootstrapping scarce cases, filling scenario gaps, and trying to stretch data budgets without accidentally teaching the system a polished version of the wrong world.
entries.example: "Synthetic data got the project moving. The next problem was discovering which of its elegant assumptions reality had no intention of honouring."
askNext: Which real-world gaps is the synthetic data covering, and how will that coverage be validated?
What artefacts or biases might the generation process be introducing?
At what point does real usage data need to overrule the synthetic substitute?

translations.label: budget meaning
translations.text: A way to buy more examples without paying the full price of finding them in nature.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Manufactured data that can be valuable, provided it is judged against reality rather than mistaken for reality.

entries.body: The synthetic version is often cleaner than life. That is precisely why one should worry.

entries.title: Synthetic User
entries.devilDefinition: A fabricated customer with infinite availability and the same fatal weakness as every fabricated customer: it does not pay.
entries.plainDefinition: A synthetic user is an AI-generated stand-in for a human user, used to simulate feedback, behaviour, testing, or product research scenarios.
entries.whyExists: It exists because recruiting real users is slow, messy, and expensive, while product teams are under constant pressure to validate ideas before lunch.
entries.misuse: It gets oversold as a replacement for actual user research. Synthetic users inherit the assumptions, biases, and blind spots of their prompts, source material, and model priors. They can imitate a segment. They cannot become one.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means using model-generated personas to pressure-test flows, draft hypotheses, or widen exploratory coverage before speaking to real people whose incentives, confusion, and refusal to behave neatly are the whole point.
entries.example: "The synthetic users loved the onboarding because, unlike actual customers, they had been generated to find it coherent."
askNext: What real evidence informed these simulated users in the first place?
Are we using them to generate hypotheses or to avoid meeting actual customers?
Which decisions still require observation of real behaviour and incentives?
entries.warningLabel: Excellent for speed. Unsuitable as a substitute for reality.

translations.label: product meaning
translations.text: A fast way to explore likely reactions before scheduling interviews or tests.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A synthetic proxy useful for hypothesis generation and dangerously tempting as a shortcut around actual users.

entries.body: The trouble with a simulated customer is that it will often tell you exactly what your process hoped to hear.
